label: Analyze
text: These examples show the different functions of the Analyze CHOP.

label: Analyze
text: This Analyze CHOP becomes -1 when there are no peaks in the input number.

label: Analyze
text: These two examples get the RMS (Root Mean Squared) values of both the incoming Noise CHOP, and even more useful, for the incoming audio file. RMS is a way of measuring "power" of a signal, and is similar to amplitude, but favors the peaks.The viewer of audiofilein1 is fixed at a range of -1 to 1 because of Viewer Active -> rclick -> Vertical Range set to -1 to +1.

label: Analyze
text: The top case triggers off based on time thresholds, but assumes there are no jittery values in the signal.The next two examples will analyze, in this case a series of pulses to detect when the input signal is on (value 1) for a continuous .6 seconds.The second is do-it-yourself where you can nervously tap the button and nothing happens in trigger2. But if you hold, it triggers.It is useful for filtering out jittery inputs from inexact devices like Kinect or Leap. Analyze looks for the minimum value over .6 seconds, and if all samples over the last .6 seconds are above zero, the Trigger CHOP is triggered. The Trigger CHOP assures the output signal stays on for at least a definitive 2 seconds, and when Analyze goes to 0, the output signal stays on for an additional 1.5 seconds and then drops to 0 during 1 more second. Asjust all these parameters to taste.You can unbypass the Filter CHOP to be more lenient: the input signal can drop to 0 for up to .2 seconds.You can bypass the Logic CHOP to work with more raw analog data.Note the Filter CHOP is not Time Sliced - it's filtering the entire .6 seconds each frame it cooks.

label: Analyze
text: This keeps the XY range between 0 and 1, whether you are moving the input slightly or in a wide range.The Analyze CHOPs get the maximum and minimum values for X and Y over the past 10 seconds (Trail CHOP is set to 10 sec).Then the math_rangeX and math_rangeY CHOPs re-range the max/min so the values go from 0 to 1.If you want to fix the max/min range, just lock the Trail CHOPs or the Analyze CHOPs right after you move the inputs around to the XY full range that you want to operate in.If you want it adaptive over the last 20 seconds, unlock the Trail CHOPs and then unbypass the Lag CHOPs. It gives it an 10 extra seconds to blend to new max/min values. See their Clamp Slope values.The math_*Bump* CHOPs make sure that when the inputs go silent, the output is .5.props to elburz for original concept.Trail CHOPs have fixed range of -1 to +1 via the graph Viewer Active rclick menu.

The first vector represents the direction to the right (over x), the second upwards (over y).In the top example the up vector is rotated 45 degrees over X.The vectors dont have to be normalized.

label: Audio Device Out
text: Making audio not pop when you adjust volume is a necessity. If you just attach a slider to a Math CHOP (via export, expression, or as an input to the Math CHOP), will make it pop as the audio samples jump from say .7 to .4 in one step.To demonstrate popping, bypass timeslice1 and play with adjustSlider. Then un-bypass timeslice1 and ply with adjustSlider.This method interpolates the volume control of the audio rate (735 samples per frame (T 60 Hz)), meaning that at the start of a frame it is v0 and at the end of aframe, 735 samples later it is v1. WHen thisn is multiplied against the audio wave, then the audio wave rises or falls cloothly over  the course of 1/60 seconds.Timeslice is set to interploate the  slider linearly.Audio Device Out is set to NOT cook every frame so when you are not looking at this demo, it's not outputting audio.

label: Audio Filter
text: The second input on the Audio Filter CHOP is used to drive some parameters at higher frequency than an exported CHOP can on the parameter, or a CHOP expression. These are limited by the frame rate of the timeline, default 60. If you want to modulate the cutoff frequency at say 1000 cycles per second, you need to feed the Audio Filter CHOP's second input a channel that's called cutofffrequency, at a high sample rate. In the case of the LFO CHOP, it is set to 8000 samples per second, and as you can see from the graph, there are about 120 samples per frame.This distorts the audio a lot, pleasing at some combinations of the 2 sliders and the Filter Cutoff parameter of teh Audio Filter CHOP.Then send audioosci_sine to the filter via null1.Try it wuth LFO set to a Square Wave.If you are up for it, try the white noise in the Audio Oscillator CHOP.

label: Audio Oscillator
text: Try the various waveforms (Type) and various Base Frequencies. (First un-bypass the Audio Device Out CHOP.)Try -2, -1, 0, 1, 2 in the Constant CHOP. Each step is an octave relative to the Base Frequency.Then try 0, 1/12, 2/12 etc in the Constant CHOP - each step of 1/12 is a "semitone" higher than its predecessor (one note up on a conventional keyboard). 12/12 = 1 is an octave, helping to de-mystify the 12-note scale.7/12 is 7 semitones, called a "fifth", and is almost 50% higher in frequency than the Base Frequency, giving it a pleasant relation to the base frequency, and pleasant to the ear.Connecting lag1 to the oscillator first input gives a 2-octave glissando.

label: Audio Render
text: You feed an audio channel to Audio Render, it is associated with the Source object's position (the throbbing sphere), and it will generate the number of channels that is implicitly requested... binaual and stereo are two channels, for example.

label: Audio Render
text: The Audio Render CHOP uses Simulation mode, which unlike Simple Positional allows for multiple sources and scene meshes (POPs/SOPs with multi-polygons).Enable audiodevout1 and select your desired device to hear the rendered audio.On the Audio Render CHOP, the SceneMesh has a transmission of 0 (ie. 0%), allowing for no transmission of sound through the wall.The Geometry component Wall contains a single Rectangle POP that forms a wall and is tied into the simulation on the Meshes page block 0.The Geometry component Source1 is positioned on a circular path that can be controlled using the SOUND SOURCE slider.  It is tied into the simulation as a Source on the Audio Render page block 0.As it passes beyond the wall, you can notice the effect the wall has on the sound when placed in between the listener and source. You can also play around with the absorption, scattering, and transmission values (on the Meshes page) to see how that effects the sound.Boundary surrounds the entire scene and helps demonstrate how reflections behave within the simulation. It is tied into the simulation on the Meshes page block 1.Baking is enabled for optimization purposes. Baking significantly lowers computation time for reflections and therefore will provide significantly lower cook times. Reflection Bake Mode is set to Auto, which means whenever there is a change in the simulation that require a re-bake, it will perform that. If playing around with a lot of parameters, it may be preferable to set to Off, or Manual mode, then bake as desired.To see the position of the baked probes in the camera, enable the display flag of datto1.

label: Audio Spectrum

label: Audio Spectrum
text: audiospect_raw in this example has a linear x-scale representing Hz. Its "Fraquency <> Logarithmic Scaling" parameter is set to 0 (Frequency).  In this mode, each sample represents some # of Hz (depending on the audio sample rate and the timeline frames per second). So sample 5000 is  5000 Hz times the number in the info_spectrum. The "range" CHOP specifies a low and high frequency range to extract. The Trim CHOP extracts that range of Hz.  But to get the Hz, its sample range on the x-axis needs to be multiplied by the Hz per sample.See also graphPlot in the Community Posts.

label: Blend
text: In a Blend CHOP, the multiple  channels of input 1 are the blend weights for all the other inputs.In this example the sliders control how much each wave will be taken into account during the blend. In Proportianal, all three waves are blended proportionally, so you  always get a value between the three waves. When all three weights are 0,  it give a discontinuous result when one goes off-zero.With Differencing, it is preferable in  most cases. With weights at 0, it starts with the first wave and the other 2 channels are added to it depending on the the weighting channels.The Trim CHOPs make the weight channels 600 samples long (10 seconds) like the 3 wave CHOPs, as waves and weights are blended sample-by-sample.Experiment with different values and watch the output wave.

label: Blob Track
text: fakeData1 simulates an object moving back and forth in X in a rectangle -5 to +5.It simulates what a scanner would see from a distant point in -Y where one object is being occluded by another object.blobRender1 shows the process. The blob area in the Area of Interest, defined by the boulding box corner parameters.White is a dot for every X Y sample. fakeData1_ is 1000 samples long.The red dots are the centers of the detected blobs that are output from the CHOP. The red lines are actually boxes surrounding the points that are associated with the blob.The white lines at the top are excluded from the blob detection because they are outside the blob bounding box.Lost Blob Timeout was increased to alow for one blob going behind the other.

label: Blob Track
text: This is the kind of data one would expect from a radial scanner when the data is converted from its (distance, angle) form into a (x, y) form.Because the moving blob is going in a curved path, Max Blob Movement had to be raised to qualify as the same blob that went behind the stationary blob.(fakeData1 here is the same as fakeData1 in example1 but with radial toggle turned on.)

label: Body Track
text: This is the NVIDIA body Track solution as part of their Maxine kit.It does a 2D solve (z=0), and tries to do a 3D solve as well.The coordinates of the detected features are given in u, v positions relative to the bottom-left corner of the input image. By default, the values range from 0 to 1, but the 'Aspect Correct' parameter can be enabled to scale the values so that they can be used as 3D coordinates while maintaining the aspect ratio of the original image.

label: Clip Blender
text: This is an example of the clipblender working with the clip CHOP.  The clipblender is an animation clip player.  It loads animation from only clip CHOPs.You will see Quaternion attributes are applied if you look at the info for a clip CHOP vs it's input.The animation clips are retrieved from the humanoid FBX archive.The clipblender CHOP references an empty table DAT called sequence.  The clipblender works by reading the sequence DAT when its contents change - particularly when an row is added.  The row must contain a valid path to a clipCHOP.  When a valid clipCHOP is found with  animation The clipblender CHOP will load that new clip and blend it with the currently playing clip.  When the new clip has played through the clipBlender will look again in the sequence DAT for another clip to play.  If it doesnt find one then it will continuously loop the new clip until another clip request is made.To see this in action run the runAnimSequence dat. - Right click on the DAT and select Run Script from pop menu or just press CTRL R.The animation in this FBX example is not correctly setup for blending so the animation has discontinuties and is not blending.----Alternalte descriptopn:The "clipblender CHOP" has the ability to read animation data that is stored in "clip CHOP"s.  The clipblender CHOP requires a default animation to start.  This default animation will be loaded and played in a loop until the clipblender receives a request to play a new clip.New clip requests may be sent to the clipblender CHOP using a table DAT that contains a column of clip names, which server as a playlist for the clipblender to consume. When the table DAT - which in this example is called "sequence" - contains rows, and the clipblender is reaching the end of the clip it is currently playing, it will look to the DAT list for a next clip to play, which is to be found at the top of the list.When the clipblender CHOP loads the clip listed in DAT list, it will delete that row from the table.  This process will be repeated until the DAT List is empty.  When the DAT List is empty the clipblender will continue to loop the animation clip that it ended up on.The python method clipblenderCHOP.trigger() forces the clipblender to look into the DAT List immediately. Take notice of the "runAnimSequence" script DAT. The way this example works, is it will create an animation sequence in the DAT List table - ['clipPunch', 'clipShot', 'clipRun'].Once the sequence table is populated with valid clipCHOP names, the clipblender CHOP is triggered and the clipblender CHOP starts consuming clipCHOP names from the sequence table, playing each clip, then consuming the next one, until it empties the table.The clipblender will blend the animation based on the Blend Time and Next Blend Time.  The Blend Time is used when the specified clip chop is loaded when the clipblender is triggered.  The next Blend Time is used when the clip is currently being played and is ending and being blended with a new clip.  The current clip's "Next Blend Time" length is used to blend out of said clip into a new clip.The script can be run over and over to restart the process. -----Extra notes:The animation clips are selected out of the humanoid FBX component.  Notice on the Play page the animation parameter has a field drop menu parameter populated with "run", "punch" and "shot".  The FBX component Play Mode parameter is set to Output Full Range.  This permits the Import Select CHOP to extract an entire animation clip from the FBX archive.  Therefore animationRun, Punch and Shot are extracted into their own respective Import Select CHOPs.Its important to note that the clipblender CHOP requires all channels for each clip to match in order and channel name and number of channels. On closer inspection the animationRun CHOP has 263 channels while animationPunch and Shot have 353.  Therefore a trim and replace CHOP are used to match the length and channel order and naming for all chops.  Once cleaned and matching the channels are sent into the clip chops and are ready for playback in the clipblender.

label: Constant
text: A lot of people use a Constant CHOP to hold their control data. But custom parameters are much better in several ways.- readable labels- default values (easy to reset to default via RMB)- slider goes in any range, not just 0-1- min/max clamping- integer controls can be strictly integers- toggles and pulses too, menus/strings too- multi-pages better- easy to re-order- section dividers- more standard way to set values with pythonTo get it outputting channels, use a Parameter CHOP inside.We love the Constant CHOP but maybe it's time to switch!

label: Copy
text: The Copy CHOP produces multiple copies of the second input along the timeline of the first input.The second input is copied at the first input's trigger points only. A trigger point occurs whenever the first input's channel crosses the Copy CHOP's Trigger Threshold value. In the node "merge_viewMe" you can see the pulse triggering the copies.

label: Copy
text: The Copy CHOP produces multiple copies of the second input along the timeline of the first input.The second input is copied at the first input's trigger points only. A trigger point occurs whenever the first input's channel crosses the Copy CHOP's Trigger Threshold value. The Copy CHOP can copy channels in two different modes:One Channel per Template Channel - Each output channel is a channel from the first input combined with the corresponding channel from the second input.One Channel per Copy Channel - Each output channel is a channel from the second input, with copies triggered by every channel of the first input.

label: Copy

label: Copy
text: The Copy CHOP copies the values of "dataChannel" additively onto the timeline of "triggerChannel".Stamping is enabled on the Copy CHOP. This means its second input is recooked for every copy. The Stamp parameters are also re-calculated for each copy.In this example the parameter "copySwitch" has a Python expression which makes it zero if me.copyIndex is even, and 1 when it's odd. This parameter is used in the upstream Switch CHOP, which has its index parameter set to the Python expression:fetchStamp("copySwitch",0)The result is for every copy the Switch CHOP outputs a different input.In the node "merge_viewMe" you can see the pulse triggering the copies.

label: DMX Out
text: This is the most basic usage of the DMX Out CHOP. In "Packet Per Sample" format, the DMX Out CHOP is expecting up to 512 single sample channels.If you need to send non-consecutive DMX addresses, use something like a Constant (or Replace) CHOP to add spacer channels.  The names of the channels (especially names with numbers in them) have no effect on which DMX channels the data is sent out to. Only the channel index matters.Make sure to scale all of the input channels to 0 through 255, though you don't need to round those numbers to integers before they're sent.In this example, two RGBAW lighting fixtures are patched to DMX Addresses 11 and 21, and it is being sent to the 4th Art-Net universe (AKA Universe 3 as Art-Net is base zero)

label: DMX Out
text: If you going to send multiple DMX universes from a single DMX Out CHOP,  you can only do so in "Packet Per Channel" format. Here, each channel is a universe and each sample is a DMX value.Remember you can only send up to 512 dmx values ranging between 0 and 255 per universe. The "Net", "Subnet" parameters are for older Art-Net implementations that split every 16 universes into 16 subnets and then 128 nets. These parameters do NOT refer to IP Addresses or Subnet Masks.NOTE: You don't have to increment the Net and Subnet parameters to get to higher universes numbers, just set the Universe parameter to any number and TD will split it into nets and subnets internally.By default, the DMX Out CHOP comes with a routing table docked. In "Packet Per Channel" format, this allows you to specify which CHOP channel name gets sent to which DMX Universe number. You can send the same CHOP channel to multiple DMX universes in any order.If you clear the "Routing Table" parameter, TouchDesigner will use the net, subnet and Universe parameters in the DMX Out's parameters to send the first universe of data and then every consecutive universe after that.

label: DMX Out
text: Here is an example of an LED tape PixelMapping setup that uses SOPs to define where from the "raster" RGB values should be sampled from. The key to performant PixelMapping is controlling what does and does not cook.  In this case, none of the SOPs cook and there is only one TOPto CHOP.There is also a rough preview system setup where a circle (representing a single LED) is being instanced using the positions of the LEDs obtained from the SOPTo CHOP and the color that is being send out to the LED controller.

label: DMX Out
text: Takes care of both cases Per Sample and Per Channel

label: Envelope
text: The Envelope CHOP outputs the maximum amplitude in the vicinity of each sample of the input. It takes the absolute value of the input, and uses a sliding window of a number of samples to find the maximum amplitude near each sample. The size of the sampling window is the envelope width.In this snippet, look at the contents of each Merge CHOP to see the original channel (yellow) and the resulting envelope (red).Envelope CHOP is different than Lag or Filter CHOPs in that its output value is always higher than the input, for every sample.merge_envelopeAroundPositiveInput shows that Envelope ins built around the input made positive.(absolute value).

label: Envelope
text: THis is the Envelope CHOP on live data.In this snippet, look at the contents of each Train CHOP to see the original channel (yellow) and the resulting envelope (red).merge_envelopeAroundPositiveInput shows that Envelope ins built around the input made positive.(absolute value).

label: Event
text: The Event CHOP can be a great tool to trigger overlapping events. In this example keep clicking button1 at different intervals to  create instances of a Tube SOP. The ADSR settings here will make the adsr channel simply rise once triggered and fallback to 0 once the Attack Level is reached.

label: Event
text: The Event CHOP also accepts multiple inputs. The index channel will return the value of the input that was just triggered. In this example you can create different textured instances of a cube. The adsr channel is used to lookup the y position and rotation of the cube over the events lifetime while the index channel is used to lookup the x position as well as the used texture.

label: Event
text: You can also trigger events in the Event CHOP using python. This is especially usefull as it lets you define the ADSR parameters per event. In this example we are giving each event a random speed.op('event3').createEvent(speed=randomSpeed)The id channel is used to lookup a different color for each instanced geometry.

label: Event
text: One TOP is created per row.Note that the id that item1 renders for example, changes. Sometimes it doesn't matter, like when there is no info preserved in the replicants from the last frame it cooks.Here we stuff expressions into the parameters when the replicants are created. Because there is no row 1 sometimes, circle1 cannot have expressions that  refer to row 1.

label: Expression
text: This is a very useful network when working with inconsistent / noisy devices.  In  this case, if the button is down for the  length in seconds expressed in the speed1 Maximum parameter value, then the output goes on.There are several ways to do this!

label: Extend
text: The Extend CHOP sets the "extend conditions" of a CHOP, which determines what values you get when sampling the CHOP before or after its interval. To illustrate this we're sampling the same CHOP (pattern1) 5 times, each time giving pattern1 CHOP different extend conditions. We will sample pattern1 before, during, and after its interval, by driving a Lookup CHOP from -2 to 3 (while the Lookup CHOP's normal range is 0-1). This way we can see the extend conditions visually in each Trail CHOP:(To zoom the graphs of the Extend CHOPs, with Viewer Active, click/drag left-right with middle mouse.)trail1: extend conditions are set to cycle, so the pattern loops.trail2: extend conditions are set to hold. So anything sampled before the interval is holding the first sample of the pattern1 CHOP, (in this case 0), and anything sampled after the interval is holding the last sample of the pattern1 CHOP, (in this case 0).Tip: change the "phase" parameter of pattern1 to see the effect in trail2.trail3: extend left is set to hold. Extend right is set to mirror. trail4: extend left is set to a value of -1, and extend right is set to cycle.trail5: extend left is set to hold, extend right is set to Slope, so sampling after the interval of pattern1 will continue the slope of pattern1.

label: Face Track
text: Four examples here.Unbypass videodevin1 first, and get you on camera.  It fits it to be 16x9 as some of the sloppy math of this assumes 16x9.top-left - estimates head position in 3D space and moves the virtual camera as if you are looking at the cube from different angles. Put render1 in a floating viewer near your line of sight when looking at your webcam.mid-left - a Rectangle TOP transformed with x, y, and rotate (rz) - purely 2D.Bottom left -  This uses the NVIDIA-provided face model. This network then transforms it to be placed over the video of your face.right - all the  face tracking points instanced as stars.

label: Fan
text: This example takes an LFO and fans out the value  into 8 channels.Quantization keeps the output values to strictly on and off.

label: Fan
text: The same as the example above, without quantization. This means the channels blend from the current to the next smoothly.

label: Fan
text: You can also use a Fan CHOP on  non Timesliced  channels.

label: Fan
text: The Fan CHOP Operation is set to Fan In to convert a bunch of 0-1 channels into a single integer.Select CHOP is an alternate to a Merge CHOP where you don't have to wire anything. LOgic CHOP's Channel Pre-OP is set to Radio to hold the last button pressed.

label: Feedback
text: The Feedback CHOP allows connecting CHOPs in circular loops without Infinite Recursion errors. It simply copies its input without cooking it first. In this example the output of lfo1 is used to set a parameter on that same lfo1.Set the switch1 index parameter to 1 to circumvent the Feedback CHOP, and notice the yellow "cook dependency" error popping up.

label: Feedback
text: The Feedback CHOP stores channels from the current frame to be used in a later frame, without forcing recooking back one frame. It allows you to get the state of a CHOP as it was one frame or time slice ago. In this example currentFrame channel  CHOP has the value of the current frame number. When this number is subtracted from the same channel fed through a Feedback CHOP, the result is -1.

label: Filter
text: The Filter CHOP most often is used to smooth out fast changes.Type "Box" gives more linear smoothing.

label: Filter
text: This example shows a filter de-spiking an incoming pulse.

label: Filter
text: Here are a few examples of different filtering types. You can see their different attributes in the Trail CHOPs.Note the graphs are set to a minimum/max value of 0 to 1 or -1 to 1.

label: Filter
text: Filter each sample using the Filter per Sample parameter.This turns Time Slicing off.The Lag CHOP has the same feature.

label: Filter
text: Filter each sample using the Filter per Sample parameter.This turns Time Slicing off.The Lag CHOP has the same feature.

label: Filter
text: Apply a different filter amount for each channel using expressions.Or you can put different filter times in a Constant CHOP.

label: Filter
text: tx2 is the One Euro filter - responds quickly to large chnges in user input while smoothing out the jittery data.  See Filter CHOP for a procedure to adapt to your use cases.

label: Filter
text: If you are applying a Filter CHOP to any channels, and at some point you want to jump immediately to the input values and not do the filter, you can (1) pulse the Reset Parameter on the Filter CHOP(2) toggle on the Reset parameter until you want to return to filtering again.(3) send a value of 1 into the third input (0 otherwise). Click the Reset button for example.

label: Function
text: Look at the range of each Pattern CHOP feeding the Function CHOPs.Then look at what values you get out of the Function CHOP based on its input channel.The first example is 100 sample long, and you can see one dot per sample.

label: Function
text: Look at the range of each Pattern CHOP feeding the Function CHOPs.These deal with the triginomic functions - the basic sine, cosine and tangent functions.You by default give it an angle in degrees, and if the radius is 1, it gives you the y value for sin, the x value for cos, and the y/x value for tangent. function_sin_radians shows that by changing hte Angle Units parameter, you can give it not degrees but radians (remember there are 3.14 radians in a half-circle, 6.28 for a whole circle).or when Angle Units is set to Cycles, that 1 means the same thing as 360 degrees, a full rotation.The inverse cos, invere sin and inverse tan outputs an angle. Think of it this way... if the sine is .5, then what is the angle?Arc tangents (atan and atan2) are more useful when you want to get out an angle... if you have point x,y, and you feed it to atan2, what is the angle ... play with constant_x and constant_y.

label: Function
text: Logs and their opposite, exponentials are very useful when dealing with numbers that have a high range and/or tiny range.function_log10 - think of it this way - the input is 10 to the power of what? The input goes up to 1000. That's 10 x 10 x 10, so log10 of 1000 is 3.  So 3 is the highest value of function_log10.( Expert note: The error page deals with numbers that are infinite or interminate. log10(0) is -infinity, so you can choose what to output for those cases on the Errors page.pattern2 shows what you get when the numbers are very small, under 1. Negative input number give undefined output values so they are clamped to -3.Instead of 10 as a base, the most common base is 2 as you see in function_log2 which uses the LogN function. When base Value is 2, these are the powers of 2.  So where pattern3 is 2 to the power of 5 (2**5) = 32, then function_log2 is 5.function_ln uses the "natural log", which is a special exponent 2.718. What's so special about that is the ln(x) is always x, which mathematicians like.

label: Function
text: The pow function is the most useful... There are two forms of it:base ^ input   and  input ^ exponent   (here ^ means "to the power of", same as **)In the first form, for  base ^ input, with a base of 2, it outputs the powers of 2. When input is 5, output is 2**5 = 32.pow with a base of 10, the output is 10**5 = 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 = 100,000.For the second form where input ^ exponent, you can get the squares, cubes and square roots, for example.  like input ** 2.These can be used to shape inputs slightly, like when you want a slider to have more fine control at the lower end or upper end. Though the S Curve CHOP does this well too.

label: Function
text: These are the relationships of decibels, power and amplitude in audio.20 dB is a factor of 10 in amplitude, or 100 in power.You can use mclick on nodes to get their lower/upper ranges.The Error page is used on some examples to deal with cases like 0 amplitude is -infinity decibels.

label: Gesture
text: Make any 1D slider auto-gesture by putting the autoGesture component inside your slider (or beside it).It works by pressing Shift before you click/drag/release on the Slider or Container COMP.After you release, it will repeat until the next time you click the slider without pressing Shift.Filter Time gives smoothing control.autoGestureContainer was changed to work with Container COMPs - autoGestureContainer's panel is simply set to 'u v'.You can look inside autoGesture to see what it's doing.This component may also be found in the Palette.

label: Gesture
text: Look in the Palette for gestureCapture in Techniques for a more complete system for capturing and maintaining multiple gestures, putting them into a set of keyframes, and outputting smoothed channels. Each gesture is any set of channels.

label: Hold
text: This example samples the value of input 1, whenever input 2 of the Hold CHOP is on.

label: Hold
text: This example features non Timesliced inputs, where the Hold CHOP samples input 1, whenever input 2 turns on.

label: Hold
text: This example is different in that the Hold CHOP will continuously sample input 1 for as long as input 2 is On.

label: Hold
text: same example but timesliced.

label: Hold
text: This is the Family Feud technique, if you're familiar with American TV (for 3 families).c to clear. first one to hit the 1, 2 or 3 keys is the winner and nobody else can override.logicOr - if any of the 3 keys are pressed, this goes on and timer1 starts, the running channel goes on, renamed winner.fanIn -> mathMult gives 1, 2 or 3 based on which key is pressed.when the running channel goes on, hold1 samples the input value, which is 1, 2 or 3 (it favors lower number if two or more are pressed at same time)Press "c" and the timer resets, making running be 0, making mathMult be 0.timer1 is set to cycle (run) forever until it is initialized again.you could detect ties by adding the 3 channels in merge1 and affecting mathAddOne to be 4 if sum is 2 or more. you need a new Constant TOP for that.

label: Hold
text: This example helps to avoid discontinuity. When the timer loops, the Noise CHOP jumps in value (it uses its tx position in its input to sample noise space).The Slope CHOP jumps every loop (see noise1 channel in trail1). slope1 and logic1 detect when the timer loops. (You can use the Cycle Pulse parameter on a Timer CHOP instead.)When the second input of hold1 goes to 0, it holds the previous slope2 value, minimizing the effect of the pulse. This works even if you are dropping frames (try it by setting the Hog CHOP to .05 seconds).

label: In
text: The In CHOP is used to create a CHOP input in a Component. Component inputs are positioned alphanumerically on the left side of the Component.In this example base1 COMP has a green CHOP input on its left side. Zoom into base1 to see the In CHOP, and notice the In CHOP has an LFO CHOP actiong as fallback data connected to its second input. This will be output by the In CHOP if nothing is connected to the CHOP input of its parent, the base1 COMP.Zoom out of base1 and connect constant_new_input to base1, and see the output change.Also, when you rollover the input, you see a custom label, set with the In TOP's Label parameter. Similar for the output.

label: Info
text: Many TOPs have useful information that can be accessed using an Info CHOP, such as index, resolutions, file lengths, and etc.

label: Info
text: Many CHOPs have useful information such as index fraction, file length in frames, audio sample rates, number of channels, and etc.

label: Info
text: You can get a lot of information about a SOP using the Info CHOP. You can get number and position of points, number of primitives, cook times, and etc.

label: Info
text: You can get information about many COMPs, such as useful information about a Window COMPs location, size, and whether it is open.

label: Interpolate
text: The Interpolate CHOP treats its multiple-inputs as keyframes and interpolates between them. The inputs are usually single-frame CHOP channels like those produced by a Constant CHOP. Here each Constant CHOP is not always located at frame 1 of the timeline. constant2 has a start/end time set to frame 500, and constant3 is at frame 1000. Middle-click on the node to see these numbers in the pop-up info.The Interpolate CHOP first sorts the input CHOPs in time (without shifting them) and interpolates between them to fill the gaps.The Constant CHOPs have their 'start' parameter set to different values, so they can be sorted in time. (See the Channel page of their parameter window)The number of channels in the output of the Interpolate CHOP is the same as the number of channels in the first input. Notice each Interpolate CHOP is using a different interpolation shape (ease in, easy out, lineair, etc)

label: Join
text: This joins CHOPs end-to-end.join2 overlaps the two with a blend region.join3 shows you can re-use inputs.Join4 shows how you can specify inputs using the CHOPs parameter.join5 inserts a blend region with a cubic interpolation curve.All the graphs are set to -1 to +1, with unit Samples on the x-axis (via the RMB menu with Viewer Active on.

label: Keyboard In
topic: control Geo Text COMP from keyboard
text: The Keyboard In CHOP receives ASCII input from the keyboard, and outputs channels for the number of keys specified. It creates a single-frame channel representing the current state of each key. In this example the numeric keys 123456 influence the size of their corresponding characters in a Geo Text COMP. The Left Control key decreases green and blue in the fontcolor, making the output appear red.How to test: press the 1,2,3,4,5,6 and Left Ctrl keys on your keyboard.

label: Lag
text: Equally lags value in both directions.

label: Lag
text: Much more lag applied on increasing values.

label: Lag
text: Overshoots final value by 1 second.

label: Lag
text: The Lag CHOP is useful for  smoothing out noise.

label: Lag
text: Example of smoothing non-timesliced values.

label: Lag
text: This shows how to reset a lag, but using its reset value.

label: Lag
text: rises at a constant rate (.5 per second), and  falls at a different constant rate ( .25 per second) as per Lag's Slope  parameters.Just click anywhere on slider1 to set the target value.NOTE: The Trail CHOP is forced to display in the range 0 to 1: turn on Viewer Active, right-click -> Vertical Adapt -> 0 to 1

label: Lag
text: Lag each sample using the Lag per Sample parameter.The Filter CHOP has the same feature.

label: Lag
text: If you are applying a Lag CHOP to any channels, and at some point you want to jump immediately to the input values ane not do the lag, you can (1) pulse the Reset Parameter on the Filter CHOP(2) toggle on the Reset parameter until you want to return to lagging again.

label: Lag
text: Capture the maximum value cumulatively.slowly increase and decrease the Constant CHOP value. The value does not go down.And you can Reset.Achieved by not allowing Lag CHOP to decrease in value via Clampo Slope parameter.

label: Laser
text: a few ways to generate laser patterns.

label: Laser
text: Often you want to trace an image that has a lot of contrast variation.You will want to work in as low resolution that you can to minimize the number of pixels the Trace SOP needs to deal with.It's better to feed the Trace SOP a monochrome image.  mono has a single-channel Pixel Formt to minimize memory.blur_softenCurves eliinates the tiny detail that you don't need for lasers.level_applyGammaOrWhatever you can adjust gamma or leve to get a nice contrast.lookup_offToOnTransitionsForTrace with the of-to-on steps in pattern_steps gives an image with a lot of zero to one transitions which is wht Trace will follow to generate its polygons.  It is like generatingOften you want to trace an image that has a lot of contrast variation.You will want to work in as low resolution that you can to minimize the number of pixels the Trace SOP needs to deal with.It's better to feed the Trace SOP a monochrome image.  mono has a single-channel Pixel Formt to minimize memory.blur_softenCurves eliminates the tiny detail that you don't need for lasers.level_applyGammaOrWhatever you can adjust gamma or level to get a nice contrast.lookup_offToOnTransitionsForTrace with the off-to-on steps in pattern_steps gives an image with a lot of zero to one transitions which is what Trace will follow to generate its polygons.  It is like generating topographic lines for brightness gradients. On pattern_steps you can adjust Number of Cycles and/or Phase.trace_adjustStepSize with point displayed lets you adjust how many points you generate and the un-snoothness you can accept.Then look at your laser beam and re-adjust everything. topographic lines for brightness gradients. On pattern_steps you can adjust Number of Cycles and/or Phase.trace_adjustStepSize with point displayed lets you adjust how many points you generate and the un-snoothness you can accept.Then look at your laser beam and re-adjust everything.

label: Laser Device
text: The Laser Device CHOP receives channels from the Laser CHOP andpasses it on to a supported DAC such as:* Etherdream* Helios or* ShowNETHelios/ShowNET both have auto-populating menus for devices.biggest issue people may run into are IP discovery for EtherDream, but we have the EtherDream DAT for that.

label: LFO
text: The LFO CHOP can  produce many different wave types ranging from Sine waves to Pulses.

label: LFO
text: You can create multiple  channel of LFOs at once using patterns in the  Channel Name parameter.

label: LFO
text: You can control the octave of an LFO using the first input of an LFO CHOP.Octave is different than frequency control. If the frequency is and the octave control is 1, the frequency doubles to .6. If the octave control is -1, the frequency halves to .2.(Octave is exponential (powers of 2), frequency is linear)

label: LFO
text: You can purposely use phasing to create opposite wave forms.

label: LFO
text: You can optionally input your own source wave  into the LFO chops third input.

label: Limit
text: In chopto2, if you imagine your camera is in the center, that is, math2 is your camera zx position, it will always see a field of data in front of it.limit1 recycles the points.chopto1 is a semi-randomized field of points.

label: Limit
text: The Limit CHOP will always clamp the input between the assigned minimum and maximum values.

label: Limit
text: This Limit CHOP will always loop between the maximum and minimum values, regardless of input value

label: Limit
text: When the Limit CHOP reaches it's maximum, it then descends to the minimum, before it ascends to the maximum again.

label: Limit
text: This example limits the output to positive values only.

label: Limit
text: This example normalizes the output value between -1 to 1.

label: Limit
text: These examples quantize the incoming value.The first quantizes to value steps of 1. The second quantizes to value steps of 0.5.

label: Limit
text: This Limit CHOP will always loop between the maximum and minimum values, regardless of input value

label: Logic
text: This example goes off when zero or less.

label: Logic
text: This example inverts the  "Off When Zero or Less" so that it's On when zero  or less.

label: Logic
text: This example turns off when the value is outside the bounds -0.5 and 0.5

label: Logic
text: This example takes 0 to 1 inputs pulses and creates a toggle value.

label: Logic
text: This example turns, and stays on, while the value is being changed.

label: Logic
text: This example will stay on if one OR the other incoming value is not 0.

label: Logic
text: Both input1  AND input2 have to be on for the Logic CHOP to turn on.

label: Logic
text: This example shows the above functions performed on a non-Timesliced input.

label: Logic
text: This example shows using OR and AND, similar to the above OR and AND examples, with non Timesliced input.

label: Logic
text: a way to make a single-frame pulse:click button_momentary

label: Logic
text: Example of how to make radio button functionality from regular momentary buttons.

label: Logic
text: You can use Logic CHOP to find the range of inputs.

label: Logic
text: Time Slice is default On (logic1, logic3, logic5). That means the start-end is at the current timeline frame.It always causes the down-stream operators to cook. Middle-click on logic1, null1.Time Slice is turned off for logic2, logic4, logic6. The Null CHOPs are not cooking (dashed line is off) and their result remains the same as the input at Frame 1.Time Slice off has the same effect as having it on for the first 2 examples (press Button to see).But logic5 and logic6 depend on the value of the previous frame (Channel Pre OP is set to Rising Edge), so Time Slice must be on for that to work properly (logic5).Turn Time Slice Off when appropriate.

label: Logic
text: You can use the Logic CHOP with the Math CHOP to do comparison operations like  >  <  >=  <=  ==  !=limit1 and limit2 are samples that have been quantized for the purpose of this example.Logic CHOPs here need the Time Slice parameter off since the input is multi-sample.

label: Lookup
text: Interporlation avoids stepping that can occur between two values.

label: Lookup
text: This gives fine control at the lower end of the slider.The slider is allowed to go above its  0-1 range (you can set Clamp U High on) and because the Extend Right condition on the Wave CHOP is set to Slope, when  the slider goes above its 0-1 range, the lookup value goes up linearly above 10.The Trail CHOP's viewer display is set to be fixed at the 0 to 10 range.

label: Lookup
text: You can scrub through the Triangle wave using a 0-1 index.

label: Lookup
text: You can scrub through the Sine wave using a 0-1 index.

label: Lookup
text: A Lookup CHOP can perform its operation on multiple input indices, using multiple input lookup tables.

label: Lookup
text: A Lookup CHOP can also perform a lookup of 3 different indices across a single lookup table.

label: Lookup
text: If your input is an on-off channel per key (like the Keyboard CHOP or MIDI In CHOP), then pressing a key can cause a lookup into a table to get the corresponding r, g, b values.

label: LTC In
text: Linear Time Code (LTC) can be generated and interpreted by TouchDesigner.ltcout2 is driven by a Timer CHOP.  ltcin2 has to be set to an Input Frame Rate of 60, since that is the sample rate that the Timer CHOP is driving ltcout2.

label: Math
text: Math can perform a variety of mathematical operations on multiple input CHOPs.

label: Math
text: Math can also be performed on multiple channels in a single CHOP.math_length treats its chnnels as a vector and computes its length. That is   squareroot of (x*x + y*y + z*z)  math_length1 doe the same thing in 3 steps, but still in a single CHOP.

label: Math
text: You can use the To and From Ranges to scale values, like a -1 to 1 sine wave LFO into a 0 to 10 sine wave.You can do the same thing using a Pre-Add, Multiply, Post-Add.Use whatever is convenient.In math_invert, is helpful tobe able to invert button and slider ranges from 0 to 1, and instead making them from 1 to 0.

label: Math
text: You can also use Math CHOPs to block values from reaching a certain destination by multiplying them by the value of a button, which is 0 when off, and 1 when on. This means the value would remain 0 while the button is off, but the value would be passed unaffected if the button is on.

label: Math
text: It is helpful tobe able to invert button and slider ranges from 0 to 1, and insteadmaking them from 1 to 0.

label: Math
topic: pre and post multiply and add
text: You can affect a limited set of channels by using Scope on the Common page.You can list the channel names to affect. Or you can use pattern matching to select a bunch of channels.Search for "Pattern Matching" in the wiki.

label: Math
text: This take 2500 points of a grid, converts it to 2500 samples of a CHOP, use the first Math CHOP to subtract an XYZ position, then the second Math CHOP to compute a distance from teh XYZ position, then usesa a Lookup CHOP to simylate a wave displacement.The CHOP channels are then convereted back to a SOP.

label: Math
text: noise1 and noise2 are both non-timeslied and have the same number of samples. So they are added sample-by-sample in math_non_timeslice.math_timeslice takes a timesliced CHOP and a non-time-sliced CHOP by taking a slice of the non-timesliced CHOP (at the current frame, seen by turning on Timebar on noise4's viewer.) and combines the two together.

label: Math
text: You can apply a different range to each of 3 channels using 3 Math CHOPs and the Scope parameter on the Common page, which applies the operation to only channels you select.Scope is common on many CHOP filter operators.Another way to apply a different range per-channel is to use me.chanIndex in a parameter expression, which makes the expression get evaluated once per channel, where me.chanIndex is 0 for the first channel, 1 for the second channel and so on.   me.chanIndex is an index into the list [0, 5, 99].  chanIndex is available in about 15 CHOPs. Look at a CHOP's Class page to see if it's available for a particular CHOP.

label: Math
text: With abrupt volume changes, audio will pop.Listen to audiodevout1. Yank the slider of constant1 up and down rapidly. The audio tone will pop when the value changes because the sine wave changes value abruptly when the volume changes from one frame to the next.On the Math CHOP, turn on "Interp Pars per Sample". Yank the slider around again. The audio does not pop.The timeline steps forward about 1/60 second every time step. Parameters get sampled once oer time step.  In that time, about 735 audio samples play (44,100 samples per second times 1/60 second). With this Math CHOP option on, the Math CHOP's Multiply parameter is interpolated from the previous frame's value to the current value, in 735 steps in this case, and multipied by the 735 audio samples.

label: Merge
text: Merging multiple channels into 1 CHOP.

label: Merge
text: If multiple channels of the same name are  merged, you can automatically give them unique names using the Duplicate Names options.

label: Merge
text: This allows you to keep only the first of the duplicate named channels being merged.

label: Merge
text: You can merge inputs from a  variety of CHOPs.

label: Merge
text: You can merge non timesliced channels that have multiple samples. You can also merge non timesliced channels, then apply timeslicing to them.

label: MIDI In
topic: map MIDI CHOP channels to parameters
text: This uses a mapping table to change parameters based on MIDI controller messages.First, you need a MIDI device attached. Use Dialogs -> MIDI Device Mapper to Create New Mapping, and select your device from the menu under In Device.Make sure the Device ID parameter of the MIDI In CHOP matches the ID of the MIDI Device Mapper.  (See wiki and Dialogs -> MIDI Device Mapper)Then when you move your controllers on your MIDI device, new channels will appear in midiin1.There are several ways to map MIDI messages to parameters, but here, add a row in 'map' for each controller, where the first column contains a name combining the channel and controller numbers as shown. ... ch1ctrl3 for example.The "op" column is the path to the operator to modify, and the "par" column is the parameter name to change. The "channel" and "ctrl" columns are not used in this example.The 0-127 range of the MIDI controller value is re-ranged to 0-1, then to the "low" to "high" range, then written to the parameter.You can change built-in parameters (pattern1) or custom parameters (base1).This deals with MIDI controllers. MIDI Note events and other MIDI message types can be handled in a similar way.

label: MIDI In
topic: map MIDI via export, chan reference, bind
text: The more direct (and less flexible) way to map MIDI channel values to parameters is via exporting or by channel references, or binding bia the Bind CHOP.Note that the Export Method should be by channel name (Common page of "prep").(See Bind CHOP for the binding procedure.)

label: MIDI In Map
text: The MIDI In Map CHOP is for pre-configured devices you see in Dialogs -> MIDI Device Mapper.  Otherwise you can simply use the MIDI In CHOP.You can process MIDI events with the  MIDI In DAT as well. Use the MIDI Device Mapper dialog (in Dialogs dropdown) to select your device. Be sure that the Device ID in the MIDI In Map CHOP matches the ID declared in the MIDI Device Mapper Dialog.Also, make sure that your devices are plugged in before you open TouchDesigner.

label: Mouse In
topic: get mouse when outside TD window
text: This gets the mouse position even when the cursor is outside the TouchDesigner window. Ironically it doesn't use the Mouse In CHOP.  It runs on WIndows-only it appears.The Nouse In CHOP works specigivally with TouchDesigner windows.

label: Mouse Out
text: The Mouse Out CHOP forces the mouse position and button status to be driven from TouchDesigner using the incoming CHOP channels.On clicking the button, the current mouse position will be stored using a Hold CHOP. Also a Timer CHOP starts, which acts as a lookup index for the Lookup CHOP. Its output is multiplied (using a Math CHOP) with the current mouse position, and the new mouse position is sent to the Mouse Out CHOP. The Timer CHOP is re-initialized when it done.This example doesn't work well with multi-monitors in general.

label: Noise
text: This is random generated noise.

label: Noise
text: Animating the seed creates motion in the noise.

label: Noise
text: Timesliced noise is useful if you're working with realtime signals.The Trail CHOP shows its time history.See "TimeSlicing" in the wikil. If you are dropping frames, it will output multiple frames of samples per-cook, which is why the time history is smooth.Middle-mouse click on the node to see how many frames of samples it is outputting.The Trigger CHOP can also output a full waveform or a single sample.

label: Noise
text: You can specify the length of the noise channel. In this case the noise is 10 samples long.

label: Noise
text: This noise's values' have been normalized to lie with 1 and -1.

label: Noise
text: You can animate noise by animating the Translate X parameter.

label: Noise
text: You can create multiple channels simulatenously by using a pattern in the  Channel Name. The Seed parameter is a constant value of 1, but interanlly each channel is generates with a slightly different unique seed.

label: Noise
text: This contextual example demonstrates adding noise to the points in  an animated 3d geometry.

label: Null
text: By default, Null does nothing and takes no memory, but it can be used to force-cook nodes before it to make sure operatoes cook when TouchDesigner is in Perform Mode or the nodes are not being displayed.OSC will only output when lfo1 is 1. Without the Null CHOP forcing everything to cook, nothing will happen in Perform Mode.

label: Object
text: For any 3D object (Target Object) you can get its transform relative to the world origin (by leaving Reference Object blank), or relative to another object (specify any Reference Object).  You can get just position or rotation (and more), or with the Compute menu set to Transform. the full set of 9 transform channels: the position, rotation (euler angles) and scale. (You will need to specify the Transform Order, and the Rotation Order that you want.)The Object CHOP takes into account all 3D object parenting hierarchies, and all manners of transforming the 3D Components, like the Bullet Solver COMP which transforms each moving object.The Compute menu set to Distance will give the straight-line distance from the origin of one object to the origin of the other (or the world origin).The Object CHOP ignores the SOPs (including Transform SOPs) inside the objects - they are irrelevant.

label: Object
text: These is an example by Matthew Ragan that was first posted in the forum athttps://forum.derivative.ca/t/object-chop-techniques/134436In this example we imagine that we have many objects in our scene, but we only want to know the distance between our assorted objects and one other object.Here we want to know the distance between our green spheres and our blue box.Each sphere has an object CHOP that's used to retrive the position measurement of our target geometry.Now read the other readMe DATs in here.

label: Object
text: These is an example by Matthew Ragan that was first posted in the forum athttps://forum.derivative.ca/t/object-chop-techniques/134436We might, however, want to measure the to our spheres from multiple reference points. We can still accomplish this with one object CHOP (that's doing the calculating at least).We need to first ensure that we have the positon information for our reference ojbects. Here our blue boxes are that element.We can use an object CHOP to find the position of these  geometry COMPs. Next we'll want to make sure that we have four samples in a single CHOP - our goal is to combine our position data into a single CHOP that we can use later. Now read the other readMe DATs in here.

label: Object
text: These is an example by Matthew Ragan that was first posted in the forum athttps://forum.derivative.ca/t/object-chop-techniques/134436Here each line is correctly rotated, scaled, and placed based on calculations from the object CHOP.Our joined data from our targets needs to be set up to cycle (or repeat). This makes sure that we CHOPs of mis-matched lengths are used together that there's an explict set of instructions about what happens. Each set of samples coresponds to a sphere, and we want that to repeat when it's used in the next step.Now read the other readMe DATs in here.

label: Object
text: These is an example by Matthew Ragan that was first posted in the forum athttps://forum.derivative.ca/t/object-chop-techniques/134436You can also use the object CHOP to just calculate bearing - or rotation from one object to another. Here you can see how this might be used to rotate instances to sit flat on a sphere’s surface, or rotate an arrow to point towards an object:position measurement of our target geometry.

label: Object
text: These is an example by Matthew Ragan that was first posted in the forum athttps://forum.derivative.ca/t/object-chop-techniques/134436vative.ca/t/object-chop-techniques/134436Or you might use the combination of bearing and distance to make some strange abstract art.

label: Object
text: These is an example by Matthew Ragan that was first posted in the forum athttps://forum.derivative.ca/t/object-chop-techniques/134436Using Object CHOP to determine if objects are "selected" (in proximity) or not.Now read the other readMe DATs in here.

label: OSC In
text: These two examples both send and receive a sine wave over OSC.The top one is sending the data out as Timeslice, while the bottom one is using samples.In most cases you should use the "Sample" Format option in the OSC Out CHOPs as most other applications work with samples, and  not time slice.

label: OSC In
text: This example shows how to select specific channels when using an OSC In CHOP to only retreive  the channels you need.

label: Override
text: The Override CHOP lets you take inputs from several CHOP sources, and uses the most-recently changed input channels to determine the output. In this example, whenever the LFO is above zero, the CHOP Execute DAT will set a new seed in noise1, thereby generating new output values. Whenever the LFO is below zero, it will do the same for noise2.Notice the Override CHOP outputting the most-recently changed inputs. Also it can generate a channel showing which input has the most recently changed channel.This technique works, but you will have to use other techniques if you want to, for example, initialize it a certain way, or restore from values somewhere. For this you may need to store your values in tables and use the CHOP/DAT/Parameter/etc Execute DATs to manage the table and convert to CHOP channels.See also the Bind CHOP.

label: Panel
text: Here we can get the panel values from a button, including click state, rollover u and v, as well as rollover state.

label: Panel
text: Here we're monitoring the u and v, as well as which mouse button is  clicked over the panel.We're also monitoring whether the ctrl, alt, or shift keys are pressed over the slider.

label: Panel
text: We can monitor whether a Field COMP is selected and in Focus.

label: Panel
text: Here the Slider COMP is set to be a UV slider, or a 2-dimensional slider.

label: Panel
text: Similar to the previous slider example but also features rollover monitoring, and uses the  Circle TOP.

label: Panel
text: and even simpler using expressions in the Circle TOP.The insideu and insidev update even when not clicking the gadget, unlike rollu and rollv.

label: Pangolin
text: pangolin example

label: Pan Tilt
text: The Pan Tilt CHOP calculates the pan and tilt angle of a movinghead platform to point it into the direction of a target taking into consideration constarains of each axis.It requires as an input the transform between a target and the movinghead platform which can easily be obtained with the Object CHOP.If multiple platforms and / or targets need to be calculated, the Object CHOP's DAT Table parameter can be used with the first column being the targets and the second column the movinghead platforms.Something to remember is that with the rotational constrains of a platform, not all directions can be achieved directly but sometimes, the platform needs to counterrotate to be able to point towards the target.

label: Pan Tilt
text: The Pan Tilt CHOP calculates the pan and tilt angle of a movinghead platform to point it into the direction of a target taking into consideration constarains of each axis.It requires as an input the transform between a target and the movinghead platform which can easily be obtained with the Object CHOP.If multiple platforms and / or targets need to be calculated, the Object CHOP's DAT Table parameter can be used with the first column being the targets and the second column the movinghead platforms.Something to remember is that with the rotational constrains of a platform, not all directions can be achieved directly but sometimes, the platform needs to counterrotate to be able to point towards the target.

label: Parameter
text: get parameter values into a CHOP.This gets 3 specific channels among the Built-In parameters.

label: Parameter
text: Get parameter values into a DAT where you can hold non-numeric data like from strings and paths parameters.We use the Parameter CHOP par1, which is only used to get the channel names. We then convert it to a DAT where you can re-evaluate parameters including ones that are strings, paths, menus, etc in an Evaluate DAT.This gets all channels that are the Built-In parameters.

label: Pattern
text: Use the Range page to set range to 0-10, where you can also quantize values to integers.

label: Pattern
text: Convert from SOP points to CHOP samples, then feed that to Pattern that will create 3 random numbers for each sample, maintaining the same length as the incoming CHOP.Convert that back to a SOP and check the data with a SOP to DAT.

label: Pattern
text: One Pattern feeds another and they are added together via the Combine Channels parameter.

label: Pattern
text: Start with a Constant curve and taper it down.

label: Pattern
text: Take an input CHOP and taper it up from the beginning by setting the Combine Channels parameter to Multiply.

label: Pattern
text: Make 9 steps of 10 samples. In the Amplitude parameter, you need to multiply by .999 so that the last sample does not reach 10.  You make the Interger parameter Floor.  See also the Limit CHOP to quantize.

label: Pattern
text: Pattern CHOP can generate multiple or single random numbers. The Seed parameter can be given any number to generate a random number between 0 and 1.The seed can be driven by a slider, but a random number will recur when you go back to any value (which you may want sometimes).The Clock CHOP's "week" channel expresses the time-of-month as a 0-1 value, and changes by a small amount every frame. pattern3 will repeat every week.You can get a random number on-demand when a counter is incremented. mathRange offsets the seed numbers to asssure another counter doesn't generate the same sequence of random numbers. Connect lfo1 to count1 to get a random per-second.pattern5/shuffle1 gives 10 random numbers at a time.

label: Pattern
text: Say you have integers in a certain range, and you want to see all of them once, but in a random order.  This example gives the 77 numbers from 9 to 85 in random order. Furthermore, after you see them once, you will see them all again but in a different order. First it generates the linear sequence of desired numbers, then randomizes the order with the Sort CHOP.  Change the seed of Sort to change the order.trim1 selects a differnt sample per frame.timer1 causes Sort to re-randomize the order of the integers at the end of each 77 frame cycle.  It does this through the timer's callbacks, to prevent sort1 from cooking every frame.(You can also select cycles in timer1, pass it to a Null CHOP and set it to minimum cooks, and export that to sort1's seed parameter.)You can slow it all down with the Timer CHOP's Speed parameter.pattern2 randomly gives a different integer, but it may repeat a number soon after.

label: Pattern
text: The python object for the Pattern CHOP has a .chanIndex member, so if Pattern generates three channels you can put something like      [.3, .5, .7][me.chanIndex] in any parameter to customize its value for each channel. For example, in the Type parameter, put the expression     ['sin', 'cos', 'ramp'][me.chanIndex] to get 3 different curve types.

label: Perform
text: You can quickly access the FPS and frame cook  time of a project via the Perform CHOP. Milliseconds is a better measurement as it is not affected by the monitor refresh sync.

label: Perform
text: You can also access useful GPU and CPU information, such as memory usage and active operators in a network.

label: Perform
text: This example shows you if there are any frames skipped or dropped. 'cook' is 0 on frames that are skipped because the previous frame had not finished.

label: Perform
text: Un-bypass the Hog CHOP (the arrow flag on the node). The performance will reduce. The Perform CHOP's "cook" channel indicates frames are dropped.  Remember to bypass the Hog CHOP again, as it will cook every frame until it's bypassed.

label: Phaser
text: Author of CHOP and examples: David BraunThe Phaser CHOP is set of values (Num Samples), that start at 0, and will all rise to 1, each sample starting to rise at a different time, and rising at different rates.The first input is an index, often a time index, which must be between 0 and 1. Typically it is an LFO CHOP set to Ramp.When the index is 0, the Phaser CHOP will output 0 for all samples. When the index is 1, the Phaser CHOP will output 1 for all samples. Play with constant1.

label: Phaser
text: The second input to Phaser CHOP is a CHOP of "phase" samples, kind of like a time-offset. Phase samples must be between 0 and 1.The second input determines "which sample goes from 0 to 1 first" (samples with higher values go first), and also overrides the Num Samples parameters on how many samples to output.As the time input goes from 0 to 1, samples with higher phase values will reach 1 sooner.

label: Phaser
text: The Edge parameter determines how quickly the values get from 0 to 1 once it starts moving. A lower value gets there in less time.The third input to Phaser CHOP is equivalent to the Edge parameter. For simplicity, start out by using the parameter "Edge" on the CHOPinstead of wiring into this third input. Small values of edge will make local changes in phase "sharper". If you reduce the edge, the animation will still take the same amount of time because  the duration is solely determined by the phase input. However, with a small edge, you'll notice that samples animate more individually. With a large edge, samples animate as more of a cohesive group.

label: Phaser
text: The Phaser CHOP has a parameter for the output format - one channel with all samples (good for render instancing), and multi-channels with one sample per channel (good for replicants of a Replicator COMP).This is like using a Shuffle CHOP to swap samples for channels.Here the Num Samples parameter controls the number of channels.

label: Phaser
text: Look at how varying the Edge parameterchanges the output of each Phaser CHOP.The Lookup CHOP gives a time-offset bounce to the Phaser samples.Right-click on animation1 and select Edit Animation to alter the curve.change switch1.In the bottom example, constant1 and constant2 represent two "poses". All samples of lookup2 go from the second pose to the first post, time-offset.

label: Phaser
text: If the phase input has multiple channels, it outputs multiple channels.An edge input with one channel can be applied to multiple input phase channels.

label: Phaser
text: The first snippet shows that you can pass multiple edges in. You would need the same number of phase channels going into the second input.The second snippet shows that the edge input's samples matter. Note the rapidness of the movement as the sample index gets lower (smaller edge value).

label: Phaser
text: In this practical example, toggle the state button between "Up" and "Down", waiting for the animation to finish each time.Now change the Edge value to something else, and play the animation again a few times. Notice that although the animation took the exact same amount of time from start to finish, it looked different.Next, change toggle the Random button on and change the state again. Change the seed value, and then toggle the state again. Notice that the order in which the blocks move changed, but the total duration of the animation didn't change.

label: Phaser
text: play with Number of Cycles in pattern3.

label: Phaser
text: Phaser as a GLSL shader. Play with switch1 index.Yes you can do the phasing with a Level TOP's ranging too.And this is a bit like what Time Machine TOP does.

label: Record
text: The Record CHOP takes the channels coming in the first (Position) input, converts and records them internally, and outputs the stored channels as the CHOP output. The optional second (Active) input is used to enable and disable the recording. Toggle the buttonRocker to start/stop recording, and notice the different graphs in each Record CHOP, depending on their `record` parameter value:On - always records when playing forward. When the active input is in the off state the input is maintained at its current values.Add - adds (offsets) to an existing set of channels previously recorded in the Record CHOP. The channels are left unmodified while the Active input is in the off state.Auto range - if the Active input goes on, and TouchDesigner is playing, then any existing storage array is cleared, and the channels are recorded in a new storage array until Active goes off. This behaves like a sampler.

label: Rename
text: Alphabetic characters between [] get expanded one at a time.If between [] is a number range, it's expanded to all numbers in that range.

label: Rename
text: See the Select CHOP too.

label: Render Pick
text: The Render Pick CHOP samples a rendering (from a Render TOP or a Render Pass TOP) and returns 3D information from the geometry at that particular pick location. In this snippet there are 24 Geo Instances, and the Render Pick CHOP is set to sample the Instance Id.In the Base COMP "renderpick to rotation per instance" we use the instance Id to trigger an animation curve used to control rotation per instance.If you LMB click (or LMB hold & drag) in container1, notice the "instance" channel in renderpick1 show what instance is picked, and then generate a rotation animation for that instance.

label: Render Pick
text: The Render Pick CHOP samples a rendering (from a Render TOP or a Render Pass TOP) and returns 3D information from the geometry at that particular pick location. In this snippet the Render Pick CHOP samples the world position, and we use the callbacks DAT to run a Python script on each pick event.In this case when the terrain is picked, we use the sampled world position to set the position of the geo_indicator cone.

label: Render Pick
text: The Render Pick CHOP samples a rendering (from a Render TOP or a Render Pass TOP) and returns 3D information from the geometry at that particular pick location. In this snippet we sample a custom SOP attribute "MyCustomAttribute", which is added to each point of the Box SOP using a Point SOP.Click and hold LMB in container1 COMP - during mouseover on the box you'll see the output from lfo1 CHOP update in the Renderpick CHOP.

label: Reorder
text: These two examples reorder the incoming channels from lowest to highest, and then from highest to lowest.

label: Reorder
text: These are some examples of different reordering methods applied to multiple incoming RGB channels.

label: Reorder
text: This example puts the values of the first input, in the same order as the values of the 2nd input.

label: Replace
text: The Replace CHOP can replace channels very quickly.Channels in Input1 will be replaced by channels found in Input2 if a matching channel exists in Input2. Any channels that are in Input1 and not in Input2 will be output without being replaced.

label: Replace
text: The Replace CHOP can be used to quickly delete channels (it can be faster than a Delete CHOP).To do this send all your channels to the second input, and send the channels you want to keep to the first input.

label: Resample
text: This example doubles  the amount of sample over the same time period.

label: Resample
text: This example triples the length of the channe and samples it at 25 samples per second.

label: Resample
text: This examples keeps the same sample rate, but quadruples the  length of the channel.

label: Script
text: This example takes all the inputs of the script, creates 3 new outputs, and does some simple math operations on the inputs, and assigns new output values.

label: Script
text: This example creates sine waves based on the 'absTime.seconds*50' value entered on the Custom Parameter named Count. The sine waves are all offset by the 2nd Custom Parameter named Offset.

label: Script
text: This example scales all the channels by the value input in the Custom Parameter named 'Scale' on the 'Custom1' page of the Script CHOP.It also deletes channel 'chan3', and creates a new channel named 'zzz'.The graph range is -20 to +20 set via the graph right-click menu via Vertical Range...

label: Script
text: This example creates two new channels, with a variable number of samples that can be controlled using the 'Samples' custom parameter made on the  'Custom1' page of the Script CHOP.

label: Script
text: Script CHOP threshold example where any samples that do not exceed value of a threshold (0.5) will be deleted. Creates long channel, interates through samples of the input, and shortens the result.via barakooda

label: Script
text: This example creates a very simple counter using custom parameters to control incrementing the value as well as resetting it.The value in this example is held within the Operator's storage. The Examine DAT can look into an operator's storage and get att it data.

label: Script
text: This initializes itself up as a 1000-sample CHOP, the same length as pattern1. It then creates a numpy array that is 1000 samples long, with values going from 0 to 1. It then creates a new channel with the 1000-sample ramp as its samples.Next it creates a new numpy array from the first channel of pattern1.Then it creates a new channel and tries to name the channel c1. Because c1 already exists it finds the next available channel name, c2 and names the new channel c2.  It then copie sthe numpy array to that new channel.

label: Script
text: THis copies multiple channels into a 2D numpy array. Then it copies that array into script1's output.You don't need to specify its number of samples nor number of channels.

label: S Curve
text: a variety of simple curves using S Curve CHOP.Drag-drop any S Curve CHOP to the select1 CHOP and operate the slider to see how the slider can be made non-linear.scurve_lookup shows how you can eliminate the lookup CHOP by doing the lookup itself.See also Animation COMP to generate more complex keyframed curves.See the Function CHOP for a variety of non-linear transformations.Or the Trigger CHOP to get attack-decay-sustain-release..

label: S Curve
text: If you have values coming in for a channel that are say between 2 and 5 (math1), and you want to limit them to be between 3 and 4, without hard-clamping them (as you would with a Limit CHOP). you would use an S Curve with a Lookup CHOP to soft-limit them to between 3 and 4.Lookup's Input Range is set to 5. S Curve range is set to 3 to 4.  With this, the values at 3.5 don't change.

label: S Curve
text: S Curve with the Timer CHOP.

label: Select
text: Select CHOPs are often used for simply renaming channels.

label: Select
text: You can select channels using pattern chan* where * matches any text after "chan".Search "Pattern Matching" in the wiki for the numerous ways of matching channel names with patterns.

label: Select
text: This picks out 2 specific channels and changing "chan" to "d" in their names.

label: Select
text: These examples fetch 1 or moreCHOPs using the CHOP path parameter.

label: Select
text: You can pick out multiple specific channels by entering them one after another, separated by a space.Note the order is determined by the order you specify in Channel Names.

label: Select
text: You can use pattern matching to merge channels from multiple CHOPs.select1 gets the first channel of the first output of base*seelct2 gets the second channel using a more general expression. It renames the channels also.

label: Select
text: This example fetches CHOPs constant1 and constant2 as they match the constant* path parameter.From these 2 CHOPs, the channels selected are in the range chan1 to chan8. The last Select CHOP swaps what is before the ':' for what is after it.

label: Select
text: expands a number range inside the []

label: Select
text: expands every alphabetic character inside the []

label: Select
text: This is a more robust way to make sure you get some channels.If your source does not exist, the op() expression return None, in which case it uses op('default").This is a nice behavior of the "or" operator in python.

label: Select
text: And see the Rename CHOP. It is a subset of the Select CHOP.

label: Shift
text: The Shift CHOP time-shifts a CHOP, changing the start and end of the CHOP's interval. However, the contents of the channels remain the same.

label: Shift
text: In this example the channel is shifted by an offset of 250 samples.This means the start/end of the CHOP's interval are still the same (0 to 999) but we sample a different part of the channel. The pattern CHOP's Extend Left and Extend Right parameters are set to cycle, so now that we sample outside the interval, we see a cycle of the sinus wave.(also see snippets for Extend CHOP for an explanation how CHOP extend conditions work)

label: Shift
text: In the Shift CHOP, the Python expression me.chanIndex is the index of the channel being generated. This expression can be used to give each channel a unique offset.

label: Shift
text: The optional second input of tHe Shift chop, the Start/End Reference, is used to align the first input CHOP relative to another reference CHOP. This outputs the channels of the first CHOP only, and the shifts are based on the interval of the second CHOP. It is useful for making several CHOPs match up to the same time.

label: Shuffle
text: Here are a few ways to manipulate channels and samples.See the Fan CHOP too.

label: Shuffle
text: This example takes each sample of a Noise CHOP and creates a channel with that value.

label: Shuffle
text: This example takes  all the scattered RGBA values from the TOP to CHOP and sequences them by name, so that you have a seperate R,G,B, and A, channel, for further processing.The Movie File In TOP is down-resolutioned first.

label: Shuffle
text: This example takes every 10th sample from the original to form a new  channel.

label: Shuffle
text: This example forms a new channel based every 9 samples of the  input value. You can follow the sine waves values, starting on chan0, going left to right, then continuing to chan1, going left to right, all  the way to chan9.

label: Slope
text: These examples calculate the slope of the Speed CHOPs, whose slope we have set using a Constant CHOP.They show that the rate you feed into the Speed CHOP can be extracted back using a Slope CHOP.In math-talk, the derivative of the integral of a function is the function.

label: Slope
text: Here we're calculating the acceleration rate of the incoming values.wiggle the slider around.It's not exact as there are round-off errors.

label: Slope
text: Here we're calculating the acceleration rate of the incoming values.

label: Slope
text: The Slope CHOP can be used to detect if a CHOP's value has changed. It is 0 when nothing has changed (from the  previous sample). Move the slider value around.The Logic CHOP is set to output 1 when its input is non-zero, so all the slider's changes are detected.In this case the LFO is  sampled whenever the slider moves.You can also do this with scripting using the CHOP Execute DAT.To Hold CHOP sampling is less jerky when the Filter CHOP is un-bypassed.

label: SOP to
text: In this example we're using the SOP to CHOP to access the point position of box1, then we're multiplying each point by noise, and using a CHOP to SOP to apply the new point positions.

label: SOP to
