text: In this example we're  able to access the position of every particle in the  particle system.

label: SOP to
text: Here we've changed the direction of the normals of the circle that is being used as the particle source. This allows us to emit particles in different directions.

label: Sort
text: sort1 sorts the samples by lowest-to-highest value. Each channel is treated separately. So each channel has ascending values regardless of the other channels.sort2 uses the first channel (using Channel Indices parameter) to sort the samples of the three channels together: for example, chan1[31], chan2[31] and chan3[31] travel together.sort3 does the same with chan2, but sorting in decending order.sort4 does ascending, but chooses the channel by name "chan3".sort5 randomizes all the samples, but keeps the channels together. Then sort6 re-sorts by the first channel's ascending value, making it the same as the output of sort2.

label: Speed
text: An example of how to count seconds using a Speed CHOP.The Pulse will restart the counter onthe same frame as the pulse happens, whereas with the Restart, it's 0 for at least one frame.

label: Speed
text: Speed CHOP is useful for driving Movie In TOP indices.

label: Speed
text: Value zig zags between minimum and maximum values.

label: Speed
text: Values loop from maximum back to minumum.

label: Speed
text: If pattern1 is the speed going into the Speed CHOP, then the output of speed1 is the time-history of the distance travelled.

label: Speed
text: If you input an  acceleration value, your output will be  position, if you set the order type to  Second.

label: Speed
text: Every button press bumps up the current value by a certain amount (1.2 as seen in the Panel Execute DAT), and it starts counting down from that value to 0 (the lower limit specified in the Minimum parameter of the speedBump).  It prevents the upper value from exceeding a certain number (the Maximum parameter in speedBump).The decay rate is specified in the constantSpeed CHOP (-0.5 means it goes down by .5 every second).The Speed CHOP is reset with resetpulse.pulse() every time the button is pressed.

label: Speed
text: Pressing forces a rise at a given rate (Constant CHOP), and releasing forces a drop at another rate.

label: Splice
text: This Splice CHOP inserts one CHOP into the other, preserving the original CHOP's samples.

label: Splice
text: This Splice CHOP does a simple replace of one CHOP into the other.

label: Splice
text: This extracts a part of a CHOP, alters it, then replaces the original samples.The two Splice CHOPs have their "start" and "trimlength" parameters tied together with expressions.

label: Splice
text: If your Splice CHOP is used to instance geometry, adn each sample represents an instance's x y z and size, you would use this trick to grow the size whenever a different instance was chosen. Change the value of the Constant CHOP and look at spliceInstance.Here we use a Constant CHOP to represent the "current" sample (0 to 1 is mapped to 0 to 599), but you could use a Render Pick DAT to take a picked instance number and feed that down the chain.THe Slope CHOP detects any change in its input, and the Math CHOP makes sure it's always positive. That's good to feed to teh Trigger CHOP. You can also use a CHOP Execute DAT to detect a change and trigger the Trigger CHOP's Reset Pulse parameter.

label: Spring
text: The Spring CHOP creates vibrations influenced by the input channels, as if a mass was attached to a spring.Input channels can either represent position or force. In this example the input represents position (the Spring CHOP's "input effect" parameter is set to "position").Now the Sping CHOP acts as if, for every channel, there is a mass at the end of a spring, affected by a distance from the actual position (the output of the channel at the previous frame) to the desired position (the input channel at the current frame). When the distance (output - input) is zero, there is no force and therefore no movement.

label: Spring
text: The Spring CHOP creates vibrations influenced by the input channels, as if a mass was attached to a spring.Input channels can either represent position or force. In this example the input represents force (the Spring CHOP's "input effect" parameter is set to "force").Now the input is used as a force on the spring/mass, and the CHOP reacts to this force plus the force of the spring/mass. In this case, the mass would always stabilize at value 0 if the input is a force of 0.

label: Spring
text: There are two ways to do multiple springs in one CHOP.

label: Stretch
text: reverses the order of samples and keeps the same length of CHOP (10 seconds).

label: Stretch
text: half th number of samples (300), half the  length (5 seconds).

label: Stretch
text: ten times longer, but also makes the CHOP start at 1 second (frame 31).See the popup help to see the range.The second example has each sample repeated 10 times as you see when you zoom in on the graph.

label: Switch
text: Use the radio to switch between the different inputs.

label: Switch
text: This example is the same as the above example except that the index selection is passed as the first input.

label: Timecode
text: A Timecode CHOP holds a timecode that other operators and python expressions have access to.Timecodes can be set in differnet ways. timecode_sequential is like the Timer CHOP. Press Start.timecode_locked_to_timeline co-relates to the timeline frame number.timecode_specify_index gets it from the Index par.timecode_by_parameters specifies with separate parameters.timecode_timecode_object1 shows that the python tdu.Timecode() creates a timecode object, and you can set its time via the arguments. See wiki for tdu.Timecode, or the "timecode python" snippet.timecode_timecode_object2 shows that many operators have a built-in timecode object, so you can fetch by referring to the operator's timecode object.timecode_timecode_op_reference gets the timecode by just giving the operator's name, assuming that operator type has a built-in .timecode object. See the wiki page for Timecode for a list of the operators containing timecode objects.timecode_chop1 and timecode_chop2 lets you use specially-named input channels to set the timecode.

label: Timecode
text: In this example we have a Timecode CHOP stepping forward sequentially with a custom length of 10 seconds. Current timecode value and countdown timecode value can be fetched through the Info DAT.If the Timecode CHOP's Length parameter is set, the countdown will count down from that value.

label: Timecode
topic: frames per seond (fps) and drop frames
text: The Rate parameter sets the frames per second since any time code assumes some number of frames per second.The default CHOP uses the timeline's fps, but timecode2 overrides it to 25 so it doesn't need to match any timeline.example3 demonstrates how drop-frames are added to the timecode output. We have a drop-frame rate specified (ie. non-integer, 59.94, 29.97), which is a standard drop-frame rate requiring an additional 2 frames added every minute not divisible by 10. Drop Frame mode is set to Automatic so the 18 extra frames are added automatically for an output timecode of 10:00.18 from the input of 10:00:00.

label: Timecode
text: This example demonstrates how to set the timecode with frame/second/minute/hour parameters. The output timecode will be the weighted sum of all the parameters.timecode2 shows that individual components can be higher than their respective maximums (eg. second parameter can be >60).

label: Timecode
text: This example demonstrates the Timecode CHOP's input CHOP. The input CHOP can have any subset of the following channels: hour, minute, second, frame, negative. The Timecode CHOP's output is calculated relative to the input CHOP, ie. the input CHOP's timecode is added to the output. In other words, our sequential timecode in timecode1 starts at 00:10:00:00 (ie. 10  minutes) instead of the default 00:00:00:00.

label: Timecode
text: This example demonstrates differences between SMPTE mode on/off. timecode1 has SMPTE mode disabled so the negative timecode is allowed. timecode2 has SMPTE mode enabled, so it does not allow for a negative timecode, and instead the timecode loops around to the maximum value of 23:59:59:59. With SMPTE off, times can go over 24 hours.It's also worth noting the countdown values. Countdown is 00:00:00:01 in both cases because countdown is compared against the looping value: 100:00:00:00 and 24:00:00:00 aka 00:00:00:00. timecode1 goes below zero so it's compared against the looped value 00:00:00:00.timecode3 shows you can have non-standard frame rates, like 240 fps and up.

label: Timecode
text: This example demonstrates how the Timer CHOP can drive the Timecoce CHOP or the Movie File In TOP using the Timer CHOP's built-in timecode object.Notice that ANY OP that has a .timecode member can give you a set of timecode channels in an Info CHOP, timecode in an Evaluate DAT, or

label: Timecode
text: This example shows how the Timecode CHOP can drive the Timer CHOP.

label: Timecode
text: Here are python expressions to get values, shown in timecodePython's parameters.The DAT shows how python can set the state of any timecode object.

label: Timecode
text: timecode objects can be added or subtracted to get sums or differences.This is good for syncing up devices that are offset in time but keep in sync.It manages times that have different frames per second and drop frame settings.

label: Timecode
text: The Timecode CHOP can behave as if it has a fixed length.When you set a Custom Length parameter, it can behave such that it does not go past a specified length, or it cycles when it reaches that length.  Start timecode1, see it cycle, then try it with Cycle off.Here is is run internally (Sequential) i the top example, and at the bottom, run externaly with a CHOP channel expressed in seconds.

label: Timecode
text: This Snippet demonstrates two methods of converting the stream of MTC quarter frame messages to a readable timecode format. To start streaming, a MIDI device that streams quarter frame messages must be setup in the MIDI Device Mapper Dialog.

label: Timecode
text: A few ways of doing things.timer1 drives moviefilein1 via timecode in timer1.time2 drives audiofilein1 via a master_seconds channel, but audiofilein1 gives its timecode info to an Info CHOP, an expression in eval2, and timecode2, which provides info2a with its timecode data, including countdown.Linear timecode, which travels via an audio out / in path, is driven by the Timer CHOP, the eval3 gets it from the LTC In CHOP directly.In ltcout2 we are trying to send the length in the user data, which has limitations.

label: Timer
text: The Timer CHOP was created because this was too hard:Ring a bell after 5 seconds.... too many OPs, too many parameters, click Reset on the Speed CHOP... too much thinking to get right.

label: Timer
text: This is the basic timer with Length that you can set to any number of seconds.Click Initialize to get ready, then Start to  begin counting.Or just press Start at any time to initialize and begin counting right away.You can set Length to seconds, frames or samples using the menu on the right of Length.

label: Timer
text: This is the same timer but you can get out more channels via the Outputs page:Seconds (in the Timer Count menu) Ready (means the timer has been initialized) Running (It's been started) Done Pulse (another form of Done)On the Timer page, you can click Go to Done at any time.Start and watch trail2 a few times to see how the channels relate to each other.constant_timerFrame shows you can also get some values from the Timer CHOP python members.

label: Timer
text: Delay will cause some seconds of delay after the Start and before the timer starts counting up.Timer can also cycle: Click Start and watch a few times. It will cycle 4 times.You can interrupt and make it cycle fewer times:  Click parameter Exit at End of Cycle, or Go to Done to exit cycling immediately.Cycle controls: Cycle on/off, Cycle Limit (infinite when off), Maximum Cycles (when Cycle Limit is on).The cycles_pulse channel triggers the depth-finder sound.The done_pulse channel triggers the "done" text in Text TOP.( Expert NOTE: To make sure the cycle_pulse and done_pulse channels are always exported correctly, the  Export Method on the Common page is set to DAT Table by Name. This is true of the Timer CHOP and other CHOPs with selectable channels and specific names, like Beat and Perform. )The parameters Speed and Play vary the timer speed.

label: Timer
text: Segments can be like back-to-back timers in one Timer CHOP. We call back-to-back timers "Serial Timers".Make a table with columns named like the parameter internal names: length, delay, cycle, ..., and put in some values for length or delay, etc.The units of length, delay etc is determnied by the Segment Units menu, the default is seconds.A segment is one timer per row, the first segment is numbered 0, starting at row 1 of the table.They replace the parameters temporarily.Click Start and watch a few times.If you go to the Segments page, you can press Previous Segment or Next Segment etc to jump ahead or back in time.

label: Timer
text: Alternately you can specify the begin time of each timer, as you would with a bunch of video clips on a timeline.Replace the Timer CHOP's Segments DAT parameter with table_begin4.Note that you have to be careful if overlapping the segments. If they do overlap, the later segments take precedence.The segments do not have to be in order - they are pre-sorted, as in table_begin_anyorder4.

label: Timer
text: With Serial Timers, you can generate a curve that interpolates from the current value to the new values over the segment time Length.The column "v1" is not mapped to a parameter. It is output from the Timer CHOP as a channel. To do this, on the Segments page, in the parameter Columns to Custom Channels, you put the names of the columns to output as channels, in this case "v1". By default v1 will step to its new value at the start of a segment.To make it interpolate, select from the  Custom Channel Interpolation menu, for example, Linear to Value.With a 0-length first segment, its v1 determines the "initial value" of v1.

label: Timer
text: Advanvced technique:If each channel needs to interpolate differently, say one is linear, one is ease in/out, you  need to interpolate outside the Timer CHOP. A method is shown here.You put the names of the columns to output as channels, in this case "v1" and also "length".v1 and length are passed to a Filter CHOP to do the interpolation, length is used as the  Filter Width.Click Initialize and Start and watch the length and V1 channels step according to the rows in the table.The Filter Width parameter is the "length" that it retrieves from the table.When you change the Filter CHOP's Type menu to Gaussian, the filters act as an ease-in ease-out.The filters are reset when you Initialize or Start: The "ready" channel is exported to their Reset Pulse parameters.

label: Timer
text: The same tables can be used to run the timers in parallel. On the Segments page, set the Segment Method to Parallel Timers, then press Initialize, then Start.Note there is one channel per segment for each channel type selected on the Output page.Try this Timer CHOP with tableBeginAnyorder.

label: Timer
text: You can feed text into an Info DAT for each segment of the timer. You can create a column of any name in the segments table, "message" in this case, and add text in each row. Then you put that column name in the parameter Columns to Info DAT on the Segments page.That will cause a row in the Info DAT, "message" to contain the text of the segment as it plays.Click Start and watch the Info DAT.

label: Timer
text: Callbacks are located in the docked node, timer8_callbacks.Callbacks are functions that are called when timer events occur. They can be used to set parameters and trigger events, run scripts and many other things.In this example it prints the current time, "running seconds" when it initializes, when it is ready, starts, on segment and, cycles and on done.Open up the textport (Dialogs -> Textport), press Start and watch the output of the callbacks.Look at the DAT that's docked to the Timer CHOP.whileTimerActive() is especially noteworthy as it is called every frame that a segment's timer is counting, and not at any other time.

label: Timer
text: When you Initialize, you can initiate actions and check conditions to assure the initialization is ready, and if not ready, have onInitialize() called again some number of frames later.On timer1, open the textport first and then press Initialize. THe timer CHOP will call onInitialize() 5 times separate by 20 frames of time before reaching the ready state.To test timer2, first make sure the Busy button is on. When you press Initialize or Start, the onInitialiize() callback checks if the Busy button is on, and if on, onInititalize() returns 50, which will cause onInitialize() to be called again in 50 frames, with the callCount set to 2. When onInitialize() returns 0, it proceeds to the ready state, and then starts the timer if the user had pressed Start.If Busy is on and then you press Start, it will wait until Busy is off and then start counting right away.This is useful for preparing movies, loading files, waiting for servers to respond or other things that will keep the timer from being ready.

label: Timer
text: Before a segment ends, you can have the Timer CHOP call onCycleEndAlert().In timer10, the Cycle End Alert time is set to 2 seconds, so onCycleEndAlert() gets called 2 seconds before timer10 finishes.onCycleEndAlert() plays an audio cue, and  initiates timer10_next to start.However the onInitialize() in timer10_next return 10 until timer10's "running" channel goes to 0 and the depthfinder audio sample finishes playing. So every 10 frames, onInitialize() is called and stays in its "initializing" state until the audio finishes and timer10 is done.

label: Timer
text: This is set up to capture a clean start-to-end set of channels in the Trail CHOP.When the timer initializes, the onInitialize() callback resets and starts the Trail CHOP, then when onDone() is called, it turns the Active parameter Off on the Trail CHOP after another 10 frames.On some segments, the cycleendalert column of table14 causes the onCycleEndAlert() callback to be called some number of seconds before the  segment is over, supposedly to prepare for  the next segment.initialize14 and start14 show you can drive the initialization and starting with the two inputs.

label: Timer
text: The goto_scrubber slider will cause the Timer CHOP to jump to a specific time or frame in the sequence.  The slider 0-1 range is re-ranged in the Math CHOP to be 0 to 25.goTo(frame = 601): goes to frame 601, where frame 1 is the first frame. goTo(segment = 3, cycle = 2, seconds = 1):  Jumps to segment 3, cycle 2, and will set time to 1 second.goTo(seconds = 35):  If cycle is off, and segment 1 = 10, segment 2 = 15,  segment 3 = 20, this will jump to segment 3, with time set  to 10 seconds.goTo(seconds = 35):  If cycle is on for segment 2 (max cycles = 10), and  segment 1 = 10, segment 2 = 15, this will jump to segment 2,  cycle = 1 (as in: one cycle has completed),  and with time set to 10 seconds.You can set the global timecode directly with the script  in set_timecode14. You can also get the global timecode with the expression in text_timecode14.

label: Timer
text: poemPlayer takes a list of phrases (one phrases per sequential parameter block) that can overlap in time - at most two phrases at a time. You can interactively move/trim/extend any phrase.Click/scrub and release the timeline slider to see it operating. Or click above the slider to pause/play.Each segment has a rise time and fall time that fades-on and -off the lines of text. Inside poemPlayer, this is accomplished with the Trigger CHOP exported to the Text TOP.It splits the phrases into two groups, the even segments and the odd segments appearing as two rows of red text strings. Each group goes to one Timer CHOP.Clicking on the slider or above it will scrub or play the timers by setting .masterSEconds or .masterFraction on each Timer CHOP. That's in the Panel Execute DAT.It renders one Text TOP for each Timer CHOP and adds the images together.In the interface you can change the red bars' start time, end time, and you can shift the bar: click/drag the left, middle and right part of any bar. That changes the specLyric.poemPlayer is simpler - it does the same thing but hides the table inside and you set everything with parameters on its Spec page.

label: Timer
text: Put multichoiceGame in a floating window and go into that network. Press Begin Match.Timer CHOPs can trigger each other and implement state machine behavior.Most Timer CHOPs here initiate some action in another Timer CHOP using:(1) its "ready_pulse" channel is exported to the Initialize parameter on another Timer CHOP.(2a) its "done" channel is exported to a Start parameter on another Timer CHOP, or(2b) some of its callbacks running a python command to .pulse() another Timer CHOP. You would see this in onDone()  sometimes.Normally you pulse the Initialize parameter of a bunch of Timer CHOPs, then Start one Timer CHOP, The behavior continues forever or until one Timer stops awaiting further action from the user.The node color is set with its .color member.

label: Timer
text: Timer CHOPs can implment an animation technique called Start-Holdloop-End - SHE.There are three animations that fit together seamlessly, and where the second is a loop that can run perpetually.An "Exit at End of Cycle" signal is received while the second loop is running so that the second animation finishes its loop and then continues on to run the third loop.The Switch TOP is set to 0 1 or 2 by the three Timer CHOPs by their onStart() and onInitialize() callbacks.Complete has no effect until the middle timer is running.

label: Timer
text: Each timer can drive an Animation component that drives a 3D object.In this case, the times are expressed in frames in the Timer CHOP, and frame (relative to 0) is sent to the Animation Component.The latter is set to be driven by frame number,  starting from 1. The duration in the Timer table needs to match the duration of the keyframes in the Animation  component.Not complete: eval15 automatically fetches the lengths from the Animation components.ALTERNATE:If you want to control the speed that the animations play back at via the segments DAT, you can put the desired lengths in seconds or frames in the segments table, then use the 0-1 timer_fraction channels in the Animation Components by setting their Input Index Units parameter to Fraction, and alter the Select CHOP in the Animation component to use  timer_fraction*.

label: Timer
text: The buttons cause the Timer CHOP to jump to different segments after the current segment completes, thanks to the endofcycle=True in goTo().Even after it reaches Done, you can jump to any segment and keep counting.The running_seconds increases when you jump to any segment. The cumulative_seconds jumps to whatever would be if you had initialized and then reached that segment naturally.If you want to reset to 0, you need to press Initialize and then you can jump anywhere.

label: Timer
text: This runs a schedule of segments that are back-to-back, do not overlap, and each segment has a variable length.You can click the right of a segment and lengthen or shorten the segment.Launch backtoback in a floating viewer, go into backtoback, play the backtoback timeline, watch textport and nodes. Then scrub its timeline, see how onSegmentEnter() gets called any tin you scrub into a segment.

label: Timer
text: This takes a list of movies and generates a timed playlist where each movie crossfades to the next over N seconds, default 1 second (in crosstime DAT).Set the Folder DAT Root Folder to a location where movies exist on your computer.Click/scrub and release the slider to see it operating. Or click above the slider to play it from the start.Each movie crossfades via a Trigger CHOP curve that fades out/in the movies before adding them together.It splits the movies into two groups,  the even movies and the odd movies appearing, as two rows of red text strings. Each group goes to one Timer CHOP.Clicking on the slider or above it will scrub or play the timers by calling the goTo() method on each Timer CHOP. That's in the Panel Execute DAT.It renders one Text TOP for each Timer CHOP and adds the images together.

label: Timer
text: This takes a set of animation clips that can be sequenced end-to-end, with the second clip optionally looping.It can be one animation that is cut into three or more parts.This example doesn't do blending.The "chop" column sets which CHOP to select from, and its Lookup CHOP gets its index from the "timer_fraction" channel.Click "End Segment at End of Cycle" while it's looping to cleanly go to the next segment.

label: Timer
text: When clicking Start, this waits a frame (Delay parameter = 1 frame), runs a command, then optionally checks on subsequent frames for a desired condition.This is an alternative to running the run(cmd, 'delayFrames=1) and writing a python script to loop one or more frames to wait until a condition is satisfied.It is set to time out after 10 frames with the Cycle parameters.

label: Timer
text: To run a timer without end, change the Length Type menu from its default value Fixed to Infinite.Once you start, timer_seconds, master_seconds and running_seconds will endlessly rise, as if the length was infinite.timer_fraction will remain at 0.The channels ready and running will behave like they do when Length Type is Fixed.You can pulse Go to Done, and the *_seconds channels will freeze at their current value.Segments do not apply in Infinite mode, and the Segments page is disabled.The Delay parameter works in Infinite mode. If Speed is below 1, or Play is off, it will slow down timer_seconds and cumulative_seconds,  according to their normal rules.

label: Timer
text: Show the Textport. Press Start on the timer. It prints the segments it is starting and the segment's length.This will run the sequence, and stop once it's done.Then turn on Sub Range.onSubrangeStart() is called every time it loops.scrubbable with slider1

label: Timer
text: Adjust the Seconds parameter on the pars COMP. info1 displays the seconds value as timecode, both count-up and count-down.With any CHOP containing a channel that is expressing time in seconds or frames or fraction of a Length, you can pass the channel to the Timer CHOP whose Time Control parameter is set to External CHOP Channel. Then you get the timecodes in an Info DAT table. To use the Frames parameter instead, change timer1's External Units parameter to Frames, and the External Channel parameter to 'Frames'. This will pick up the 'Frames' channel of par1. Now you can adjust the Frames parameter of the pars component. Same idea with Fraction. it’s a 2-node timecode generator. But text2 shows that you don't need the Info DAT for count-up.Note: The Timer CHOP's Length parameter is set to 1000 seconds. To change it you have to change Time Control to Sequential, change the Length, Initialize, the go back to External Time Control. Or if count-down is not needed, change Length Type to Infinite.

label: Time Slice
text: Move the slider and see the Trail CHOP.Now un-bypass the Hog CHOP above. Now TouchDesigener is skipping most frames.When you move the slider, the slider values are stepping too, as you see with channel v1 in the Trail CHOP.But the Time Slice CHOP figures out the in-between values and passes all those samples to its output.The third channel shows which frames were cooked (1) and which frames were mossed (0).See also:the wiki page "Time Slicing".the training video "Time Slicing Vid"

label: Time Slice
text: With the Hog CHOP un-bypassed, TouchDesigener is skipping most frames.This shows the three ways the Time Slice  CHOP deals with its input, which is a 2-second smooth-noise and Extend Conditions set to mirror outside its 2-second range. Each time the Time Slice CHOP cooks, it samples one value of the input CHOP  and holds that value for the entire CHOP.In teh second case, it linearly interpolates from one frame to the next.In the third case, it extracts multi-samples from the input.

label: Time Slice
text: The first 2 CHOPs simulates a case where you get only one new sample every time a CHOP cooks, or an input device is read. An example is getting data from a Kinect or a Leap Motion device, you are taking camera input into CHOPs via a TOP to CHOP.Without a Time Slice CHOP set to Linear the values step from one time slice to the next. The Time SLice CHOP can smooth that.The Filter CHOP smooths it a bit further,  though making it a bit mose sluggish.

label: TOP to
text: You can get the  RGBA values of a single pixel.Adjust U Start and V Start parameters.

label: TOP to
text: Here we have the RGBA values of the middle column of the image.

label: TOP to
text: Here we have the RGBA values of a full row of the image.In the case where you want the values to be between 0 and 255, the second CHOP sets the RGBA Units parameter.

label: TOP to
text: This is a basic example of how you could make your own color picker, using the u and v of a Panel CHOP as co-ordinates to analyze.

label: TOP to
text: This takes all valid points of a point cloud in a TOP and converts to CHOP channels.On the TOP to CHOP, you have to turn on Output as Single Channel Set, then set Crop to be Full Image. In topto2 you see the lingering unused pixels at the end with alpha 0, representing the orange pixels at the top of pointfilein1, which are unused pixels of the point cloud. To eliminate them, topto2 has Active Channel set to Alpha, which strips those off. Also in the point cloud TOPs, the unused pixels may have NaN (not a number, a very high floating point number) in the RGB values. If you want to strip these out too, turn on the Exclude NaNs parameter.

label: TOP to
text: If you want to sample the image with a specific number of rows and columns, you should use the Resolution TOP to scale down and get the exact number of rows and columns, and then TOP to CHOP the entire image.  For more flexibility if re-shaping the image, you can preceed it with a Transform TOP.You then need to choose whether you want to average pixels when scaling down, or just sample the nearest pixel, which will give you more contrast. To see exactly what your TOP to CHOP gives you, make the Viewer Smoothness be Nearest Pixel. That doesn't change the output, it only makes what you see in the viewer image more precise.

label: Touch In
text: The Touch Out CHOP can be used to create high speed connection between two TouchDesigner processes. Data is sent over TCP/IP or UDP. The Touch Out CHOP (server) sends its data to a Touch In CHOP (client).Unbypass touchout1 and touchin1 and touchin2.NOTE for Windows OS - If experiencing connection issues make sure Windows Firewall is disabled.(same snippet for Touch In CHOP)

label: Touch Out
text: The Touch Out CHOP can be used to create high speed connection between two TouchDesigner processes. Data is sent over TCP/IP or UDP. The Touch Out CHOP (server) sends its data to a Touch In CHOP (client).Unbypass touchout1 and touchin1 and touchin2.NOTE for Windows OS - If experiencing connection issues make sure Windows Firewall is disabled.(same snippet for Touch In CHOP)

label: Trail
text: These examples show the different lengths of trails for the same input

label: Trail
text: The Trail CHOP trail1 is forced to display in  the range 0 to 1: turn on its Viewer Active, right-click -> Vertical Adapt -> 0 to 1 or one of the other ranges, or make your own range.

label: Trail
text: The Trail CHOP displays a dot at each sample. You need to stretch  the CHOP tile or reduce the range  of the Trail before the dots appear sine htey are normally too close to  eadh other.turn on its Viewer Active, then right-click -> Dots

label: Trail
text: The Trail CHOP is set to display multiple graphs: turn on its Viewer Active, right-click -> Graph Per Single Channel and set Number of Graphs.When channels have very different ranges, it is better to display them in separate graphs.

label: Trail
text: Sometimes you may want to capture something in a Trail CHOP and freeze it to examine or use later.This activates the Trail CHOP only while a slider is active. The "select" channel goes on while you are clicking on a slider.(try the rollover panel value in  the Panel CHOP as an alternative.)You can also clear out the Trail channels by exporting the Panel CHOP to the Reset Pulse button on the Trail CHOP.

label: Transform
text: The Transform CHOP has translate/rotate/scale parameters like all 3D objects, but it outputs a set of channels that can be passed to any of the 3D objects. (this form to-be-implemented shortly)By default it outputs the transform as tx ty tx and scale, plus "euler angles" rx ry rz which is a way of representation rotations.transform2 has the output set to 16 channels that represent the 4x4 matrix describing the same transform, which can be fed to geo2.On the Geometry COMP's Pre-Xform page you need to turn on Apply Pre-Transform, then put the path of the CHOP containing the transform into the parameter Xform /CHOP/DAT.geo3 shows it getting from a DAT whose cells are the same as transform2's channels arranged in columns.transform3 outputs the transform as an XYZ position plus a 4-channel quaternion, along with scale and the xord transform order channel.

label: Transform
text: In transform2, the translate and rotate channels that are output depend on what is called "transform order" and "rotate order". That is, the tx ty tz rz ry rz sx sy sz numbers depend on if you apply them in the order translates, then rotates, then scales, if you do the rotates in the order rotate around the X axis, then Y axis then the Z axis.The order that is assumed is determined by the xord and rord channels. 0 0 is the default, and the integers in these channels is the index of the selected menu item in the Transform Order parameter and the Rotate Order parameter.transform4 has different Transform Order and Rotate Order than transform2 on their Output pages. When you change those menus to anything else, they apply the same transform on the objects, as you see in geo1 and geo2.This is evident when you convert the output to a 4x4 matrix: The 4x4 matrix (transform3_4x4 and transform5_4x4) remains the same when you change transform2 and transform4's transform orders.

label: Transform
text: The "inverse" of a transform does the opposite of a transform. If a transform takes an object from the origin to a new postion/rotation/scale, then the inverse of that transform will take an object at that new position/rotation/scale back to the origin with no rotation/scale.So if you multiply a transform by its inverse, you will get back the default "identity" matrix.

label: Transform
text: THis takes a transform and applies a further transform to it.transform1 scales by .7, then rotates in Z by 45 degrees, then moves in X by 3.transform2 and transform4 both move in Y by 3, but they differ in their Multiply Order parameter. transform2, whose Multiply Order is set to "Input, then Transform Page" does the input's transform first, then applies the transform of the Transform Page.transform4, set to "Transform Page, then Input" does the transform of the Transform Page, then the input transform from tranform1.How you get the 3D background grids is by making Viewer Active, right-clicking on the viewer, selecting Display Options, and choosing the middle grid on the bottom, giving an XY grid.Side note for your education: https://www.enviral-design.com/matrices-are-hard/

label: Transform
text: Two 4x4 transforms in rename1 and rename2 are merged as 32 channels named left* and right*, and then transformed again as two 180 rotations, and displaying in geo3 and geo4.In transform3, each set of legal channel suffixes (m00, m01...) that have a unique prefix (left, right) are treated as separate transforms that get affected separately.select1 and select2 pull them apart for display. Then join3 combines them another way: as a 2-sample CHOP with 16 channels. Again these can be transformed as 2 sets of transforms.In this case the resulting 2-sample CHOP is used to instance two toruses on geo5.

label: Transform
text: 3 transforms are in set1 to set3.The Join CHOP puts them together as 3 samples of 16 transform channels.transform1 does a 180 degree rotation on all 3 of them, and outputs them in translate/rotate/scale format.geo1 instances them.select_sets merges them using set* pattern matching of node names (could have used set[1-3]).transform2 also does the 180 rotation on all sets.select_set1 to select_set3 pulls them apart using pattern matching: see the expression in the Channel Names parameter.g1 to g3 are copies of each other - see their expression in the parameter Xform Matrix/CHOP/DAT.

label: Transform
text: work-in-progress pending transform interpolation feature.

label: Transform
text: This converts from Polar (radius/lat/long) to Cartesian (rectangular orthogonal xyz) coordinates. This example represents the approx latitude-longitude of Toronto, Canada.You represent your polar coordinate in any way you want. Here, starting from the origin, you start with a point on the x axis at x=2 assuming your radius is a sphere of size 2.Then you rotate in z (latitude) to bring it up to 45 degrees (using the right-hand rule with thumb pointing along +z axis). Then you rotate in y (longitude) -73 degrees.You pass that to the Transform TOPs and you have to set their implied transform order to Translate Rotate Scale (as you did above), and your rotate order to Rz Ry Rx.On the Output page you indicate you want to output the Position.The bottom gives the flight path from YYZ to Narita. I gets the xyz of each, interpolates 20 points which give a straight line through the earth, pushes those points to radius 1, then raises the points while in flight.

label: Transform XYZ
text: The Transform XYZ CHOP is used to transform positions and vectors. The difference between transforming a position vs. a vector is that a vector won't have the translation portion of the transformation applied to it.The Transform XYZ CHOP first groups the channels from the first input by looking for 'x', 'y' and 'z' as the last character in the channel names. Then it treats each of the sets created as either a Position or a Vector depending on the parameter choice. Here we rotate a vector in CHOP channels - visualized by pink vectors in the Geo COMPs (using a Line MAT which has vector output enabled).

label: Transform XYZ
text: The Transform XYZ CHOP is used to transform positions and vectors. The difference between transforming a position vs. a vector is that a vector won't have the translation portion of the transformation applied to it.The Transform XYZ CHOP first groups the channels from the first input by looking for 'x', 'y' and 'z' as the last character in the channel names. Then it treats each of the sets created as either a Position or a Vector depending on the parameter choice. CHOPs with multiple-samples can be provided - in this snippet we rotate the 1522 Normal vectors of a sphere (the normals are indicated by the blue lines in the SOPs).

label: Transform XYZ
text: Here the Transform XYZ CHOP is used to transform positions.The Transform XYZ CHOP first groups the channels from the first input by looking for 'x', 'y' and 'z' as the last character in the channel names. Using the Splice CHOP, it's easy to transform only a selection of position samples of a CHOP.

label: Trigger
text: basic triggering setup to generate an attack, delay, sustain, release envelope.Click button1 quickly to see the minimum envelope.Click and hold botton1 for a second or more to to see the effect of the sustain.Make the Lengths longer and shorter, and try different Attack Shape, Decay Shape and Release Shapes.Increasing the Delay Length will cause the full envelope to start later.

label: Trigger
text: You can get a complete waveform by turning off the Time Slice parameter on the Common page. One form is a controllable linear rise time, hold time, fall time.There are other CHOPs that can behave in dual fashion - as a full envelope or as a single-sample current value: Noise CHOP, For more elaborate shapes you can use the Keyframe CHOP inside an Animation Component.

label: Trigger
text: You can use Trigger to extend the length of time a channel stays on.You usually don't need logic1 because CHOPs are "on" while the value is above zero.You can follow it with a CHOP Execute DAT taht triggers when onToOff.

label: Trigger
text: You can delay a starting pulse, and then make sure the trigger stays on an exact number of seconds, no matter how long the input stays on.You adjust the Delay Length parameter and the Peak Length parameter.

label: Trigger
text: For triggering with jittery inputs.This will trigger only if the noise1 CHOP has been on for .3 seconds or longer."On" in CHOPs means a value that is greater than 0.Two ways are illustrated: noise1 + count1 + trigger1 (preferred). And noise1 + logic1 + speed1 + trigger2.noise1 is varying around 0. The Count CHOP is set to 0 when its input is 0 or less. When it's above 1, it is counting time (While On parameter).The Trigger CHOP's Trigger Threshold parameter is is set to .3. The trigger happens when the Count CHOP reaches .3 (seconds).Logic/Speed operate similarly. When logic1 == 1. speed1 counts seconds.

label: Trigger
text: For triggering with jittery inputs:This will trigger if the input has been on more often than it's been off.Speed is counting seconds, and Trigger's Threshold is is set to .3.

label: Trigger
text: other forms of triggering without the Trigger CHOP

label: Trim
text: Trim a 10-second CHOP down to 3 seconds (600 frames down to 120, and 600 samples down to 120). The graphs have been set to  display sample number on X.The result starts at sample index 120 (frame 121).The Shift CHOP shifts it to sample index 0.

label: Trim
text: Take a time-sliced CHOP which  would be 1 or more frames, and trim it to be one frame long, the most recent frame.To see this, make the Hog CHOP Active.By trimming like this, the CHOP becomes non-time-sliced, which you can see by middle-clicking on the operators.

label: Warp
topic: speed up and slow down animation
text: The Warp CHOP time-warps the channels of the first input (the Pre-Warp Channels) using one warping channel in the second input (the Warp Curve).Feeding the Warp CHOP a Warp Curve with a constant value of 1 makes the output identical to the Pre-Warp Channels, assuming the two inputs have the same start-end interval. That is, where the rate is 1, the Pre-Warp Channels are not warped.Where the Warp Curve is above 1, it causes a speed-up in the animation. Where the Warp Curve is below 1, it causes a slow-down in the animation.  (the range of pattern2 here is .5 to 3.)

label: Warp
text: The Warp CHOP time-warps the channels of the first input (the Pre-Warp Channels) using one warping channel in the second input (the Warp Curve).In this snippet the Warp CHOP's method is set to "Index Control".Now the Warp Curve acts as an index into the first input. If the Warp Curve is a straight ramp with a slope of 1 (in Units), it produces unwarped output channels.Note that it treats pattern2 as seconds (0 to 16.66 seconds) and mattern1, converted to seconds is 16.66 seconds.

label: Warp
text: The Warp CHOP time-warps the channels of the first input (the Pre-Warp Channels) using one warping channel in the second input (the Warp Curve).Feeding the Warp CHOP a Warp Curve with a constant value of 1 makes the output identical to the Pre-Warp Channels, assuming the two inputs have the same start-end interval. That is, where the rate is 1, the Pre-Warp Channels are not warped.Where the Warp Curve is above 1, it causes a speed-up in the animation. Where the Warp Curve is below 1, it causes a slow-down in the animation.And in this example shown that if warp curve is below zero, it makes the input channel go in reverse.

label: Wave
text: Use slider to scrub through Wave CHOP.Recommended to use the Pattern CHOP as it deals with curves as samples better.

label: Wave
text: single-cycle waves, some are repeating, some not.all are 600 samples long  which become 10 seconds since the sample rate is 60 set to samples/sec.Recommended to use the Pattern CHOP.

label: Wave
text: ease-in and ease-out with a Hold  extend condition at the start-end.It is often used as a lookup table.And when you use the easeinout for both the lookup table and index, you get tighter and tighter rises, keeping the slopes smooth.Recommended to use the Pattern CHOP
