(2) 60 indexes will be more than a second if you are dropping frames (the LFO is switching at 1 second, no matther how many dropoust there are.

label: Cache Select
text: This shows Cache Select POP interpolating between snapshots that were not captured every frame. Unbypass the Hog CHOP (expect it to stutter) and let it run for a few seconds with the Cache POP's Active parameter on.  Then turn off the Active toggle on the Cache POP. The bypass the Hog CHOP so TouchDesiner is running more smoothly again.In the info1 DAT you can see in the abstime_frame column that there are several-frame gaps between the captures. With Interpolate Frames set On on the Cache Select POP however, if you use the value ladder to step its Cache Index backward or forward by steps of 1 or .1, you will see interpolated shapes coming out for every stap.   With Interpolate Frames off it will stutter.Even when the Cache POP is Active, the Cache Select POP will interpolate whatever is in the Cache.

label: DMX Fixture
text: We now use per-point or per-primitive attributes and send these to a DMX Fixture POP where we specify the DMX channel layout. Each specified DMX channel will in sequence loop over all points and primitives to create all necessary DMX channels. For an LED strip we can think of each light to be part of an LED fixture with 3 channels: R, G, and B. So all that is to do is to specify a single DMX channel in the DMX Fixture CHOP and reference the input POP's Color attribute. As DMX channels now can be set to being normalized, it is in many cases not necessary to provide 0-255 values but a 0-1 range is used.The DMX Map DAT can be used to inspect the channel layout the DMX Fixture POP is creating.

label: DMX Fixture
text: A moving head light such as this (Chauvet Maveric Force S Spot in 29 channel mode) has various dmx channels of which some are 16 bit while others are 8 bit. To simplify this process, you can specify a DMX channel's value resolution on the DMX Fixture POP itself by setting the Value Resolution parameter appropriatly. FOr this particular fixture, there are some values that are great to be used normalized (like pan / tilt - Position) while for others, like Strobe or control for the wheels, it is more practical to use the DMX 0-255 value range.The fixture itself can be described as a single point with all attributes specified there. With the single point you can now also multiply (copy it), do your attribute lookups after and send all the points that represent the same fixture into a single DMX Fixture POP.The DMX Map DAT shows how it's using all points to create channel mappings for all the fixtures. For demonstration purposes we are adding a channel gap between all fixtures, which will insert n DMX channels inbetween each fixture. Also enabled is "Quantize Universe" making sure that if a fixture does not fit into a universe it will start in the next subsequent one.The fixture here has a single light source. You can find the DMX Chart here: https://www.chauvetprofessional.com/products/maverick-force-s-spot/

label: DMX Fixture
text: When dealing with multiple fixtures of the same type, it is possible to specify the dmx address by primitive attribute.The attribute names are:* DMXFixtureNet (uint)* DMXFixtureSubnet (uint)* DMXFixtureUniverse (uint)* DMXFixtureChannel (uint)* DMXFixtureNetAddress (uint4)In this example they are specified with the copy template and the created as primitive attributes by setting the Destination parameter of the Copy POP to Primitive.The fixture here has a single light source. You can find the DMX Chart here: https://www.chauvetprofessional.com/products/maverick-force-s-spot/

label: DMX Fixture
text: Many fixtures (like this Chauvet COLORado PXL Bar 8) have instances where a 16 bit Dimmer channel is followed by 16bit RGB channels for each light in the fixture requiring a sequence of DDRRGGBB.To make this work with the POP attribute approach, the "Merge with above Block" parameter must be turned on - following the example above - for the RGB channels. to allow interleaving on a per-point basis. Otherwise the Dimmer channels would be created per led followed by the RGB channels for each light. Also of note is the construction of the light here: The Pxl Bar 8 has 8 LEDs mounted on a tilting platform. The platform itself (Linestrip primitive) holds some attributes connected to the platform (tilt, speed, etc) while the LEDs (points) hold the attributes used for the LED DMX channelsYou can find the DMX Chart here: https://www.chauvetprofessional.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/COLORado_PXL-Bar_8_UM_Rev11.pdf

label: DMX Fixture
text: In this example of the Chauvet Maverick Storm 3 BeamWash, the LEDs of the outer ring have Dimmer, Strobe and RGB control while the main LEDs have Dimmer, Strob, and RGBA control. So the 2 sets of geometry must be treated seperatly ontop of being seperate from the Pan-Tilt platform they are on. For this requirement we can use the Group parameter of each DMX Channel sequence block. This example has a total of 424 channels. The main rule is still that each fixture is represented by a single primitive. While here initially 2 Linestrips are created with their respective attributes, after merging, the primitives are deleted and recreated as a single primitve. The attribue combine is used to copy the body channels onto the single resulting primitive. As a result the whole fixture can again be copied with a Copy POP and via lookups induvidually controlled while still all accessible via the single DMX Fixture POP.You can find the DMX Chart here: https://www.chauvetprofessional.com/products/maverick-storm-3-beamwash/

label: DMX Fixture
text: We have seen fixtures where the 16 bit position channels are not ordered in [Pan Coarse, Pan Fine, Tilt Coarse, Tilt Fine] order but the coarse channels are used first before the fine channels are send over. In these cases, you can make use of the DMX Fixture POP Sequence Parameter's Interleave Byte parameter. Enabling it for a 16 bit or merged block, this will order it in a way to use the first bit of all channels first, before using the second bit allowing for such uncommon addressing.

label: DMX Fixture
text: For a default LED Volume, use a Grid POP and convert to Line Strips according to your addressing needs. Making use of the Auto Layout feature for addressing would mean grouping the strips for a group of strips to fit into a universe. The less primitives the better for performance.The Primitive POP allows for this via the Method parameter's "By Set" option on the Primitive page.

label: DMX Out
text: The DMX Out POP is used to collect all fixtures from the network and send the DMX channels out to the actual devices via ArtNet, sACN, Kinet, or DMX via supported serial devices.Each fixture must have a unique DMX address. The DMX Out POP will not rearrange channels but warn or error for overlapping addressing.DMX Fixture POPs can either be connected to the input of the DMX Out POP or referenced on the POP's Fixtures parameter page. Each seperatly referenced fixture can be activated or deactivated on the DMX Out POP.One can also make use of wildcards while specifying fixtures.

label: DMX Out
text: Often times fixtures have a dimmer channel and with larger setups it might be useful to control all dimmer channels of all fixtures.The DMX Out POP allows for specifying, also using wildcards, DMX Channel names specified in the DMX Fixture POP and multiply them with the Multiplier Value parameter. Be advised that this can be a costly operation in setups with large setups like RGB volumes. It's best used as a Master Dimmer for the full installation.

label: Facet
text: Facet POP can make every vertex of every primitive be a unique point. When doing so, the normals of the points change since they are not longer shared with adjacent primitives.  Therefore the Compute Point Normals parameter is on by default, but if you som't care about normals, turn it of to gain some speed.null_normalVectors0 and null_normalVectors1 show this difference.The same is true when going fro unique points back to shared points when Operation is Consolidate Points. (facet2)facet3 makes the points unique if the angle between adjacent polygons exceeds a certain value, in this case 14 degrees.

label: Force Radial
topic: force radial, axial, spiral, planar, drag
text: A versatile particle force generator that can blend radial, planar, axial and spiral forces in any combination. Create everything from gentle gravitational wells to chaotic vortexes by mixing inward/outward radial pulls, directional streams, and rotational spirals. Each force component can be independently enabled, rotated, and tuned with customizable falloff curves. Perfect for simulating complex natural phenomena like tornadoes, magnetic fields, or abstract energy flows where multiple force types need to work together seamlessly.

label: Force Radial
text: When Falloff is set to S Curve, the Radius is the distance where the curve has dropped to 0. Plateau is a fraction of that distance up to which the value is constant, then dropping to 0 using an S Curve shaped by Steepness and Bias.For Falloff being Inverse Distance, Radius is the distance at which the value is 1 (before Strength is applied as a multiplier).Typically you will make Limit Near-Range be between 1 and 10, preventing an infinite force as you get closer to the center Position.Strength is applied as a straight multiplier at the end, after the above clamped rolloff curve is defined.Play with the pars after the Falloff menu.

label: Histogram
text: A histogram shows the distribution of numeric data and is most easily understood as a visualization of how frequently different values appear in a dataset.In this example here, a image is converted for its channels to only contain luminance values. These values are then counted into 256 individual value bins giving you as a result how many times a value is used in the image. The Histogram POP saves this information into an attribute which then, using the Analyze POP to give us the number of points in the POP, is normalized and the square root taken to compress the dynamic range and making the visualization more readable. The resulting attribute is then used to control the y-position of points of a Linestrip.

label: Noise
text: The Noise POP will generate noise based on the specified Noise Lookup Attribute. By using P in the Noise Lookup Attribute parameter you generate 3D noise based on the position of each point.

label: Noise
text: Sometimes it can be easier to generate a scalar noise attribute and then use that scaler noise to multiply 3D vectors like normals or positions or other attriburtes like color.Scalar attributes are simply attributes that have a single component - like float1 or int1.

label: Noise
text: This examaple uses 2 separate scalar noise components - "NoiseA" and "NoiseB". It's easy to operate on each noise attribute seperately, and in this example a Limit POP is used to clamp each noise attribute to be above 0. The two noises are then multiplied together and added back to P.z in the Math Mix POP. This exmaple uses 2 seperate scalar noise components - "noiseA" and "noiseB". It's easy to operate on each noise attribute seperately, and in this example a Limit POP is used to clamp each noise attribute. The noise is then multiplied together and added back to P.z in mathmix.

label: Noise
text: Using scalar (single float value) noise attributes in 3D is easy. This example shows the same process of combining two scalar attributes into a 3D deformation by multiplying the scalar noise by the 3D normals and subsequently adding the displaced normals back onto position.

label: Noise
text: A variety of Noise POP features from Overview.toe

label: Noise
text: A very basic particle system with Y motion, curl noise and particle death.

label: Noise
text: A very basic 3D particle system with Y motion, curl noise and particle death.

label: Normal
text: After some operations, the normals are not pointing in the correct direction. The Normal POP recomputes normals based on all the primitives that share a point or vertex.The normals attribute is always called N. It can exist on a point, vertex or primitive, and the Normal POP can compute point, vertex or primitive normals.In the case of the primitive normals, it uses the position of the three or more points of a primitive.

label: Null
text: By default, Null POP does nothing and takes no memory.Put it at the end of POP chains if you are likely to put POPs befroe it.

label: Out
text: Go inside base1. out1 is an Out POP that is ued to wire POPs from inside the component to nodes in its parent component, in this case null1.When you rollover the output connector, of base1 you see a custom label, "Nice Geonmetry", set with the IOut POP's Label parameter. The selec POP gets the same data but it's assoming the name of the Output POP is out1.

label: Particle
text: In the Particle POP...To Initialize a particle system at its initial state, press Initialize. You can usually set Pre-Roll to run it a few seconds to get it primed.To start a particle System, press Start.To end it, press Go to Done.

label: Particle
text: See also the Force Radial POP.

label: Phaser
text: The Phaser POP does staggered (time-offset) 3D animation interpolation. Phaser POP outputs point attributes that animate from one 3D position/state to another over a cycle, with each point transitioning at different times.

label: Phaser
text: This example shows how the phaser can quickly generate animation over a 3D grid.

label: Phaser
text: This example shows how the using the phaser attribute in the math mix we can convert the phase into rotation values for more complex animations.As well the ramp TOP is used to generate differnt transition patterns. As long as the transition patterns are switched when the phase fraction parameter is at 0 the animation transition is seemless.

label: Point
text: When simply one or more points need to be created.This cam be used to define constant-value attributes for Math Mix POP or Math Combine POP.THis can be used to drive the Specification POP input of Field POP or Force Radial POP, which defines one field or force per-point by naming the attributes like the parameter names.You can optionally create Point Primitives, but nothing more.

label: Polygonize
text: Polygonize POP will draw polygons or line strips wheren the input image crosses a specified threshold. This POP does not create arbitrary closed polygons like the Trace SOP.

label: Polygonize
text: This example shows how a rectangle can be used to create a bounding box that can subsequently be used to apply normalized texture coordinates such that the output of the Polgyonize POP can sample colors from the original image.

label: Projection
text: The Projection POP transforms point data between different coordinate systems. This allows you to manipulate points in the most suitable coordinate space for your desired effect.

label: Random
topic: random distribution using a lookup curve

label: Topology
text: The Topology POP allows you to limit particular topologies from passing onwards.  For example you can remove all quads or line strips from your POP chain.  Useful for troubleshooting and cleaning up geometry sets.

label: Topology
text: a variety of GLSL Advanced techniques with the Topology POP

label: Trig
text: These functions are also in math Mix POP, Math POP and Math Combine POPs.

label: ZED
text: When you install TouchDesigner, select the ZED option to install the correct set of libraries it needs.  First you need to cedeaet a ZED TOP and pick the appropriate camera.  The ZED POP can generate a point cloud or a triangleular "mesh".For Mesh to work, only one ZED POP can be active at a time.In Depth mode, each ZED POP can process data from either stereo camera left or right.Note: The Depth parameters are the same as those found in the Depth page of TOP to POP.The quality controls for the resulting point cloud shown in ZED POP are found in ZED TOP through the Camera Resolution and Depth Quality parameters.For more information about the Depth Quality models see https://www.stereolabs.com/docs/depth-sensing/depth-modes#neural-plus Sensing Mode menu for the ZED TOP gives these choices:Standard - Uses depth map that preserves edges and depth accuracy.Interpolate - Interpolate between depth edges.For generating meshes set the Output Mode to Mesh and the Connectivity to Triangles or Point Primitives.The Normal and Color toggles will generate meshes with point Normals and point Color attributes.The Mesh Output Mode follows the Initialize Start convention similar to Timer CHOP. Pressing start will accumulate geometries that can be seen in the preview and Go to Done will generate the final mesh.Play can be toggled while sampling to pause generation and enabled to resume.A smaller Resolution creates a detailed geometry and higher Resolution keeps only bigger vairiations in geometry.A smaller Range will use objects close to the camera and higher Range will use objects that are farther from the camera.The ZED TOP is able to playback from an SVO (StereoLabs Video Format) file recorded with the ZED SDK.

label: Add
text: Polygons in clockwise order.

label: Add
text: In this example we add a second box to the scene that contains a sphere.

label: Add
text: In this example we manually added 4 points and created a square.

label: Add
text: The Add SOP has a useful function in that it can delete the geometry of an input while keeping the points for later use.

label: Align

label: Attribute Create
text: This example illustrates how to use the Attribute Create SOP to create tangents per-vertex. middle-click on the Attribute Create SOP to see the T attribute created.If you bypass Attrbute Create, the Render TOP complains when you use a normal map but don't set the tangent attributes, so you can just follow its warning and create the tangent attribute.Note: To create a user-named attribute, you can use the Point SOP or the Vertex SOP.Note: the Transform TOP adds alpha 1 to the background, which you can also do many ways, like with the Level TOP or Reorder TOP.Reference:Outrun / Landscapes – TouchDesigner Tutorial 19 - YouTubehttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K8vIg2t3wDo&ab_channel=bileamtschepe

label: Attribute Create
text: See the Point SOP to create custom attributes and set values in them.

label: Basis
text: This example re-parameterizes the knot sequence manually by input values.

label: Blend
text: The Blend SOP can blend between two inputs.Here we blend between two different sized boxes.

label: Blend
text: The Blend SOP is unique in that you can individual weight how much each input effects the blend.Here we've individually weighted the various inputs to create our output geometry.When Diferncing is On, the Blend  Weight of Input 0 has no effect.

label: Boolean
text: The Boolean SOP lets you use one SOP to add to subtract from another. NOTE: Both SOPs muse be polygonal and must be closed, eg. no open tubes etc.James Hunter

label: Box
text: These examples show different ways of using the Size parameter to change the shape of a Box.

label: Box
text: The Center parameters allow you to translate the Box in 3D space.

label: Box
text: The Box SOP will create a Box around the input  geometry.

label: Box
text: This example demonstrates that you can consolidate the corner points of the Box and the compute correct normals for each primitive.

label: Box
text: These examples divide the Box SOP by the assigned values in the Division parameter.You can also create enforcement bars for the geometry when you create divisions.

label: Box
text: The Box SOP has a handy feature in that you can assign your texture UV upon creation.

label: Cache
text: The Cache SOP collects its input geometry in a cache (in RAM memory) for faster random-access playback. It can be used when cook times for a chain of SOPs is long and a quicker playback is needed.In this example the SOP animation of a bouncing sphere is cached. Hit the "Start Playback" button to start plackback of the cached animation.The Cache Size (240) matches the Timer CHOP length (4 seconds at 60 fps).  For the Output Index parameter, the beginning index is 0 and the ending is 239.chopexec1 is set up to initialize on the first frame of the cache when you press and hold down Start Playback, then to start the playback when you release teh button.Exercise: disable the viewer pane of the "transform1" SOP. (the icon on the top-left of that node). Notice how the Transform SOP stops cooking (the wire from transform1 to cache1 is no longer animating). This demontrates TouchDesigner is a pull based system: nodes only cook if nodes downstream ask for their data. In this case the cache SOP is already filled, so the nodes upstream don't have to cook anymore.

label: Cap
text: This example places a rounded end cap on the input geometry.

label: Cap
text: This example places a faceted cap on the input geometry.

label: Carve
text: Here we've carved out two of the sides of the rectangle using the First U and Second U  parameters.

label: Carve
text: Using breakpoints means that the Carve SOP will be applied at breakpoints for for curves, and vertices for polygons, as opposed  to exactly where your carve points are.

label: Carve
text: These examples show the difference between the Keep Inside and Keep Outside

label: Carve
text: You can extract the 2D or 3D isoparametric profile of your geometry using the Carve SOP.

label: Carve
text: These two examples feature some more complex geometry being carved out of a sphere.

label: Carve
text: The Carve SOP can also extract the points specificed, instead of extracting a cross section.

label: CHOP to
text: Basic CHOP to SOP. You need to match channel names to SOP attributes.

label: CHOP to
text: This example uses a SOP to CHOP to get all the point positions of a circle. Noise is applied to each of the point positions, then using a CHOP to SOP, we apply these new values to the Normals.This allows us to control the direction in which the particles in particle1 are emitted.

label: CHOP to
text: This is a more advanced example of using a CHOP to SOP.In the example a few SOP to CHOPs are used to  gather data on the points and normals. After modulating, the point positions, along  with newly created RGBA values are added back to the geometry using a CHOP to SOP.

label: Circle
text: These examples explore the the circle's Radius, Center and Anchor parameters.Moddle mouse click on the nodes to see that the top set is a polygon woth 40 points, and the second one is a circle primitive, which only has a center point and a radius.We most often deal with circles as polygons.Anchors are very useful. Instead of the "Center" X Y Z being at the center of the circle, you can make the center X Y Z be where the left, right, top, bottom edges of circle are placed.Anchor .5, .5 is center of the circle.  circle_center_using_anchor has anchor x = 0, the left side.

label: Circle
text: This example controls the divisions of a polygon circle. Just 6 poinst around the perimeter, where the first (point 0) starts at the right-most side... where x = 1, y = 0.Note that this polygon is "closed", set by the Arc Type parameter, which means the last point (5) is connected back to the first point (0).Turn on Viewer Active, press w for wireframe, right-click on the viewer to get the Viewing menu, then select Display Options (shortcut is p), and turn on the top-left 2 flags - dots and point numbers.

label: Circle
text: This is an example of a closed arc type, that starts at angle 180 and ends at 360.

label: Circle
text: This is similar to a closed arc except that every point is connected to the center of the circle forming 10 triangles.It is then faceted (Unique Points on), and then each polygon shrunk to show them as separate polygons.

label: Circle
text: Open Arc types don't close themselves.Start to end goes counter-clockwise if start angle is less than end angle.     (right hand rule for increasing angles - put your right hand thumb pointing along the +z axis (from the screen toward you), and positive is in the direction of your other fingers. You can go past 360 degrees and below 0 degrees.If start < end, it's clockwise.

label: Clay
text: The Clay SOP deforms faces and surfaces by pulling points that lie directly on them.The point that defines the area to be modified is called a "target point" or "target" for short. It is expressed as a (u,v) pair in the parametric space of the primitive and ranges between 0 and 1 in both U and V. The image of the target point on the primitive is a 3D point which Clay can displace in several ways.This snippet shows examples of the warp method "Point".

label: Clay
text: The Clay SOP deforms faces and surfaces by pulling points that lie directly on them.The point that defines the area to be modified is called a "target point" or "target" for short. It is expressed as a (u,v) pair in the parametric space of the primitive and ranges between 0 and 1 in both U and V. The image of the target point on the primitive is a 3D point which Clay can displace in several ways.This snippets shows the warp method "Matrix":  this method relocates the target point based on a transformation matrix.

label: Clay
text: The Clay SOP deforms faces and surfaces by pulling points that lie directly on them.The point that defines the area to be modified is called a "target point" or "target" for short. It is expressed as a (u,v) pair in the parametric space of the primitive and ranges between 0 and 1 in both U and V. The image of the target point on the primitive is a 3D point which Clay can displace in several ways.This snippets shows the warp method "Vector":  this method relocates the target point based on a distance and a vector to translate along.

label: Clay
text: The Clay SOP deforms faces and surfaces by pulling points that lie directly on them.The point that defines the area to be modified is called a "target point" or "target" for short. It is expressed as a (u,v) pair in the parametric space of the primitive and ranges between 0 and 1 in both U and V. The image of the target point on the primitive is a 3D point which Clay can displace in several ways.This snippets show the warp method "Primitive":  this allows the target point to be snapped to a point on another primitive. Any primitive type is allowed, including metaballs, all quadrics, and even particle systems. The point on the destination primitive is expressed parametrically as a (U, V) pair.

label: Clip
text: These examples show how the Clip SOP cuts the geometry where at the animated plane.

label: Convert
text: This example changes the Mesh sphere to a polygon sphere.

label: Convert
text: This example changes the faces of the box to circles.

label: Convert
text: This example shows how each triangle of the polygon sphere was changed to a Bezier surface.

label: Copy
text: With Transform Cumulative set to On, the transform values that each copy inherit are cumulative.

label: Copy
text: This example makes a copy of the Copy SOPs input 1 at every point of input 2.

label: Copy
text: You can stamp any parameter as long as the parameter is upstream.In these example we use the number of copies to change a parameter upstream of the Copy SOP, in this case transform1 and circle2. Use the expression fetchStamp('name', devaultval) and a downstream Copy SOP will evaluate it and use it for each copy it makes.

label: CPlusPlus
text: See samples in the GitHub repository:https://github.com/TouchDesigner/CustomOperatorSamples

label: Creep
text: This example demonstrates how to use a Creep SOP to animate the first input's geometry along the surface of the second input's geometry.The second geometry should be a mesh of rows and columns. For each input point, its x and y is moved to the position on input2's mesh, where u=x and v=y, and u=0 to u=1 span the mesh's columns, and v=0 to v=1 span the mesh's rows.If the input1 points are outside the 0-1 range they are looped to always be in the 0-1 range.The Z value of the points of input1 will lift the points off the surface for z>0 and under the surface for z<0.All the points of input1 are first transformed according to the Creep SOP's Translate, Rotate and Scale parameters.Since straight edges are going to be warped, it's best to subdivide the surfaces that you are creeping.The divide SOP is also good for chopping up surfaces for deforming.If you connect box1 to the first input, it creeps on the surface and is deformed as well.

label: Curveclay
text: The Curveclay SOP is similar to the Clay SOP in that you deform a spline surface by directly manipulating the surface. However, instead of using a point on the surface, you use one or more faces to deform that surface. Note that the Curveclay SOP does not yet support polygonal meshes.

label: Curveclay
text: The Curveclay SOP is similar to the Clay SOP in that you deform a spline surface by directly manipulating the surface. However, instead of using a point on the surface, you use one or more faces to deform that surface. Also, Curveclay SOP does not yet support polygonal meshes. When using three inputs, the Curveclay SOP can do its own projection. The amount of displacement is decided by the amount the 3rd input (Deformed 3d face) is transformed from the 2nd input (Rest 3D face).In this example the geometry has been translated 0.25 in z and rotated in y, so the difference for each point is the amount of displacement.Experiment by changing the Translate/Rotate/Scale parameters in transform1 SOP.

label: Curvesect
text: The Curvesect SOP finds the intersections or the points of minimum distance between two or more faces (polygons, Bziers, and NURBS curves) or between faces and a polygonal or spline surface. In this snippet we extract the points of intersection between two NURBS curve circles. These points are then used to instance red spheres at those positions.

label: Curvesect
text: The Curvesect SOP finds the intersections or the points of minimum distance between two or more faces (polygons, Bziers, and NURBS curves) or between faces and a polygonal or spline surface. In this snippet we use the "cut" method of the Curvesect SOP, and we keep only the even-numbered (top) or odd-numbered pieces (bottom) of the first input of the Curvesect SOPs.

label: DAT to
text: Three DATs are needed for DAT to SOP: 1) For points that hold the XYZ positions and other attributes like Texture UV and point color.2) For Vertices that specify other attributes that may also include Texture UV.3) For the Primitives that specify which points are connected for each polygon or other primitive type.

label: DAT to
text: The first example creates the squares using all the data in the tables.The second example uses all of the information about the squares but then closes them on it's own.

label: DAT to
text: You can also use a DAT to SOP to edit specific values of the input, in this case we changes the point positions.

label: Delete
text: This example deletes primitives by pattern.

label: Delete
text: This example deletes points by pattern.

label: Delete
text: This example deletes primitives by range.The ranges start and end values of 0 and 6, include all the  box's primitives.And selecting 1 of 2, means that half of the primitives between primitive 0 and 6 will be deleted.

label: Delete
text: This is another example of deleting by range.In this example 1 of every 5 primitives between primitive 0 and 361 are deleted.

label: Delete
text: You can use Delete SOP to delete by bounding box.This means that everything that falls inside the box will be deleted.

label: Delete
text: This is another example of deleting by bounding box.In this example the bounding box's movement is animated.

label: Delete
text: You can delete primitives by spread angle.This compares the angle of each normal against the direction you specify using the Direction values, and i the angle is greater than the Spread Angle value, that primitive will be deleted.

label: Delete
text: This is another example of deleting by spread angle.You can see in this example the spread angle deletes all the peaks from the simulated geography.

label: Divide
text: The Divide SOP divides incoming polygonal geometry. It will smooth input polygons, dividing polygons, as well as sub-divide input polygons using the Bricker option.

label: Divide
text: The Divide Sop can Bricker polygons, where it divides the incoming gemoetry into grid-like squares. Also the offset and angle can be adjusted.

label: Divide
text: The Divide SOP can also be used to find all polygon centers. When using the "compute dual" option, the Divide SOP creates a new mesh (the dual graph) that has a point at the center of each previous polygon. You can then use an Add SOP to delete all geometry and only keep those points for further processing.

label: Divide
text: The Divide SOP can give interesting effects when playing with the weight parameters of the Smooth Polygons options, combined with Compute Dual.

label: Divide
text: Here the "remove shared edges" option of the Divide SOP removes all internal edges of the Grid SOP

label: Extrude
text: This example creates 3D extruded text using the Extrude SO, startin with the Text SOP.The Attribute Create SOP is used at the end to compute the normals.

label: Extrude
text: In extrude3 for example, you can visualize it as placing the cross-section on each vertex of the input so that the first point in the curve (1,0) is on the vertex, the top of the curve (0,1) extends outwards from the surface (opposite the normal, along +Z) and then curves back down to the surface outside the bounds of the original geometry. The letters are hollow because only the side mesh (the geometry along the cross-section curve), is generated. The front and back faces of the extrusion are disabled by setting their parameters to 'No Output'.The main things to adjust are the Thickness Scale and the Depth Scale as well as the circle arc angles, which you can leave at a radius of 1.

label: Extrude
text: Be careful you are not extruding faces that are already triangulated because each triangle will be extruded.It is better to extrude the/outlines of letters in the Text SOP. The Text SOP is by defult triangulating its faces, so you need to turn its Output menu to Closed Polygons.

label: Facet
text: This example consolidates corner points on this sphere.

label: Facet
text: This example creates a series of unique points at corners where different primitives meet.Notice in sphere2 where there is a single point at each corner, whereas in the viewer of the Facet SOP, you can see multiple points at each corner where primitives meet.

label: Facet
text: This example computes the normals of the sphere after creating unique points, allowing you to more clearly see the effect of creating unique points. In the Primitive SOP, each of the newly made primitive is scaled down to create the 3D pattern.

label: File In
text: You can import a variety of 3D geometry from other 3D suites using the File In SOP.

label: File In
text: If your imported geometry is missing normals, you can compute them in the File In SOP.

label: Fillet
text: This example shows how a Fillet SOP can create a surface inbetween two curves or polygons without affecting the input geometry.

label: Fit
text: These two examples  use the approximation method to fit Test to the data points, without having to touch all of them.This is useful for leaning down very complex data sets.

label: Fit
text: The Tolerance parameter is the precision factor for the Approximation method. The higher the value the less amount of vertices are made during the fit.Additional smoothing can  be applied via the Smoothness parameter.

label: Fit
text: This example uses the interpolation method to fit Test to the data points, making sure to touch every point.This is generally more useful on smaller data sets, as it can sometimes produce more control vertices than originally input.

label: Force
text: Using the Force SOP you can apply different kinds of forces to particle systems.In this example, a vortex force is applied for as long as you hold down the 'Hold for Force' button.

label: Fractal
text: This example creates divisions along the input surface.

label: Fractal
text: The Fractal SOP is useful when creating terrain like geometry.

label: Grid
text: You can see the difference between the grid and the rectangle when you look at their wireframes.

label: Group
text: The Group SOP gives you the ability to limit the "scope" of downstream SOPs without forking them off individually. For example, here we create a group for the sphere, box and torus individually. Then we can affect each in isolation, despite being in the same SOP chain.Note the Group selection at the top of many SOPs.James Hunter

label: Group
text: The Group SOP gives you the ability to limit the "scope" of downstream SOPs without forking them off individually. group3 groups by normals that point up and right, and group inclusion is animated using the Spread angle parameter.

label: Group
text: Creating a group of primitives within one SOP, named "top".Transforming just that group.

label: Group
text: Points of the sphere are first sorted in random order. Then the first 500 points are grouped into groupA.In the top example, to be able to delete points from the sphere, and still see the remaining shape, the sphere mesh is first converted to polygons before points from groupA are deleted.In the next example, a new groupB is created using a bounding sphere (outlined in red). Then both groupA and groupB are combined and all their points are deleted.

label: Hole
text: This example demonstrates how you can create and fill holes easily with the Hole SOP.

label: In
text: The In SOP is used to create a SOP input in a Component. Component inputs are positioned alphanumerically on the left side of the Component.In this example base1 COMP has a blue SOP input on its left side. Zoom into base1 to see the In SOP, and notice the In SOP has an Sphere SOP acting as fallback data connected to its second input. This will be outputted by the In SOP if nothing is connected to the SOP input of its parent, the base1 COMP.Zoom out of base1 and connect box1 to base1, and see the output change.Also, when you rollover the input, you see a custom label, set with the In TOP's Label parameter. Similar for the output.

label: Iso Surface
text: These are a few examples of how Iso Surfaces are made using Implicit Functions.

label: Join
text: The Join SOP takes the input geometry, which contains various lines in this case, and joins them together smoothly.

label: Joint
text: This example shows how the Joint SOP creates circles between a pair of input circles.This can be used in the creation of circle-based skeletons.

label: Lattice
text: This example animates the deformation of the TEST geometry with the twisted grid.Notice how the Lattice SOP is set up as it takes specific inputs.

label: Limit
text: At its simplest, the Limit SOP creates geometry from CHOP channels.Here, we've made a table with some very simple point positions, which  are then converted to CHOP channels, and fed to the Limit SOP.

label: Limit
text: In this example we created out CHOP channels using a SOP to CHOP, then converted our channels back to a SOP using the Limit SOP.The difference is that the Limit SOP uses a single Polygonal Line to connect all the points of the Grid.

label: Limit
text: Directly to the right, we use two sine wave LFOs to create two channels needed to draw a circle using the Limit SOP.

label: Limit
text: Here we modulate our original sine waves with faster sine waves.When we use the Limit SOP with these channels, we see a more interesting pattern with swirls.

label: Limit
text: If we use a triangle wave to modulate both of the previous examples channels, we can create a more interesting pattern that becomes larger and smaller as the triangle wave increases and decreases.One of the two SOPs at the end have Adaptive Homing off.

label: Limit
text: The Limit SOP has different output options.These three examples show three of the common examples used to draw some Sparse noise.

label: Line
text: You can create lines by assigning co-ordinates to the start and end of the line (Point A and Point B).

label: Line
text: You can add more points to your line using the Number of Points parameter.

label: Line Thick
text: This example demonstrates how the Line Thick SOP can extrude a surface from a curved line.

label: LSystem
text: L-Systems can be quite complicated but can create very organic geometry.This is a very basic example that creates a tree with circle and triangle leaves.

label: Magnet
text: This example deforms the grid by using the metaball's force.Adding more or less weight to the metaball changes the deformation amount.

label: Material
text: The Material SOP allows you to assign textures to geometry at the SOP level. This is useful if you need to texture a specific part of a more complex piece of geometry, as it takes precedence over the Material soecified in the Geometry component parameter.

label: Merge
text: merge1 and merge2 both merge three diferent SOPs, done in two different ways.merge1 dies it by wiring, merge2 by giving the SOPs parameter a list of names.As you see in merge3, you can give it a pattern and it will merge all SOPs that match that pattern.See "Pattern Matching" in the wiki.merge3 also shows that you can mix wired inputs with SOPs specified in the SOPs parameter.

label: Metaball
text: This example demonstrates the Metaball SOPs property of changing their shape to adapt and fuse with surrounding metaballs.

label: Noise
text: This example applies a Sparse noise to the point positions.

label: Noise
text: This example applies a Hermite noise to the sphere's normals.

label: Noise
text: This example applies Sparse noise to the sphere's diffuse color.

label: Noise
text: This example applies a Sparse noise to the point alpha.

label: Noise
text: Here we've actually applied the noise to the point texture UV coordinates.

label: Noise
text: This example combines applying noise to the diffuse color and the point positions.

label: Object Merge
text: You can use an Object Merge SOP to merge geometry.

label: Object Merge
text: The interesting aspect of the Object Merge SOP is that it takes the transform parameters of the referenced SOPs Geometry Component into account.See the above and below example that yield the same results. In the above example, all the transforms are done at the SOP level, while in the below example, all the transforming is done at the Component level.

label: Particle
text: These two examples show the two different modesof the Particle SOP.The first is to use the input geometry for emitting the particles.The second is to modify the input geometry using theparticles.

label: Particle
text: There are 3 different collision modes for particles. The first is to bounce on collision. The second is to die on collision.The third is to stick on collision.

label: Particle
text: This example adds some wind to the X axis.

label: Particle
text: This example adds turbulence to the wind, giving it more natural and random movement.

label: Particle
text: This example combines wind values and stick on contact collisions to give a very simple snow-like effect.

label: Particle
text: These examples show you how to use a Metaball SOP and a Force SOP to create forces more complex than Wind.

label: Particle
text: This is an example of how to use a Particle system to instance point sprites.

label: Particle
text: Look at the Palette component particlesGpu. Particles on the GPU run much faster.

label: Point
text: In this example we've taken the points of the sphere and performed simple operations on their tx and ty parameters.

label: Point
text: On the Custom page, create new attributes and set values.

label: Polyloft
text: This example illustrates how to use the Polyloft SOP.The two arcs have differnet numbers of points. The SOP finds ht nearest neighbor.With Viewer Active, rclick -> Display Options to se the disply of points.Reference:映像音響処理概論 2018 第7回 Satoru Higahttp://satoruhiga.com/TDWS2018/day7/

label: Polypatch
text: The Polypatch SOP creates a smooth surface from an input of either a mesh primitive or a set of faces (polygons, NURBS or Bezier curves). In this example the rotated lines are used as input, which results in a smooth helix surface.The rest of the example adds small spheres to each point of the resulting polypatch surface, and then adds some random coloring using the Point SOP.

label: Polyreduce
text: The Polyreduce SOP reduces a high detail polygonal model into one consisting of fewer polygons.

label: Polyspline
text: The Polyspline SOP fits a spline curve to a polygon or hull and outputs a polygonal approximation of that spline. In this snippet you see several spline methods, starting at the top with the most basic one, creating an open Bezier curve to fit within a Rectangle.Play with the CV Tension parameter on each polyspline SOP to adjust the curve of the splines. The greater the tension, the closer the resulting shape will be to the original shape. When using the Polyspline SOP, it is useful to enable Points display in the Viewport options dialog, as done in this snippet (the blue dots indicate points). This way you can see exactly what effect the SOP is having.

label: Polystitch
text: The Polystitch SOP attempts to stitch polygonal surfaces together.In this example two grids with different detail levels, are placed next to eachother. Some primitives at the border of each grid are then placed in a group, and then stitched together.

label: Polystitch
text: The Polystitch SOP attempts to stitch polygonal surfaces together.In this example two meshes with different detail levels, are placed next to eachother. The bordering primitives are then placed in a group via a bounding box, and then stitched together.

label: Primitive
text: In this example the Primitive SOP is used to transform each individual primitive of the input geometry.We've specifically rotated each primitive 40 degrees on the Z axis.Click the 'Do Transformation' on and off to see a before and after.

label: Primitive
text: Here we've closed the open circle using a curved surface.

label: Primitive
text: Here we've closed the circle using a straight line.

label: Profile
text: The Profile SOP enables the extraction and manipulation of profiles. In this snippet the Project SOP creates a profile by projecting the half circle parametrically onto the grid.The Profile SOP is then used to extract that profile.

label: Profile
text: The Profile SOP enables the extraction and manipulation of profiles. This is an example of manipulating a profile - when the Profile Sop is set to remapping, the profile can be moved along the surface on which it is projected by the Project SOP.

label: Project
text: The Project SOP creates curves on a surface (also known as trim or profile curves) by projecting a 3D face onto a spline surface, much like a light casts a shadow onto a surface. In this example the 3d face is a triangle, and it is projected onto a tube surface.If, like in this example, the 3d face is contained within the target object, it will will be projected on both sides of the target object. Try positioning the triangle outside the tube, and notice now only one side of the target object receiving a profile curve.If a Trim Sop is used after the Project SOP, you can cut out the curves from the target object.

label: Project
text: The Project SOP creates curves on a surface (also known as trim or profile curves) by projecting a 3D face onto a spline surface, much like a light casts a shadow onto a surface. In this example the 3d face is the character X which is projected on a grid surface. The projected curve is then cuit with a Trim SOP. The surface with cutout is then copied and rotated, and a Bridge SOP is used to create skinned NURBs surface between the two cutout curves.

label: Rails
text: The example uses two Line SOPs as  the rails and creates a bigger copy of rectangle as the rails move further apart.

label: Rails
text: This is another basic example of  using the Rails SOP.It is similar to the above example except that the  rails are parallel.

label: Rails
text: This example shows the more points there are along the rails the more copies of the  input geometry are made.

label: Rails
text: This example uses curved rails. Notice how the offset of the rails creates copies of the input geometry on an angle, as it creates the copy between point 0 of both rails.

label: Raster
text: The Raster SOP converts TOP image data to geometry by scanning left to right, top to bottom, outputting a geometry point at each pixel. This output can be used to display image data on laser devices, oscilloscopes or similar devices using the Laser CHOP. This is different from the Trace SOP which only outputs contour shapes from an image.The Raster SOP will give each SOP point a color matching the input TOP colors.In this example the top Raster TOP uses horiontal rasterization, the bottom Raster Top uses vertical rasterization.  Depending on the input image, horizontal or vertical might work better. res1 lowers to number of lines and points generated.Note that it doesn't create points for pixels that are black in the TOP's RGB channels.  (to verify, add some color in constant1)To see what the resulting SOP data looks like for both methods, a Point SOP is used to remove all point colors, and then a Null SOP shows a zoomed in view of the top of the banana. Note that by default it is set to output on the frame after the TOP is generated - which is optimal and doesn't stall the GPU.

label: Ray
text: In this example we've used the Ray SOP to project the flat text onto the surface of the sphere.

label: Rectangle
text: You can select which plane you'd like your rectangle to occupy.Each one of these 3 examples uses different plane settings.

label: Rectangle
text: Try looking around in the Viewer of 'cam1' and the rectangle will always fill the camera's view.

label: Refine
text: Here we've increased the amount of points between the First U and Second U parameters.

label: Resample
text: This example resamples the input geometry with a maximum length segment of 0.1, creating more evenly spaced points along the geometry.

label: Resample
text: This examples uses the Along Arc measure to resample the input geometry.By animating the Segments parameter, we can see the arc change as the segments increase.

label: Revolve
text: This example uses the Revolve SOP to create a surface via the revolution of the input geometry.

label: Script
text: We use the Script SOP to copy the contents of transform1, as well as creating a 5-vertex closed polygon.See docs.derivative.ca/Geometry_Detail.

label: Script
text: This uses the Script SOP to run a simple operation on every point in the sphere in the first input.It also creates a 3-sided polygon.  The expressions that control the animationof the geometry are added to custom parameters.

label: Script
text: This creates a triangle and then adds some attributes, both built-in custom attrbutes and user-defined custom attributes.Note you can creatre attributes on points, or attributes on the verties of a primitive (like a triangle).It shows how to add the uvw texture coordinates as a point attribute using pointAttribs.create('uv'), and then set uv values for the points. (Note you can set uvw on polygon vertices too, independent of the points list. See the Texture SOP.)right-click on the viewer and select Display Options to get the uv coordinatas to display.The SOP to DAT shows the point attributes in a table.

label: Script
text: This creates a scattering of points along a surface.See also the Sprinkle SOP.

label: Script
text: This uses Custom Parameters to create a polygon with 3-6 points.The custom integer slider is defined in the setupParameters callback, and is read from the cook callback.

label: Script
text: This creates a scattering of points along a surface, similar to the previous example, but instead adds a Custom Parameter Pulse button to create a new seed value for the random points. This seed is stored in the callback script's Python storage, and can be seen using the Examine DAT.See also the Sprinkle SOP.

label: Script
text: Here we use the Script SOP to copy the contents of transform1, as well as create a 5-vertex closed polygon.

label: Script
text: The Script SOP creates 5 topologies of surfaces.Use the "switch1" operator to switch between the python scripts on the left. Each python script shows how to program different geometry using a Script SOP.--------tutorial: https://guidoschmidt.cc/j/2020/td-generative-meshesauthor: Guido Schmidt <mail@guidoschmidt.cc>

label: Script
text: Use glyphIndex as groupnameTry / except to created the primGroup if it doesn't existFor each prim, add to primGroup

label: Sequence Blend
text: The Sequence Blend SOP allows us to blend between different inputs.Use the slider to blend between these three different shaped boxes.

label: Sequence Blend
text: This example animates a blend across four different boxes.

label: Skin
text: This example creates a skin between the two merged grids.

label: Skin
text: This example creates a surface between the two curves, one being NURBS, and both having differing number of points.

label: Sort
text: The simplest function of the Sort SOP is to sort and change the order of the points of the incoming geometry.

label: Sort
text: You can sort the points in a random order.This example animates the Random seed, allowing you  to see the point numbers change.

label: Sort
text: Sort SOP allows you to sort the numbers of primitives.This example puts them in a random order.

label: Sort
text: This example sorts the  primitives in reverse order.

label: Sort
text: This example deletes primitives within a certain range.Because the Sort SOP's random seed is animated, the primitives that fall within that range are changing, thus creating the animated pattern.

label: Sort
text: When doing transparency two main things need to be done: Turn on Blending in the Common page of the MAT, and ensure your primitive are sorted farthest to nearest. Notice how the random sorted geometry looks wrong, even though it's the same geometry as the one that's properly sorted.If you have the horsepower, another way do to transparency is to use Order Independent Transparency on the Advanced page of the Render TOP. Adjust the Transparency Passes parameter to see the randomly sorted geometry become more/less correctly. Depending on the scene only a few passes may be needed to create an  acceptable look.

label: Sort
text: The particle system on the left is not properly sorted farthest to closest, while the one on the right is. Notice the blending artifacts only appear if they particles are not proplery sorted.Order Independent Transparency can also be used to help fix particle system blending artifacts, however many passes are needed.

label: Sphere
text: This Sphere SOP creates a sphere "primitive spheret type". which is not a set of polygons, but defined by a center point and radius.Middle-click on the node (Alt-rightclick without a proper 3-button mouse) to see that it is a spehere primitive and no polygons or meshes.In wireframe (press Viewer Active and then 'w') it is represented as three circles.To change it to polygons or a mesh, change the Primitive Type menu and check the info (again, middle mouse click or, click the "i" on the parameter dialog).

label: Sphere
text: This example lowers the frequency of the polygon count of the Sphere, making it more jagged.

label: Sphere
text: You can use meshes to create spheres, as well as NURBS and Bezier curves.

label: Sphere
text: You can control the shape of the sphere by using the Radius parameters.

label: Sphere
text: You can use the input of a Sphere SOP to create a sphere around the input geometry.

label: Spring
text: This example demonstrates the ability of the Spring SOP to deform geometry based on simple physics simulations.

label: Sprinkle
text: sprinkle1 distributes on any surface with a random uniform distribution. All surfaces are first converted to triangles.sprinkle2 disributes points inside a closed volume.sperinkle3 takes a bounding box of geometry and distributes randomly in that bounding volume.sprinkle4 again distributes on a surface with a random uniform distribution, no matter the size of the polygons it lies on.On sprinkle5, each triangle gets aproximately the same number of points, so points are more concentrated on small polygons.sprinkle6 turns on the Consolidate option, where after the points are distributed, they are reduced so that there is a minimum distance between points.

label: Sprinkle
text: If a surface is deforming, you may want the sprinked points to stick to the same part of your surface. To do this, put your undeformed surface into the first input, and the deformed surface into the second input.

label: Stitch
text: Here the Stitch SOP is used to stitch  the two sphere fragments together into a smooth surface.

label: Subdivide
text: This example changes the Box in input 1 to the low polygon count Sphere in input 2.

label: Subdivide
text: You can turn a Cube into a Sphere using the Subdivide SOP with a Depth of 2.

label: Subdivide
text: The Subdivide SOP takes an input polygon surface, and divides each face to create a smoothed polygon surface using a Catmull-Clark subdivision algorithm.By tuning the depth and crease weight parameters, you can bevel a cube to have round corners.

label: Superquad
text: Super Quadratic math generates shapes within a 1 unit cube.Including a rounded box.

label: Surfsect
text: In this example the Surfsect SOP performs boolean operations on NURBS surfaces.

label: Sweep
text: This is a simple example of how to use a Sweep SOP.This example places a copy of the input G at each point of the backbone.

label: Sweep
text: This example is the same as the above, except that the Sweep SOP is set to skin and close the output geometry.

label: Sweep
text: You are able to twist and roll the copies placed on the backbone.

label: Switch
text: The Switch SOP in this example switches between the two inputs.

label: Text
text: The Text SOP is used to create 3D type.Here we've animated the Bold and Italic parameters to demonstrate basic formatting.For more formatting options, see the Font SOP.

label: Text
text: If you want points that are more evenly separated, follow with a Resample SOP.Set Text SOP to Putput: Closed Polygons or Open Polygons.resample2 has most uniform separation.

label: Text
text: text1 doesn't render well as the inner polygons are separate from the outer ones and get filled when shaded.The Hole SOP bridges them and makes them ready for rendering, and has the nice attribute of one primitive per character.text2 trinagiates everything in the Text SOP, so it's triangulated before the renderer gets to it.The glyphIndex and cpIndex preserves which character it is from.text3 triangulates and extrudes in one shot.

label: Texture
text: All these examples show the various ways of assigning UV and W cooridnates to input geometry, with the ability to scale and offset the texture in the process.

label: Torus
text: You can use the Rows and Columns parameters to control the polygon count of a Torus SOP.

label: Torus
text: There are different Torus types you can  create, including Polygon, Mesh, NURBS, and Bezier.This example is a mesh.

label: Torus
text: This example is NURBS Torus.

label: Torus
text: You can control the shape of a Torus using the raidus parameters.

label: Torus
text: You can translate the Torus using the Center parameters.

label: Trace
text: This example traces Smiley.tif and extrudes the traced image.

label: Trace
text: You are able to also trace movies.

label: Trace
text: Notice how the 2nd example fits the steps to a curve.

label: Trail
text: The Trail SOP creates trails of movement.Here we've used it to create a trail of the movement of the different primitives of the noise affected box.

label: Transform
text: You should always try to  do most of your transforming at the Component level (on your Geo Component, instead of your SOPs), as it requires far less of your computer's  resources.This becomes especially apparent when animating transforms.

label: Transform
text: These examples translate the box in 3D space.

label: Transform
text: Transform SOP can be used to scale as well.

label: Transform
text: This example rotates the text 180 degrees on the Z axis.

label: Transform
text: This example rotates the torus 100 degrees on the X axis.

label: Transform
text: Rotate around the Torus in Viewer of 'cam1' and you'll see the Torus will re-orient itself to face the camera.

label: Tristrip
text: The Tristrip SOP convert geometry into triangle strips. Triangle strips are faster to render than regular triangles or quads.

label: Tube
text: The Tube SOP generates open or closed tubes, cones, or pyramids along the X, Y or Z axes. It outputs as meshes, polygons or simply a tube Primitive.

label: Twist
text: You can change the axis that the Twist SOP is applied to.

label: Twist
text: The Twist SOP is capable of a couple of different  operations.On the right you'll see some examples of them, including bending, shearing, tapering, and squashing.

label: Vertex
text: Use the Vextex SOp to manipulate attributes per vertex. In this example a new crease weight is written to each vertex, depending on the y position of that vertex.

label: Vertex
text: Use the Vextex SOp to manipulate attributes per vertex. In this example the vextexes of each visible faces of a cube have been grouped together (by the Group SOPs) and then each group of vertexes gets an seperate animated texture W coordinate. This lets you apply different slices of the 2dTextureArray to different polygons of the same model.

label: Wireframe
text: You can use the Wireframe SOP to create wireframes of the input geometry. This example features rounded corners and a thick wire radius.

label: Add
text: The Add TOP can take 2 inputs and add their color values together.

label: Add
text: This example performs basic transform operations on the incoming signals.

label: Add
text: A little-known occasionally-useful feature in TOPs is the Passes parameter that repeats an operation multiple times, in this case a repeated scale/rotate on a square.You can also uses Passes to act as a Bypass by setting it to 0 or 1 based on an expression or an export.

label: Analyze
text: Analyze TOPs get the darkest pixel and the  brightest pixel of the muddy "source" images. This network adjusts levels so the image range is 0 to 1 with no channels over-driven.Note that Analyze by default creates an image with 1 pixel.It might be better to work the TOPs in 12 or 16-bits per pixel because precision is lost at 8 bits per pixel channel.This technique requires you to go back to the  CPU to drive the Level TOP's parameters. You can switch the TOP to CHOP to Next Frame.Note: This technique may slightly change saturation of colors. Luma Level TOP may be used similarly to preserve saturation.

label: Analyze
text: These Analyze TOPs analyze each row or column and puts the results in a 1-pixel wide strip.The Resoltion TOPs are not changing the pixel resolution. They are changing only the image aspect ratio, so the  pixels are stretched.

label: Analyze
text: Analyze the maximum and minimum range of a TOP rgba channels.Note the Analyze TOPs' Analyze Channel parameter are set to RGBA Independent so the max / min are calculated separately for each channel.  The default Analyze Channel is set to Luminance, which finds the brightest pixel and uses the RGBA of that pixel.The nodes inside analyzeRange will not cook (consume cpu/gputime) if its viewer is turned off or you are in another network AND its output CHOP is not used.

label: Anti Alias
text: The Anti Alias TOP is used to smooth out rough edges on diagonal lines.It works by performing an edge detection (which can be manipulated by changing the Quality parameter to Custom) then it uses that information to blur the edges.

label: Blob Track
text: The Blob Track TOP can be used to calculate positions of blobs in a texture.One practical use case would be to calculate touch point positions from an optically captured touch surface.The Info DAT is used to extract the values from the Blob Track TOP.For more information on the parameters please see the WIKI.

label: Blob Track
text: Here you can see the behavior and limits of the blob tracker. The Resolution TOP scales to quarter-resolution in X and Y so the Blob Track TOP has 1/16 the number of pixels to go through.The Monochrome TOP does nothing practical as Blob Track is converting it to monochrome anyway.Bob Track does best with maximum-contrast images: Bypass the Luma Level to see.If you drop Minimum Move Distance down too much, it will think all blobs are new each frame, so the "id" channel will rise quickly for what seems like the same blob.It's using square areas, so it is not sensitive to odd shapes or optimized for circular shapes.triangles are rotated based on blob id.

label: Blob Track
text: This uses the new time counters in the blob table that it outputs, and in the new callbacks.This handles blobs that come and go from visibility. Look at blobtrack1_blobs.Blobs can be lost for some amount of time and then revived, but after the Revive Time, they are expired forever.You can keep the blobs in the table for some time after they are expired.The lost_time timer and expired_time lets you do things with the blob while it is lost or expired.Also the callbacks are for state changes in the blobs, or just a list of the current blobs.

label: Bloom
text: It's easier to understand what each of the parameters do in isolation.Turn on the bullseye on the parameter dialog to see the pars that are not at their default values.The bloomA set illustrates a first stage where you would adjust the Pre-process parameters to catch a good "signal" in the incoming image, just the right amount of the incoming image toapply a bloomot. For darker images you nees to reduce Pre-Black Level.  Pre-Gamma and Pre-Brighness accentuates what yuu have caught.Bloom B abnd C extract less of the input ignal, but shiow how to get a wider diffuse bloom.Bloom D binds together the pre-process parameters.Bloom everywhere by making Bloom Threshold 0, or below 0 to crank it up more.

label: Bloom
text: When high quality gain is ON, brightness gain happens in a separate pass, *before* mip levels are generated. If you have large, bright blobs in your image, you will get roughly the same bloom effect. If high quality gain is OFF, the brightness gain is applied *after* mip levels are generated, which can have the effect of suppressed bloom originating from small, bright blobs.In general, if you can afford the small performance cost of applying gain in a separate pass, you should use the high quality gain.

label: Bloom
text: If you want to do your own Bloom pre-processing, isolate your signal yourself (for example, chroma1 and level1 isolate and boost the reds un-naturally).  Feed that isolated / enhanced color into Bloom, then reduce Pre-Black Level to 0 to remove the effect of the built-in Pre-Processing. Adjust the Radius etc parameter to get a desired glow, and then set the Output to be simply Bloom with Alpha.Then you can composite bloom1 with the original image any way you want...   Aside from using an Add TOP, other modes in the Composite TOP are alternatives, like Screen.The bottom part of the network takes the bloom effect alone, and post-processes it before adding to the original.

label: Blur
text: The simplest blur with the Filter Size parameter.

label: Blur
text: A faster and more extreme blur by shrinking the image and blurring that.It internally scales it up again.

label: Blur
text: Assume you don't know what the incoming resolution is, but you know you want to blur 10% of the width of whatever image comes in. The Filter Size parameter is expressed in pixels and will be the horizontal resolution * .1.Try connecting butterfly128 to blurFractiom.

label: Blur
text: The sample step controls the direction that the blur is applied.

label: Blur
text: In this example the direction of the blur is rotated.

label: Blur
text: The Pre-Shrink and Filter size control the amount and quality of the blur.  Pre-shrink of 2 or 4 etc will first reduce the image resolution, then the blur is appled to the smaller image, and then it is scaled up. This is less accurate but much faster.

label: Blur
text: You can control  which channel the blur will affect on the Common page.

label: Blur
text: You can use a  Blur TOP  to dither an incoming signal. This is helpful when working with low quality assets where there is color banding.

label: Blur
text: Generates a softened alpha channel from the alpha of the original image.blur1 determines how far to cut into the edge.Near the edge, blurred alpha is about .5, so lookup eliminates alpha outside the border where alpha < .5.lookup1 TOP is set to display alpha channel. In the Inside TOP, this alpha/Input 2 is used to determine what parts of the  Input1 image are visible.

label: Blur
text: Threhhold with a little softness.  Could use Luma Level instead with Black Level raised.Blur pre-shrink to spread out more with lower GPU cost. 16 bit on Pixel Format to avoid low-level stepping.Luma Level vs Level to heep sue-saturation of glow closer to original.  Could use a little Gamma vs increasing Brightness to avoid clipping at 1.Screen in Composite to avoid the clamping that Level would give.

label: Cache
text: The Cache TOP can be used to simply delay images.Here the Cache Size is set to 11 to hold the current image and the image 10 cooks ago.The time-delay is not exact because it caches one image per timeline cook, so if you are running about 22 frames per second, it will be a delay of 10/22 seconds. The Output Index is usually 0 or a negative number, like -10, as you think of the output image being from 10 frames ago.

label: Cache
text: You can pre-fill all the frames of a Movie File In TOP into a Cache TOP and then scrub through them.The prefill needs to capture one image of the movie per timeline frame. Here this is done by making the movie play one index per frame, starting at the first frame of then movie (index 0).You need to use me.time.frame to control what goes into the cache for a certain index. me.time.fame is the local timeline frame number (not absTime.frame):The prefill is done by secretly moving the frame to 1 and stepping forward one frame per cache image. See the Index parameter of the Movie File In TOP.The Transform TOP also uses me.time.frame to zoom up from about 20%.

label: Cache
text: Two Cache Select TOPs selecting from one Cache TOP.You can pre-fill all the frames of a Movie File In TOP into a Cache TOP and then scrub through them using two Cache Selext TOPs.

label: Cache
text: This example uses 2 Cache TOPs to create trails of the original image. By setting the Output Index, you can adjust how close to the current frame the Cache TOPs output is.

label: Cache
text: This simply averages the last 4 frames, like a multi-exposure.Try Composite TOP Operations Maximum.This can be made more controllable by adding Level TOPs after each Cache Select TOP and adjust their relative Brightness (like .5, .33, .25, .2), then Add-ing them in comp1. This would make the most recent frame more visible.

label: Cache
text: work-in-progress. pixel > 0 if surface is coming toward camera.

label: Cache Select
text: The Cache Select TOP allows you to extract a frame from a Cache TOP. First you need, on the Cache TOP, to turn Active on for a few seconds to fill the cache. Then the Cache TOP has enough frames to fetch with the Cache Select TOP.In this case we can use the slider to scrub back through time and off-set the Cache TOP relative to the total number of frames in the original movie. Pausing the time line and using the slider to scrub backwards through time will help to illustrate how this works.Note that you can have several Cache Select TOPs retrieving from one Cache TOP.Matthew Ragan8/13/14

label: Channel Mix
text: R = G + B G = R + B B = R + G

label: Channel Mix
text: This example adds all the incoming RGB channels into the Red channel, (clanping at a sum of 1 if the pixels are 0-1 value, i.e. not floating point numbers that go beow 0 or above 1.)Since the green strip is low-brightness, there is a dark strip in the output.chanmix_allRed1 assures all color does not clamp at 1.

label: Channel Mix
topic: push green into red and blue
text: The adding of the Red and Blue channels into each others channels creates purple. Green is 0.Connect moviefilein1 to chanmix_push_green_to_redblue.

label: Channel Mix
text: In this example we subtract the alpha from Green and Blue channels, leaving only Red.The Level TOP can produce similar effects, but cannot mix channels.

label: Channel Mix
text: In this example we subtracted white from the signal, leaving black.R = R - 1 is always <1, which clamps at black for 8-bit color.But if the pixels are floating point, they have values outside to 0-1 range.

label: Channel Mix
text: This time the ramp is created as  floating point pixels, as you see on the Common page.That means pixels can go below zero, though they will look black when displayed on a monitor, as you see in chanmix_subtract_white1.But when you add white back in, you get the original colors back, since they are in the range 0 to 1 again.If it's done as 8-bit, then chanmix_subtract_white2 clamps at 0 and adding white gives white.The Analyze TOP and TOP to CHOP show the minimum values in a pixel, which are below 0 in the 16-bit floating point image.

label: Channel Mix
text: Here we force alpha to be 1 by adding 1 to the alpha channel.

label: CHOP to
text: This example uses the RGBA values from topto1 to  create a pixel.

label: CHOP to
text: This CHOP to creates a pixel for every sample of noise.

label: CHOP to
text: Just to show a variety of data formats and conversions, we go from CHOP to DAT to CHOP and then to TOPs. The CHOP to TOP has the ability to arrange the pixels in a square (not a long row), easier to manage and visualize as point cloud data.Because there will be some unused pixels, you can fill the unused pixels at the top with a special value (0, 0, 0, -1) in this case. You have to set the pixel format to 32-bit float so you can represent negative and positive numbers of any value. By default it puts (0, 0, 0, 0) in the unused pixel.geo1 received the point cloud from null1 and because the Active parameter is 'a', the alpha channel of null1, the pixels with 0 or less in alpha will not be rendered.

label: Chroma Key
text: The Chroma Key TOP can be used to extract parts of an image based on Hue, Saturation, and Value.One common application is to extract people and objects from a green screen.The Soft Low and Soft High parameters can be used to feather the edge.Check out the chromaKey component in the Pallete for a well-featured use case.

label: Circle
text: Circle as a rim and a bar.

label: Circle
text: Circle used as a triangle indicator.

label: Composite
text: After you look at the examples here, get the example Dialogs -> Tools called blendModes.https://docs.derivative.ca/Palette:blendModes

label: Composite
text: There are many different types of compositing algorithms available. Here are a few examples.

label: Composite
text: The Composite TOP can take many inputs and composite them simultaneously.But if the operation is the same, it's better to use pattern matching.

label: Composite
text: This example  performs basic  transform operations on the incoming signals.

label: Composite
text: This example uses the Translate Step parameter to animate inputs  3 and 4.  First input is fixed/static, that is it is not translated. Also it means the output resolution is that of the first input. The next input steps further than the previous.

label: Composite
text: more examples

label: Constant
text: Composite  a solid color with an image, and play with the different compositing operations, like Burn Linear in this case.

label: Convolve
text: The Convolve TOP uses a DAT table containing numeric coefficients. For each pixel, it combines its RGBA values and it's neighboring pixels' RGBA values by multiplying the values by the corresponding coefficients in the table, adding the results together. For example, if the table is 5 rows and 5 columns, the pixel and its 24 neighbors are combined.

label: Corner Pin
text: The Corner Pin TOP allows you to move the corner points of an image in x and y.NOTE: First there is an Extract page that has similar  parameters, but it cuts the image out before applying the corner pinning on the Pin page. Extract examples below.

label: Corner Pin
text: You can apply a background color after you have corner pinned an image.

label: Corner Pin
text: The Extract page allows you to specify a sub-section of the image to use by moving 4 points.It is different than cropping in that it keeps the original signal's resolution.The last example forces the output resolution to be square.

label: CPlusPlus
text: See samples in the GitHub repository:https://github.com/TouchDesigner/CustomOperatorSamples

label: Crop
text: The Crop TOP actually crops out the pixels in an image, whereas the Corner Pin TOP's "Extract" function keeps your image size intact while isolating a specific part of the image.

label: Crop
text: These two examples show how to crop a region of an image.

label: Crop
text: The extend conditions are used in the off-chance that pixels are added to an image after a crop operation.In these examples, the crop is outside the image 0-1 range.

label: Cross
text: The Cross TOP crossfades between its two inputs.

label: Cross
text: The Transform page of the Cross TOP allows you to do basic transform operations on the inputs, thus saving you from having to use a separate Transform TOP afterwards. The Fixed Layer is Input 1 so the transform is only applied to Input 2, and the output resolution is Input 1, the Fixed Layer

label: Cube Map
text: The Cube Map TOP builds a texture map in the Cube Map internal texture format. It accepts a vertical cross image, or 1 input per side of the cube. The Phong MAT can use the cube maps from the Cube Map TOP for reflections.See also the Projection TOP and the Render TOP.

label: Depth
text: The Depth TOP reads an image containing depth information from a scene described in a specified Render TOP. The resulting image is black (0) at pixels where the surface is at the near depth value (Camera's parameter "Near"). It is white (1) at pixels where the surface is at the far depth value (parameter "Far").The Depth TOP is used to do shadow mapping. It can have many other uses also, such as edge detection based on depth.The depth range is by definition the near plane -> far plane. Values in the Depth TOP will be 0 at the near plane, and 1 at the far plane. Generally the image in the Depth TOP will be white, unless you have your planes really tight around your object. But just because you can't visually see anything, it doesn't mean the information isn't there.Another option is Linear Camera-Space Depth. If the Render TOP's Depth Buffer Format is set to 32-Bit Floating-Point and its Linear Camera-Space Depth parameter is on, then the Depth TOP will output linear camera space depth.You can use the Level TOP to re-range the Depth TOP's values.
