However make sure you set the Pixel Format of the Level TOP to 16 or 32-bit, or you'll lose a lot of information from the Depth TOP's data (The Depth TOP has 24-bit data).The Depth TOP creates a 24-bit fixed-point or 32-bit floating-point single channel image. When the Depth TOP is used as an input to another TOP, its data will be treated like an RGBA value of (D, D, D, 1). The same is true when sampling a depth texture in a GLSL shader.

label: Difference
text: The Difference TOP computes the difference between two inputs. In this case we're looking at the difference between a Movie File In TOP and a Cache TOP that is set one frame behind the Movie File In. This allows us to track the change from one frame to another. This can be useful very when analyzing motion. If you have a camera available, you can use the button provided to look at the difference in frame to frame coming from your camera.Matthew Ragan8/13/14Often it's better to "cache back" 2 frames to get a good solid difference. (gh)

label: DirectX Out
text: Note: The DirectX In/Out TOPs are only available on Windows in TouchDesigner Educational, TouchDesigner Commercial and TouchDesigner Pro. Therefore in this snippet these nodes are currently bypassed to prevent errors for TouchDesigner Non-Commercial licenses, toggle their bypass icon to enable them.  The DirectX Out TOP creates textures that a DirectX application can access, or any instance of TouchDesigner with a DirectX In TOP. This features is only available on Windows 7 or greater, accessed though the DirectX Sharing Resources feature. This supports DirectX 9.0 and higher. The DirectX In TOP requires a "handle" pointing to the DirectX texture. You can get the handle of any DirectX Out TOP texture by attaching an Info DAT to it. You can use a Touch Out DAT to send the handle to another TouchDesigner  instance.

label: Displace
text: For every output pixel, the Displace TOP fetches from nearby the corresponding pixel in the first input image.The second image is used to warp the first image. If a pixel of the second input is grey (.5 in red and .5 in blue), the Displace TOP will get its output pixel from the pixel at the same location in the input image - no distortion.This looks boring to start, but move the sliders. The more you move slider_red from .5, the farther displace1 will get its pixels from. Similar for circle_blue.In the second case, noise1's red and blue go to extreme low and high values relative to .5.  But if you reduce the parameter Displace Weight down to around .1 or less, it will reduce the warping effect. and you can adjust both those parameters separately to warp horizontally or vertically separately.Then bring down UV Weight to get from pixels near the center of moviefilein2.

label: Displace
text: For every output pixel, it fetches from nearby the corresponding input pixel. Changing the Displace TOPs Displace Weight allows you to control how heavily the input is displaced in each direction.Note that some of the noise values go outside the 0-1 range that the colors are clamped to at 8-bits fixed point. But because CHOP to TOP is 32-bit float, the values are not clamped.chop_to_top is one pixel high, so one of its pixels will affect all pixels in a column of the eutput.The Green channel affects the vertical displacement via the Vertical Source parameter.

label: Displace
text: For every output pixel, it fetches from nearby the corresponding input pixel. Changing the Displace TOPs Displace Weight allows you to control how heavily the input is displaced in each direction.

label: Displace
text: The pixels of smiley.tif are being displaced on the X axis by blue channel of the 2nd input noise.

label: Displace
text: This example gets its pixels from random shifts in location in the input image.Red channels of the second input (the "Displace Input") determine the shift horizontally and blue channels determine the shift vertically.For each a pixel of the Displace Image, if its red is less than .5, it get a pixel from the left of the corresponding Source Image pixel. If its blue is more than .5, it get a pixel from above the corresponding Source Image pixel.Why .5? Because the Source Midpoint is set to .5 .5.Also, noise_displacer gets its resolution from moviein_displace.

label: Displace
text: This example does the same thing as the previous example but the slider adjusts the contrast of the Displace image to grey, versus the previous example where the slider adjusts the Displace Weight parameter.

label: Edge
text: The Edge TOP finds edges in an image and highlights them. For each pixel, it looks at the values at neighboring pixels, and where differences are greater than a threshold, the output's value is higher.

label: Edge
text: If you fancy yourself a mad lad, try putting an Edge Detect TOP in a feedback loop.#facemelt

label: Emboss
text: The Emboss TOP creates the effect that an image is embossed in a thin sheet of metal. Edges in the image will appear raised.

label: Feedback
text: A simple feedback setup that gives fake motion blur by using a fraction (x) of the current image (transform1) plus 1-x of the prior frame's image. With the Cross TOP, this has limited blending control.

label: Feedback
text: This takes the current image combined with a fraction (reduce_opacity) of the prior image.You can try different Composite blend modes to control the mixing, and try both rectangle1 and fit1 into rotate:  Screen - tame, versatile, doesn't burn out  Add - more dynamic  Maximum - punchy  Over - will always show source but needs alpha  Difference - psychoticIf you change Source's resolution, change res on constant1 and press Reset on feedback1. Or connect your source into feedback1 and press Reset tomake it your starting image

label: Feedback
text: Feedback with more effects. alter nodes: opacity: Opacity - fade out traillumalevel1: boost Gamma or BrightnessreduceHighRed - isolate colorshavadjust1 - Hue Offset, boost Saturationtransform1 - Scale, Translate - expanddisplace1 - un-bypass, Displace Weightnoise1 blur1 - Filter Size to softenedge1 - unbypass, Strength, Edge Colorcomp1 - different blend modeslevel2 - fill with black (Low A)Change feedback1 Pixel Format to 16 bit fixed (and press Reset) for better color quantization, or to 32-bit float for better over-driving.

label: Fit
text: The Fit TOP changes the resolution and aspect ratio of an image, but does not distort the image.In the first example, the new image in fit1 shows the entire horizontal span of the input image.In fit2, it shows the entire vertical span but the sides are cut off.

label: Fit
text: To insure the entire image is visible, no matter what the  input and output resolutions are, use Fit Inside.Secondy, to insure the output image uses as much of of the input image as possible, without distorting and without any blank areas, use Fit Outside.This example also scales an extra 1%.

label: Fit
text: A square image is made to fit 16x9.

label: Flip
text: The Flip TOP provides an easy way to flip an image in X (horizontally) or y (vertically).Flip X and Y together is same as rotating the image 180 degrees.

label: Flip
text: A useful feature of the Flip TOP is being able to "flop" an image. X becomes Y and Y becomes X.This differs from using a Transform TOPs rotation, which rotates the texture contained in the image or movie, but not the canvas.There are 2 ways to rotate the whole image 90 degrees.Flop changes the resolution.

label: Function
text: ramp1 is set on Common page to output 32-bit floating point numbers as Pixel Format for the RGBA channelsnull4 is same as ramp1 with its viewer set to Normalized Split, where the red (R), green (G) and blue 9B) channels are identical 0-1 ramps.math1 takes the ramp's 0-1 range and makes it 0 to 1000.Then the Function TOP computes:sqrt(0) to sqrt(1000) in the red channel.cos(0) to cos(1000) is in green.0**2 to 1000**2 goes into blue.The bananna is warped in the red and green channels, which is interpreted in a point cloud as X and Y.

label: GLSL
text: The GLSL TOP renders a GLSL shader into a TOP image. Use the Info DAT to check for compile errors in your shaders.Refer to the Write a GLSL TOP article for more info on using this TOP.http://www.derivative.ca/wiki099/index.php?title=Write_a_GLSL_TOP

label: GLSL Multi
text: The GLSL Multi TOP renders a GLSL shader into a TOP image. Its parameters and functionality are identical to the GLSL TOP, except it allows for more than 3 inputs.In this example 9 TOP inputs are read and placed into a grid.Experiment with the values of the uGridSize uniform on the GLSL TOP's parameter window (the Vector page).

label: HSV Adjust
text: The HSV Adjust TOP adjusts color values using hue, saturation, and value controls. This snippet shows one can narrow the the range of pixels to modify, based on their hue, saturation and value. To adjust only the color of the two red oildrums in the input image:1. Add a Difference TOP to easily see what the result of each action is2. Pick the color in the input image we want to modify. For this we crop the image and use an Analyze TOP to extract the color of the right red oildrum. We use this RGB value for the Start Color parameter of the HSV Adjust TOP. This the hue that the HSV adjustment is centered around. When adjusting a small hue range, this is the color that will be altered.2. Limit the Hue Range. This is the range of color from the Start Color that will be adjusted. A range of 1 will only adjust colors that are the same as the start color.3. Adjust the Hue Offset, watch the Difference TOP, and first adjust the Hue Range, then the Saturation Range, then the Value Range until you only see a change in the desired color areas. 4. Then finetune with the Hue Falloff, Saturation Falloff, Value Falloff. Higher values give more falloff, blending the range softly from hues/saturations/values that are adjusted to ones are not.

label: HSV Adjust
text: The HSV Adjust TOP adjusts color values using hue, saturation, and value controls. This snippet shows one can narrow the the range of pixels to modify, based on their hue, saturation and value. To adjust only the color of the red flower in the input image:1. Add a Difference TOP to easily see what the result of each action is2. Pick the color in the input image we want to modify. For this we crop the image and use an Analyze TOP to extract the color of the red flower. We use this RGB value for the Start Color parameter of the HSV Adjust TOP. This the hue that the HSV adjustment is centered around. When adjusting a small hue range, this is the color that will be altered.2. Limit the Hue Range. This is the range of color from the Start Color that will be adjusted. A range of 1 will only adjust colors that are the same as the start color.3. Adjust the Hue Offset, watch the Difference TOP, and first adjust the Hue Range, then the Saturation Range, then the Value Range until you only see a change in the desired color areas. 4. Then finetune with the Hue Falloff, Saturation Falloff, Value Falloff. Higher values give more falloff, blending the range softly from hues/saturations/values that are adjusted to ones are not.

label: HSV to RGB
text: Put hue in the first channel, saturation in the second channel, value in the third channel, and use HSV to RGB to interpret those three channels as HSV and convert the image to RGB.In this case, hue is a ramp from 0 to 1. In TouchDesigner, a hue of value 0 is red, .33 is green, .66 is blue and 1 is red again.

label: In
text: The In TOP is used to create a TOP input in a Component. Component inputs are positioned alphanumerically on the left side of the Component.In this example, base1 COMP has a purple TOP input connector on its left side. Zoom inside base1 to see the In TOP, and notice that the In TOP has the moviefilein_fallback_data TOP acting as fallback data connected to the In TOP's second input. This will be output from the In TOP if nothing is connected to the first input of base1.Zoom out of base1 and connect moviefilein_new_input to base1, and see the output change.Also, when you rollover the input connector, you see a custom label, "Nice Picture", set with the In TOP's Label parameter.  Similarly for the Out TOP out1.

label: Inside
text: The Inside TOP places Input1 "inside" Input2. The alpha of Input2 is used to determine what parts of the Input1 image are visible.See also the blendModes component in the palette.Idzard Kwadijkkwadijk@idzard.com hh

label: Layout
text: These examples illustrate how to use the Layout TOP.In the 4th example, you can specify paths for TOPs on the parameter dialog without connecting them.

label: Layout
text: The Layout TOP positions multiple input TOPs into rows, columns, or grids.It can either fit all the TOPs into a specific resolution (determined by the Common page, default from its first input), by scaling the inputs.Or it can scale its own resolution to be larger to accomodate the native resolution of all the inputs (layout2).layout3 uses its default resolution (resolution of its first input), but leaves unused pixels on bottom and top.layout4 has a custom resolution on the Common page which leaves some unused pixels.layout5 is the same as layout2 but it's Output Resolution on Common is set to 2X, which doubles what it otherwise be.Idzard Kwadijkkwadijk@idzard.com

label: Lens Distort
text: See Lens Distort TOP wiki for the meaning of the constants.lensdistort1 will distort a renderd image to match a camera. lensdistort2 will undistort a camera image as it has Invert Distortion on.lensdistort3 has its Invert Distort parameter on, so it will approximate the un-distortion using the same parameters because each distortion parameter is bound to the same parameter of the other TOP, so you can see the limits of distorting and undistorting. For one, you can't un-distort pixels that have been distorted off-screen.

label: Level
text: Black level, brightness and gamma adjustment.This operates on each red, gree, blue separately.Refer to the Luma Level TOP too, which will treat the 3 channels together as luminance adjustments, which gives better results sometimes as it maintains hue and saturation.

label: Level
text: This Level TOP inverts the colors of the incoming image.

label: Level
text: You can control an image's opacity with the Level TOP.

label: Level
text: Level TOP can adjust the upper-lower range of each RG and B. This is a different set of controls vs HSV Adjust TOP.

label: Level
text: The Level TOP can modify the incoming and outgoing High and Low values of each pixel.See the Math TOP to handle data outside the 0-1 range more generally.

label: Level
text: The Level TOP can also perform color correction operations by controlling the Low and High values of the Red, Green, Blue, and Alpha channels independantly.

label: Level
text: This shows the application of stepping to a smooth ramp.

label: Level
text: Sometimes you want to adjust gamma first and then the brightness. Use the Post page for this.You can also clamp the colors to remain in a certain range.

label: Limit
text: limit1 sets a minimum of .25 and a maximum of .7 on all channels.That includes the alpha channel, which may not be desired. limit2 affects only the red and green channel, using the Channel Mask on the Common page.limit3 clamps like limit1, but then Normalizes the result to 0 to 1, and affects RGB only.topto1 to 5 have their graphs fixed to the 0 to 1 range: Make their View Active, right-click on the graph, select Vertical Range, 0 1.ramp2 is not linear between black and white: Its interpolation is set to Ease In Ease Out (an S-curve).This helps show the difference between limit4 which Quantizes by value, and limit5 which quantizes by position (equal steps in the horizontal axis).

label: Limit
text: See RGB to HSV TOP example 2.See also the Limit CHOP.

label: Lookup
text: Compose 4 CHOP channels and apply the first CHOP channel to the red TOP channel, and so on.Unbypass Quantize for a variation.

label: Lookup
text: From the "source image" (the first input), you can use different color channels (or combinations of the color channels) as an index into the color ramp.Try changing the Index Channel parameter to all the variations in the menu and understand why you get what you see.A 0 value of the index gets the color on the left of the ramp, and an index value of 1 gets it from the right.Note about Independent Alpha: By default the chosen index looks up all four channels from the lookup table image. If the Independent Alpha parameter is On, it does the alpha lookup independently: using the first input's alpha channel as the index, it gets the value from lookup image's alpha channel to output as the alpha. (this was legacy behavior)

label: Lookup
text: The lookup1 image is tinted green to yellow because the Lookup CHOP needs a 1-dimensional line of pixels as a lookup, so it needs to choose what part of ramp2 to use. The Lookup TOP use its Dark UV and Light UV parameters to extract a line of pixels from ramp2, and here it is 0,0 to 1,0: the bottom row of pixels. Change it to 0,1 and 1,1 to get the rop row of pixels. Or any 2 sets of UV points in ramp2.lookup2 extracts a small horizontal section of the noise image.

label: Lookup
text: plug-and-play, this does the simplest tinting based on source image luminance. You choose 2 or 3 colors. Then With 2 Bias sliders you adjust how much of each color you use.

label: Lookup
text: Because the ramps are by default 8-bit, you may get un-desired banding in your output that can be eliminted.In circle, ramp1, lookup1, they are all 8-bit and if you zoom into topto_lookup1 you see how the pixels hold value for 8 pixels and then step up - a bad case.In circle2_32bit, ramp2, lookup2, the circle2_32bit TOP is 32-bit floats. topto_circle2 shows it is nice and smoothly generated, but lookup2 still steps because its ramp is qunatized at 1/255 steps, as you see in topto_lookup2.circle3_32bit and ramp2_rebit are all 32-bit floats, and therefore lookup3 is 32-bit, and all the interpolations are smooth.Instead of 32-bit floats, you can use any other pixel formats that are higher-qulity than 8-bit.

label: Luma Blur
text: These examples show the difference between using a white filter or black filter. See Black Filter Width and White Filter Width parameters.They also have different blur Types applied.

label: Luma Blur
text: This example uses the black and white Count.mov to blur the grid.

label: Luma Level
text: The Luma Level TOP is useful in situations where you don't want colors getting blown out by a Level TOPs contrast parameters.

label: Luma Level
text: You can apply luminance stepping using a Luma Level TOP.

label: Math
text: The Math TOP can help turn images that  have their RGB pre-multiplied by alpha into an image that does not have the RGB pre-multiplied by alpha. Essentially it divides the RGB by alpha, but it needs to take care of the case where RGB and alpha are very small as divide and round-off errors will give imprecise results.Level helps strip out pixels that have alpha < .04.

label: Math
text: Show high pixel value ranges being brought into "visible range" (0-1). Noise in set to be in range 5 to 6. Math brings 5 to 6 down to 0 to 1 for red and blue, and .3 to .7 for green.  Alpha remains un-affected.math1 has its Viewer Smoothness set to Nearest Pixel so you can better see the individual pixels.null1's viewer is set to Normalized Split so ou can see the ranges there.

label: Math
text: Show high pixel value ranges being brought into "visible range" (0-1).math1 sets the From Ramge max to be whatever is is in the jellybean image, and sets the To range to 0 to 1. Note the analyze_independent's Analyze Channel parameter is set to RGBA Independent so the max / min are calculated separately for each channel.  (The default Analyze Channel is set to Luminance, which finds the brightest pixel and uses the RGBA of that pixel.)More easily, the Limit TOP can normalize the colors to 0-1 in R G B and A.

label: Matte
text: This image in input1 is revealed by the luminance of the image in the 3rd input.

label: Matte
text: In this case, the Alpha of Ramp1/3rd input controls the opacity of input 2. By default it uses the luminance of input 3.

label: Mirror
text: Pivot and Rotate defines a line that crosses through the input image. On one side of the line the image is untouched. The other side is reflection of the untouched side.

label: Mirror
text: Appearance of more arbitrary sections being transformed separately.

label: Mirror
text: The Mirror TOP mirrors part of an image on top of itself.

label: Monochrome
text: mono1 is the normal creation of monchrome images based on the response of the eye, which is a blend of .298 red + .6 green + .102 blue.In the RGB menu, which appears in a lot of operators in TOPs, Luminance is defined to be this mix.However sometimes you just want the largest component, so in the RGB menu, select RGB Maximum.For other blends, see the Channel Mix TOP, and the Reorder TOP.

label: Movie File In
text: This movie's trim value will keep it looping forever. Its Extend Right parameter is set to Cycle.Extend Right refers to what the TOP will do once it reaches the end of end movie.The second movie's trim value will hold the last frame.Its Extend Right parameter is set to Hold.The Cue button will force the movie to jump to image 20.

label: Movie File In
text: Use the slider to scrub through the movie by index

label: Movie File In
text: Use this button to play the movie. The button state has been exported to the Play parameter.

label: Movie File In
text: This movie's playback is locked to the main TouchDesigner Timeline. Its Play Mode Parameter is set to Locked to Timeline.Scrub the timeline below to scrub the movie.Using the timeline to play movies is uncommon on TouchDesigner these days. Usually Index and Sequential modes are used.

label: Movie File In
text: Use this button to reload the movie in case it has changed on the filesystem.You can use the Folder DAT to do it automatically.

label: Movie File In
text: Movie File In TOPs also support loading still images and image file sequences.For image sequences you give it the folder and it will alphanumerically-order the files in that folder.Or alternately you control it via the filename, which may load slower because the expression cannot be predicted or interpolated.Search "File Formats" on wiki. Seer also Point File In TOP to read point clouds.

label: Movie File In
text: Try the moviePlayer COMP in the palette for a more full-functioned player and read its Help wiki page.Also it can spit out movieEngine, which is moviePlayer without a UI.

label: Movie File In
text: The movieLoader is an example implementation of using the Movie File In TOP's preload and unload feature with a Timer CHOP.The setup consists of two timers where one is responsible for preloading and the other for playback of the specified Movie File In TOP.The preloading is done in the timer_preload Timer CHOP preloading in the onInitialize() callback. When initializing, the extension of the component first attempts to open the movie and then proceeds to preload it. Once preloaded the timer_preload Timer CHOP starts the timer_play Timer CHOP to playback the movie. If the option is enabled, the timer_play Timer CHOP will unload the Movie File In TOP once playback has concluded.This component can preload its internal default Movie File In TOP or you can specify external Movie File In TOPs as well as use its functionality by calling the component's Preload() function.The first output of the component is the movie being played while the second output is channels to use as exports to the external Movie File In TOP being used for playback. Use the CHOP's "Channel Name is Path:Parameter" Export Method.

label: Movie File In
text: This crossfades and switches a list of movies without a frame drop. It randomizes the order of movies every cycle through all movies.You give it a list of movie paths in a DAT, choose a crossfade time in "spec", and press Start on either of the movieEngines.It uses movieEngine, which is moviePlayer without a UI. They have been modded slightly as documented internally in purple. The callbacks it uses is engine_callbacks here.movieEngine uses mvspec components which define what to play and controls image appearance.comp1 adds the images, and forces the resolution to be constant.

label: Movie File Out
text: This is a typical Movie Out setup with audio recording synced to an audio track.The Record Movie button turns Record on and off, as well as triggers a counter to give each new recording a unique name.The Pause button pauses the movie recording while it is on.Audio is being referenced from audiofilein1.The movie is created in your project folder where your .toe is located.Press Reset pulse on the incrementFilename Count CHOP to reset the sequence number to 1.

label: Movie File Out
text: This creates a sequence of files, one file per frame.The Record Movie button turns Record on and off, as well as triggers a counter to give each new recording sequence a unique name.In the File parameter, me.fileSuffix uses artifishull intelligence to build the end of the file path, something like  .2.14,jpgThe movie is created on the Desktop using the hard-to-find python: app.desktopFolder.  See the App class for more secrets. docs.derivative.ca/App_Class

label: Movie File Out
text: The Movie Out TOP can also output still images in a similar manner as video  files.

label: Movie File Out
text: If you turn on Unique Suffix, each time you click Record on and off you get a uniquely-named file.

label: Multiply
text: The multiply TOP performs a multiply operation:input1.rgba * input2.rgbaIn multiply1 it is set to use to output the resolution of the first input. By default it outputs the resolution of the second input, like other 2-input combiners.

label: NDI Out
text: NDI frames can also carry metadata. If you pause the timeline you can see the NDI receiver gets the metata guarantueed synced to that same frame.

label: NDI Out
text: NDI can send & receive audio, drag the CHOP containing audio to the Audio CHOP parameter of the NDI Out.You can extract audio from the NDI In TOP using the NDI Audio CHOP

label: Noise
text: The Noise TOP's Seed parameter is an easy way to create animated noise. The two images are multiplied together in the Composite TOP.The Noise TOP is scaled to fit the jellybeans, so you would have to set the resolution of the Noise TOP too to make it not stretched.The second example shows you can feed an image to the Noise TOP, and via the Output page, combine the noise and incoming image in different ways.The Noise TOP takes the resolution of the incoming TOP.

label: Noise
text: You can get very different types of noise, from Random to Perlin, by changing the  Type parameter.

label: Noise
text: Shifts a random pattern around to give an apparent randomness every frame. Not perfect but may be good enough for many purposes. Much faster than generating perfectly random pixels every frame.Second example randomly rotates also, making it more scrambled though some adjacent pixels may have similar values.

label: Noise
text: The Noise TOP takes its input, interprets each pixel RGB value as an XYZ point, and for each point, outputs a pixel whose RGB values are the values of the noise space at that point in space.In the first example, chopto1 outputs 32-bit floating point pixels which can represent numbers outside the usual 0-1 color range of 8-bit fixed point pixels.The Noise TOP has been changed to a 32-bit float format so that it can output noise values that are outside the 0-1 range.In the first example, the Noise TOP's RGB values are used to color the points, but they can be used for anything.In the second example, the Ramp and Constant TOPs are set to 32-bit and combined and fed into the Noise TOP. The sliders offset the XYZ positions used to sample the noise space.

label: Noise
text: Try the "noise" component in the palette for more choices of noise types.

label: Normal Map
text: Matthew Ragan  8/11/14We can use the Normal Map TOP to help enhace the appearance of dimension in our textures by "faking" some of the lightingof bumps in a texture.Here we create a normal map from our input image, and then use this along with the original image in our  Phong MAT.An additional Level TOP has been used in this example to further draw attention to the effect of the Normal Map.Use the button to toggle the Normal Map on and off in the Phong Material to better understand the effect of this TOP.from WikipediaIn 3D computer graphics, normal mapping, or "Dot3 bump mapping", is a technique used for faking the lighting of bumps and dents – an implementation of bump mapping. It is used to add details without using more polygons. A common use of this technique is to greatly enhance the appearance and details of a low polygon model by generating a normal map from a high polygon model or height map.http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Normal_mapping

label: NVIDIA Background
text: The NVIDIA Background TOP generates a white key over a black background... usually a person, but may be anything... It uses the NVIDIA background removal ML algortithm on their ML-enabled GPUs like the 2000 series and up.Some optimizations (that may end up built-in to the TOP) are:The cache1 TOP delays the video signal by 2 frames because nvbackground1 takes around 2 frames to generate the mask, so when they are joined up again in matte1, they are back in sync.Note that the NVIDIA Background TOP by default generates a single channel, not RGBA. The Matte TOP accepts that as its matte input.If you half the res going into it, it takes less than half the GPU time. optional. The mattes mvbakground1 makes may have a bit of a halo around it when re-composited. To eat away at the edge of the mask, blur1 softens it, and lookup1 with scurve1 pulls the edge in ("erodes" the mask). To make it erode more, reduce the Length parameter of scurve1 to below 1000.  Adjust that along with the Filter Size of blur1 to you taste.

label: NVIDIA Flow
text: In this example ramp1 is used to indicate where the emission of fuel comes from.  The ramp position is randomized with noise.  This animated ramp is used as the Shape OP in the flow emitter. This means emission will happen where it is white and less emmision where it is black.Reduce Mem Usage if it gets too slow.

label: NVIDIA Flow
text: Use the cross1 TOP to cross fade in an RGB spectrum to see how color is mapped to the temperature of the flow.Mem Usage parameter is reduced from .1 to .07 to run a bit faster.

label: NVIDIA Flow
text: Reduce Speed par to ge more billowy effect.

label: NVIDIA Flow
text: Is this example linear velocity is animated to push the fuel source left and right.

label: NVIDIA Flow
text: Is this example a more intense use of linear velocity is used to create a flame thrower effect.

label: OpenColorIO
text: You can use the CDL transform and Output page to colorgradeany image.

label: OpenColorIO
text: openColorIO can be used for numerous color correction operations for various output display devices. In this case the OpenColorIO TOP node (sRGBRamp) is used to simulate the conversion of a linear ramp into a gamma lookup image (brightened iamge to use the 256 steps of an 8-bit image bettter), as it would be stored in a JPEG file.  See the wiki page 'sRGB' for more information. https://docs.derivative.ca/SRGB

label: OpenColorIO
text: OpenColorIO is a toolkit for working with different color spaces and gamma settings for image files. Different cameras and display devices have different methods to store and display color. OpenColorIO supports the loading of different lookup tables (LUTs) that can transform how the color data is stored and viewed.The TouchDesigner colorspace is linear. By using OpenColorIO an image that is stored in one color space can be transformed to another.  In the example here the image was taken with a Sony a6300 using Picture profile1, Gamma: S-log3, Color Mode: S-Gamut3.CineBy default the OpenColorIO TOP comes with only a small number of LUTs that are built in. The closest input LUT is SLog and the correct way to output the image for viewing in TouchDesigner is linear.  See the Color Space Transform page of each TOP and you will see these settings.The image when its loaded is quite dull. However this image is a 16bit TIFF and the color values are stored in such a way as to maintain the most color information. Through the muse of a LUT the Slog stored colors and viewed better in TouchDesigner with an output LUT for linear.github with many more LUTs...https://github.com/imageworks/OpenColorIO-ConfigsGood collection found in above github is aces_1.0.3 which is latest color grading kit from Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_Color_Encoding_SystemLoad the aces_1.0.3 configuration file in the aces_1.0.3 folder in the git repository and select the config.ocio.The following LUTs will be available.Input LUT - Sony-S-Log3-S.Gamut3.CineOutput LUT - Utility-Linear-sRGB

label: Optical Flow
text: This draws velocity flow vectors based on the Optical Flow TOP.Give it a live video feed. opticalflow1 outputs two 32-bit floating point channels (red and green).  The red channel is the horizontal motion it detects at each pixel, and green is vertical motion. If it's flickering, it means likely that your video source is not feeding in 60 frames per second, so some images are repeats of the prior frame, which will give 0 optical flow for the current frame.Settings defines how many columns  of vectors you want.res1 averages a bunch of the flow pixels down to one value per column. res1's resolution is the same as the number of grid1's columns and rows.topto1 gets the pixels into one long set of samples for red and green.Filter1 smooths out the jittery motion (filter per sample).chopto1 splices the red and green channels into the grid's Normal attribute's X and Y values. (Z is left at 1 but it doesn't matter here).sopt1 shows it as a table for illustration.The grid is passed to geo1 which uses a Line MAT to render the points and vectors. It assumes the vectors are coming in the Normals attribute.The render1 TOP's resolution is set to the correct aspect ratio using the Aspect parameter in the "settings" node, which is derived from the incoming image's aspect ratio.comp1 gets its images via the TOPs parameter.  Fixed Layer parameter is set to Input 1 to make sure the  output res matches the render's.

label: OP Viewer
text: The OP Viewer TOP allows you to produce a texture out of any operator. Here we can see a display being rendered from a CHOP, SOP, and DAT. This example multiples the node height and width by 4 - this maintains the aspect ratio while simultaneously increasing the display size.Matthew Ragan8/13/14On the TOPs' Commpn page, change Viewer Smoothness to Mipmap Pixels to make the viewer look smoother with those thin lines. That's purely cosmetic. It does't affect the image that the TOP outputs. (gh)

label: Outside
text: The Outside TOP places Input1 "outside" Input2. The alpha of Input2 is used to determine what parts of the Input1 image are visible.

label: Outside
text: The Outside TOP places Input1 'outside' Input2. Input1 is visible in the output wherever Input2's alpha is <1. This is the opposite operation to the Inside TOP.See also the Composite TOP and the blendModes component in the Palette.

label: Over
text: The Pre Fit parameters give you various options to how the compositing will occur.

label: Over
text: A handy trick to scale content to a  specific resolution is to use an Over TOP. But you can more easily feed the Movie File In TOP to a Constant TOP, or mroe easily yet, use a Fit TOP.

label: Over
text: Fit Outside is useful in situations where you'd like to overlay one image onto another completely, and removes the chance of any edges being missed.Better yet, see the Fit TOP.

label: Pack
text: Pack is used to put 32-bit RGBA floating point images into quadruple-wide 8-bit fixed point image to make them more transportable through lossless file formats that are just 8-bit, or 8-bit protocols like Spout/Siphon. Then the Pack TOP can unpack on the other side.In the second example, we just do it to a single floating point channel, reorder_32BitRedOnly gets the blue channel only, and outputs a single-channel TOP (see its Pixel Format parameter). Then we pack that single channel into 8-bit form (pack_8BitMono). Then we unpack it as a mono floating point channel. The last reorder takes that one channel and puts it as the blue channel of an RGBA 32-bit image.In the packed format, every 32-bit pixel is converted to 4 8-bit pixels. It's lossless.

label: Pack
text: ramp1 is normal RGBA 8-bit.ramp2 is 1-channel 8-bit.ramp3 is 1-channel 32-bit float. You can see its better precision in the ramp.ramp3 is in a similar format that you would have for a depth map or a point cloud.pack1 puts ramp3 into an RGBA 8-bit image that can be commonly saved in a file.Right-click on the node and Save Image...or you can write it out with the Movie File Out TOP.

label: Point File In
text: Point File In TOP reads a .ply point cloud file.info1 shows that it has 10 channels including point colors in 'red', 'green' and 'blue' channels.info2 shows number of points.Most TOPs can only hold up to 4 channels, so null1 gets the first 4, which are X Y Z and 'active'. 'active' (in the alpha channel of the image) is a channel that indicates if the pixel is a valid point or not (orange = Nan = not a point).pointfileselect1 is used to get the other channels into a TOP... in this case, the channels it selects are the point colors - 'red', 'green', 'blue' as they are named in the file.The colors in the .ply file are 0-255 so math1 scales them down to 0-1.geo1 contains one point that is converted to a 1-point particle system, needed to render Line MAT from TOPs.

label: Point File In
text: The resolution is reduced via the Output Resolution menu on the Common page to 1/4 by 1/4 = 1/16 the number of points in the file. This is shown in info2 and the eval1, eval2 expressions.

label: Point Transform
text: for points, x is in the red color channel, y is in green, z is in blue.pointtransform1 moves the banana to a randon location, angle and scale.pointtransform2 lines it up on the Align page: in X, the banana is centered around x=0.in Y, the bottom of the banana is on the X axis at y=0.In Z is is pushed back where the closest point is at Z=0.Then the whole thing is normalized using Align Scale to make the largest dimension (X in this case) size 1. You can see that in null2: R goes -.5 to .5.pointtransform3 does the same as pointtransform2, but then it scales the result by 20 as a "post-transform". To post-transform, you have to set the Align Transform Order to "Align, then Transform" and set the Scale on the Transform page to 20.

label: Point Transform
text: pointtransform1 moves the banana to a new position, angle and scale.pointtransform_invert moves it right back to its original position, rotation and scale, using the same parameter values as pointtransform1 and turning on the Invert toggle.Think of it as moving FROM the position/rotate/scale defined by the parameter TO a null translate/rotate of 0 and scale of 1.The Math CHOP doesn't have multi-inputs, so use the Composite TOP and in this case use Subtract to verify the resulting points are the same as the starting points.

label: Point Transform
text: You can get transform values from a CHOP, as long as you have the right channel names. The transform CHOP outputs channels as tx ty tz rx ry rz etc, and giving that to the Transform CHOP parameter works to tranform from CHOPs.transform2 take the transform channels from transform1 and converts them to a 4x4 matrix, which is also acceptable for pointtransform2.

label: Point Transform
text: This rotates the point data to make its z-axis point at a 3D object. Here we assume the point data is at 0, 0, 0. geo1 is at 0, 0, 2, which is along the positive z-axis pointing toward you. and it's oscillating in X via the LFO from -2 to +2.You think of it as the Z-axis of the TOP's axes being rotated to point at the Look at object.Here the "Up vector" is the Y axis (0, 1, 0).

label: POP to
text: Fit to Square guarantees every point gets a pixel.You can convert any attribute combination into the 4 channels.You can put all points into one row of teh texture up to the hardware limit (32,768 commonly)You can specify a resolution and let it fill the pixels up, one point per pixel, wrpping around when it gets to the end of a row.Now middle click on torus and copy to see the dimensions. or look at popto_dimensions.The two POP to TOPs at teh bottom use the dimensions to build the texture, which is the most convenient.

label: Projection
text: cubeMap is a 6-camera-in-one rendering created with the Render TOP.It renders the entire view around the camera.projection1 need the Projection Type parameter to be set to the input's type, and its output in this case is Equirectangular.You need to set its resolution and aspect ratio on the Common page.  The Rotate parameter lets you specify which direction is "staight ahead".projection2 converts the Equirectangular map to a Fish-Eye.  Again, the Rotate parameter lets you specify which direction is "staight ahead".Also Fish-Eye lets you select what the viewing angle is to the perimeter outer ring, default 180 degrees.projection3 is the same thing but it gets its input from the cube map. projection4 is rotated to look behind.

label: Projection
text: This converts from an existing equirectangular image.projection3 has its resolution and aspect ratio changed on the Common page.projection4 is rotated to be looking down-up along the horizontal center line. Same as projection5.  They are both equiractangulars too.See also the Cube Map TOP.

label: Projection
text: here the Cartesian-to-Polar conversionThe Projection TOP needs it input flipped first to get the ramp to go clockwise. Bypass it to make it go counter-clockwise.

label: Ramp
text: Ramp TOPs can be used to create circles.Extend Left parameter is set to Zero and Interpolate Notches is set to Step.But now we have the Circle TOP!

label: Ramp
text: You can edit the individual values of the ramp via its docked table. Try changing the R G or B values in the table.

label: Ramp
text: You can animate a ramp's phase

label: Ramp
text: hsvrgb1 treats its input as the hue being in the red channel, saturation in the green channel, and value in the blue channel.In this case, hue is a ramp from 0 to 1 giving hte full rainbow spectrum. In TouchDesigner, a hue of value 0 is red, .33 is green, .66 is blue and 1 is red again.HSV to RGB to interpret those three channels as HSV and convert the image to RGB.Saturation and value are full (1).null1 is just a different way to view the red, green and blue channels of ramp1 separately. rclick on the null1 viewer to see it set to Normalied Split.

label: Ramp
text: You can generate ramp tables using the Script DAT. Move the slider on constant1 to alter the mid-point of ramp table using the script1 DAT.

label: Ramp
text: Click/drag in bicolor. You can procedurally generate tables for ramps. In this case the panel "bicolor" u and v define an inner and outer hue, which is generated with an  expression for the red channel in an Evaluate DAT.The ramp is converted from hue-saturation-value to RGB in an HSV to RGB TOP.

label: Ramp
text: Click/drag blendModeIndex. Ramp TOP and several other generators can composite itself with its input using the full set of Composite TOP's blend modes. This lets you flip through all the choices when using a simple black to yellow ramp.Look at the Operation parameter of the Ramp TOPs as you move the slider.

label: Ramp
topic: red in x + green in y in two nodes
text: Adding the red of the first with the green of the second ramp.32-bit float pixels for greater accuracy.Concept useful for building maps for displacement.

label: Ramp
text: This is a skin on top of the Ramp TOP where you create and set keyframes on rampA and rampB using blocks of sequential parameters (on the Keys page), where each block is a keyframe color/position.Inside it converts the parameters into a table the feeds the Ramp TOP.rampB is simply a different set of paramters to define the colors.

label: Rectangle
text: rectangle1 with top-left justification, quarter size rectangle (res is 100x100) offset quarter right and 10% down.By default you are expressing the rectangle as fractions of the image width and height. You can also work in pixels.

label: Rectangle
text: With the rectangle fill color set to transparant, the rectangle's border can be used to create a 16:9 letterbox effect within a square TOP.

label: Rectangle
text: Use the Corner Radius parameter to give a rectangle round corners. Also each rectangle TOP is composited over its predecessor using a Difference operation (see the 'Output' tab in their parameter window).

label: Rectangle
text: The parameters of a Rectangle TOP can be animated by using CHOP channels as input. In this example the rotation is controlled via an LFO signal from lfo1.That same LFO signal (which is -1 to 1) is then converted to 0 to 1 (in math2) so it can drive a Lookup CHOP, which applies the curve from pattern1 to the size of the rectangle.

label: Remap
text: For each pixel in the second input, it uses the value in the red channel as the u (horizontal, 0 being left-most) coordinate, and the value in the green channel as the v (vertial) coordinate to fetch a pixel from the first input.Remap TOP ramps are 32-bit floats to get enough precision to index pixels of the 1280x720 input.prepared by David Braun & GH

label: Remap
text: With some source ramps you get or make, you may notice undesired sparkles around the edge of remap1. This is because the transform1 image is created anti-aliased with Input Smoothness set to Interpolate Pixels, so the values of red and green around the edge are reduced, which will make the sampling occur not around the edges of null1 but somewhere in the middle of null1.To improve this, change Input Smoothness to Nearest Pixel on transform1, or if you have no control of the input image, use reorder1 and comp1 to restore the RG around the edges by dividing by the alpha, preferably all at 16 bit fixed or 32 bit float resolutions.

label: Remap
text: resolution from second input.   Start with a red ramp in X, add a green ramp in Y.

label: Render
text: The Render TOP renders a 3D scene with a camera and lights into a 2D texture. A standard render setup includes a geometry object, a camera, light, and a material.You can wire a SOP to the input of geo1 to replace to torus.see sopRender in Palette's Tools for a more packaged setup.

label: Render
text: This setup is being rendered in a number of different ways to demonstrate some  of the functionality of the Render TOP

label: Render
text: Notice that the lights are using a lookat to point at the geometry. This is nessesary to create a proper shadow map, which is rendered from the light's point of view. You then also want to adjust the FOV of the lights camera parameters on the View page to tighten the light's view, so the shadow map has better resolution on the area's you are interested in.

label: Render
text: You can use the Depth TOP to get the depth buffer for both a Render TOP, as well as the shadow map from a light that is doing shadowing. Usually a Depth map will look mostly white, with much of the information between 0.9 and 1.0. You can visually make it more informative by tightening the near/far planes for the camera/light.

label: Render
text: The Render TOP renders a 3D scene as a cube map, which is 6 renders-in-one, using the Render Mode parameter.In this case the camera and light are at 0, 0, 0 and the geometry is around it in all directions.See also the Projection TOP to convert between different projection types.The image aspect ratio is 3x4.See also the Cube Map TOP and the Projection TOP.

label: Render
text: To render transparent objects correctly all polygons must be sorted from closest to furthest away from the camera before being passed along to the render pipeline. The polygons that are furthest way must be sent first, while the polygons that are closest to the camera you want to rendered after. With OpenGL there are no fast GPU based ways to do this explicitly. For transparency to work well for complex scenes you have to manually sort the polygons of each geometry set using two sortSOPs.  The first sort SOP sorts the primitives from closest to furthest from the camera. In this example that is "sort1". The second sortTOP is used to reverse that order so the closest polygons are rendered last.The switch1 sop in this example shows 4 different cases.  The first case on input 0 is a sphereSOP that is set to Mesh mode. The mesh topology has only a single primitive. This type of geometry can not be sorted by primitive order.  Input 2 shows the Mesh sphere converted to polygons.  This topology can be sorted as now each quad is treated as a seperate primitve.  The next 2 examples show a polygon sphere and polygon based torus which as well can be sorted correctly.For animating geometry - in this example a rotating sphere, the transform must come before the sort.  Therefore the transform1 SOP has an animating expression in the RY parameter. This kind of setup however only works for a single GEO object.  You must also correctly setup the render order for each geo object.  This is done using te Draw Priority parameter on the geoCOMP render page.  The closet geo, in this case is geoNear.  The object closest to the camera should have the highest draw priority.  The object furthest away should have the lowest draw priority.Also notice the Blending parameter is turned on in the phong1 material.  As well the material has its Alpha parameter set to 0.2. You must manually turn on transparency for the materials in TouchDesigner.The reason this might seem confusing is there are two main factors at play here. Do you want to the geometry to render as fast as possible, or do you require the scene to have transparency.  By default TouchDesigner is configured for fastest rendering speed.With this in mind, if the GPU determines the current pixel will occlude the new one, it won't bother rendering the new one. Therefore if you require transparency you must follow a process like this to ensure the contribution from each polygon is rendered. There is no fast and automatic way for this to occur.

label: Render
text: In this example there is a bgMat midMat and fgMat materials textured onto 3 quads - The background object is textured with a checkerboard. The mid ground object is textured with a spectrum that has a striped alpha channel.  The forground is a fuzzy circle. To explore the blend modes use each of the fgMat, midMat and bgMat to explore different permutations of the following parameters.Blend OperationSource ColorDestination ColorThe values of these 3 menu items are printed onto the screen as BlendOperation(SourceColor, Destination Color)For more technical information on how the blending system works go here...https://www.khronos.org/opengl/wiki/Blending#:~:text=Blending is the stage of,be combined in various ways.

label: Render
text: This example shows how to create a procedural noise texture on a sphere and addresses transparency as well.A RGB texture and Alpha texture is created and mapped to pbr1. Its important to note that although we are rendering a transparent sphere you must ensure Blending (Transparency) parameter is turned off on the common page. This is because the renderTOP is set to use Order Independent Transparency for the Transparency parameter.=====In porceduralSphereTextures, the render2 TOP directly renders a SOP. The material for this render is applied to the render2 renderTOP using the Override Material found on the Advanced page. The phong1 material is setup to render the sphere world space position using the Color Buffer 1 RGB parameter. You can render a variety of attributes to a color buffer using this method.The render select TOP is used to extract the color buffer 1 from the render TOP. This technique works well in many cases, but there are wrapping issues with that can be worked around in some cases. For a sphere the crop / flip / math trick is used to fill the missing positions to the left side of the texutre.This position map can be used to drive a noise texture's lookup - in this case noise1 second input is used to create coherent 3D noise for the sphere.

label: Render
text: This example fetches a biplane USDZ asset from the Apple Augmented Reality 3D models website. (See https://developer.apple.com/augmented-reality/quick-look/ for other examples.)A biplane 3D asset is used here to explain order independent transparency as there are many seperate objects that intersect at different angles to make polygon and object level ordering difficult.Adjust the Transparency/Peel Layers parameter between 1 and 6 to reveal more back-surfaces.The render TOP has an option for the Transparency parameter found on the main Render page called "Order Independent Transparency". Then using the Transparency / Peel Layers you can adjust the number of layers required to make the transparency look good.  This method isn't perfect but it can do a very good job in many cases.NOTE: Remember that all materials need to have their Blending (Transparency) parameter disabled for this RenderTOP option to work. The material for this asset happens to be found at the path toy_biplane/propeller/pCube20.

label: Render
text: As with conventional stereo rendering, you will need two cameras.  The main difference between conventional stereo and omnidirectional stereo (ODS) in terms of setup is that both cameras have the same transform (i.e., no offsetting in the x direction).The interpupillary distance (IPD) must be set in both cameras (View/IPDShift), negative in the left eye camera, and positive in the right.  A typical value is 63 mm.

label: Render Pass
text: The Render Pass TOP can be used to render additional geometry into the same buffer. You can optionally turn on clearing the depth or color buffers depending on if you want to add more geometry to the existing scene taking depth into account, render the geometry ontop of the existing scene as an overlay, or simply rendering a new scene and clearing the old scene.

label: Render Select
text: The Render TOP is able to render multiple cameras (more quickly than separately) in a single node.You specify multiple cameras in one Camera parameter of the Render TOP, and use the Render Select TOP to pull out those camera results.

label: Render Select
text: The Render Select TOP can pull data from either a Render TOP, GLSL TOP or a CPlusPlus TOP.In this example the GLSL TOP outputs RGB to three different color buffers. The Render Select TOP is used to pull the second and third color buffer.

label: Reorder
text: This is a simple example of how to assign incoming RGBA channels to outgoing RGBA channels.In the example, the output Red channel is the 2nd input's Red channel, the output Green channel is the 3rd input's Green channel, the output Blue channel is the 1st input's Blue channel, and the alpha is the 4th input's alpha.The Math TOP multiplies RGB by alpha to conform to the TouchDesigner image standard of "pre-multiplied by alpha", where red, green and blue are always less than alpha.

label: Reorder
text: Reorder is helpful for creating alpha masks by using the input's alpha channel as all the output channels.

label: Reorder
text: Reorder can pull individual channels.In the first case it preserves the red channel, and sets the green and blue channels to 0. It also sets the alpha to 1 making the resulting image opaque.In the second case it also converts the image to one-channel versus the ususal 4-channel RGBA. This is done on any TOP's Common page in the Pixel Format menu, The third case shows that the first channel is used for the mono channel: Green is pulled into the first channel and the image converted to mono,

label: Reorder
text: This puts into the alpha channel the maximum value for R G or B. Therefore all pixels have legal values for compositing with transparency because images must be pre-multiplied by alpha, and thus the value in any of the R G or B channels must not exceed what is in alpha.The is similar to what the Screen mode in the Composite TOP does... blend in the brighter pixels.Un-bypass the Level TOP and the alpha is increased, still legal, toward being fully opaque using the Low A parameter.Below, reorder_illegalAlpha has been created with some pixels containing illegal alpha values. Reorder_fixIllegalAlpha repairs that in one step by putting the max value of RGB and A into the new alpha channel.

label: Reorder
text: Fill background with black by putting One in the Alpha channel of the Reorder TOP.  But there are at least eight other ways to do it!

label: Resolution
text: This example brings the resolution down to 1/4 of it's original value.

label: Resolution
text: You can use the Resolution TOP to change the input smoothness settings.

label: Resolution
text: The two images are the same.It is only the viewers that are set differently. The second TOP has its Viewer Smoothness set to Mip Map, which makes images look better when you zoom back from them.The Viewer Smoothness parameter is on all TOPs.

label: RGB Key
text: The RGB Key TOP "pulls a key" from the image using the Red, Green, and Blue channel settings. If a pixel value is between the Min and Max parameters for all three settings, then its alpha is set to 1.The first three examples are each set to a different color. for rgbkey1, red has to be above .5, green below .1, and blue below .1.You can also use this operator to hide certain colors. See rgbkey3_invert, it has exactly the same Red, Green, and Blue settings as rgbkey3, but it has its alpha inverted using Invert New Alpha.See also the Chroma Key TOP and in the Palette, the Chromakey component.Idzard Kwadijkkwadijk@idzard.com

label: RGB to HSV
text: rgbhsv TOP converts pixels so that the hue is in  the red channel, the saturation is in the green channel, and the value (brightness) is in  the blue channel.After converting to hsv,level_increase_saturation  alters the g (saturation) channel via the Gamma parameter, where 0 saturation and 1 saturation remain unchanged, but all other values are cranked  up toward 1. After converting to hsv, level_push_color alters r (hue) channel so the values are in a smaller range (.1 to .2). After converting to hsv level_quantize_hue_and_value quantizes hue and saturation by using the Step parameters. The Channel Mask parameter on  the Common page quantizes in .25 steps the  red (hue) and blue (brightness) channels.The last two examples show blurring of hue and saturation by masking blue or red channels.Note: All the above get converted from hsv to rgb.

label: RGB to HSV
text: This is a useful trick to squeeze the range of colors in an image down to a smaller range (artificial of course). Like all colors fit into the yellow-to-green range.Drag-drop the Movie File In TOPs to select1 to see what it does.Then to adjust the range, right-click on hueControls and select View... to get the panel in a floating window. Then adjust Hue Midpoint. Hue Divider needs to be an integer for this to work well. Hue Divider of 5 causes the hue range to be 1/5 the full color range, 8 reduces the range to 1/8, etc.  The higher the number, the more single-color the result is.Another trick: hueControls is a set of parameters, and the UI of hueControls is defined with a Parameter COMP that is located inside hueControls, and exposed via the Operator Viewer parameter on hueControls (slight brain-twister).

label: Screen Grab
text: A Color Picker can be made using a Screengrab TOP that grabs a single pixel from anywhere on the desktop, even outside the TouchDesigner windows.Hold Shift key down and move your mouse anywhere.Inside, it use nettoyeur's findMouse component, which is also in the snippets for the Mouse In CHOP.Its cost is an Execute DAT that runs a tiny of amount of code every frame.Thanks to Harvey Moon for this, and Idzard for his findMouse component (see Mouse In CHOP Snippets).

label: Script
text: The script TOP's method .copyNumpyArray() takes as an argument a numpy array that holds all color information for the texture that is output for the TOP. The numpy array is defined by a size and type where size is specified in a list holding height, width and number of color channels. So for a 1280x720 pixel sized image with rgba color channels, the size would be [720, 1280, 4]The numpy arrays type can be of type uint8, uint16 or float32.It is important to watch out for the valuerange for colorvalues depending of the array type. uint8 has a value range of 0 to 255, uint16 has a value range of 0 to 65535 andfloat32 has a value range of 0 to 1.Especially when converting between array types this is something to keep in mind.

label: Script
text: Every TOP's content can be converted to a numpy array by using the .numpyArray() Method. The returned numpy array can then be used as an argument to the Script TOP's .copyNumpyArray() to display it.It is important to understand that .numpyArray() returns in all cases a 32 bit float numpy array. If the Script TOP should hold a texture with a different pixel format, the numpy array will have to be converted first.

label: Script
text: Every TOP's content can be converted to a numpy array by using the .numpyArray() Method. By default this Method will download the texture on the same frame and with the download from GPU to CPU being a slow process, a performance impact can't be prevented. By specifying the optional argument 'delayed', the texture will only be ready on the next frame being downloaded in the background and as a result the performance impact is minimized.A problamatic issue is that the texture will not be ready the first time the method is called. For that reason a default numpy array of the same size as the copied texture should be created and used as a standin.The returned numpy array can then be used as an argument to the Script TOP's .copyNumpyArray() to display it.It is important to understand that .numpyArray() returns in all cases a 32 bit float numpy array. If the Script TOP should hold a texture with a different pixel format, the numpy array will have to be converted first.

label: Slope
text: THe Slope TOP shows where the luminance in an image rises or falls relative to its neighboring pixel(s) (immediately left-right and below-above pixels).The Slope TOPs are set by default to put the horizontal rate of change in the red channel, and the vertical rate of change in the blue channel.The viewers of the Slope TOPs are set to "Normalized Split" that puts each color channel in a separate box. The top-left represents the red channel.Where the rate of change is 0 (the source's solid white areas for example) the color is 50% grey (the Zero Point parameter is .5).Whiter in the Slope TOP's red channel means the luminance of the input is increasing left-to-right around that pixel.ramp1 is 4 sections - each is a rise or fall in RGB horizontally. Because the ramps are linear, slope3 is a solid color in each band.By default, where the slope is 0, a value of .5 is output in the pixel. (by default, pixels don't have negative values.) slope1 shows this.But in slope2, the Zero Point parameter is set to 0 so where the slope is 0, the color channel is set to 0. In this case the slopes have some negative values where the source color decreaes left-to-right, so the Pixel Format parameter on the Common page has to be a 16-bit or 32-bit float, which can represent negative values.slope4 is only looking at the red channel.topto1 shows that ramp1 is constantly rising or falling, which gove constant values in the topto2 red channel.PS: slope is "rate of change" or "derivative" in calculus.

label: Spectrum
text: The Spectrum TOP does a "Discrete Fourier Transform" on an image. It's based on the idea that any image can be represented as a bunch of horizontal sine waves superimposed, and a bunch of vertical waves superimposed.First you have to apply spectrum on the /separated color channels: spectrum1, 2, 3 choose the red, green and blue channels of moviefilein1.These Spectrum TOPs output the same resolution as their input, but only output 2 color channels (red and green, 32 bit floats) which represent waves' amplitude (in red) and phase (in green). spectrum1_inverse, 2 and 3 do the inverse. They take the spectrum images and convert them back to the original image in reorder1.Unbypass circle2. Look closely at it - it preserves only the pixels near the center of the image. It makes spectrum3 a bit blurrier, but surprisingly most of the image is intact and circle2 just takes away the high-frequency parts of the red color channel.

label: Spectrum
text: Making small changes in the Transform TOP, which is masked (Common page, Channel Mask parameter)  to only affect the red (amplitude) channel makes radical changes to the image.pick all 3 Transform TOPs and change their parameters together... scale, rotate, translate.spectrum8, etc shows the image converted to different arrangements of pixels in the DFT image.

label: Spectrum
text: This uses the Inverse DFT. Here we synthesize an image from placing non-white pixels in a black image, and letting the Spectrum CHOP generate an image from the spectrum image feeding spectrum1.rectangle1, rectangle2 and circle1 are 32-bit images with only the red and green channels. middle-click on the TOPs to see their format. This is what the inverse spectrum needs: red controls amplitude of superimposed waves, and green controls their phase shift. Each pixel can generate a wave.Small changes produce big effects.  Non-black pixels near the center produce long waves. A red pixel at the center is the average of the whole image (the DC value), but a pixel to the right of it (seen in rectangle1) produces a horizontal sine wave that in one horizontal wave cycle, and if you move the dot to the right by one more pixel (The Center X parameter), it creates a 2-cycle sine wave. The same thing happens when moving pixels vertically by a few pixels: Un-bypass rectangle2 to see two waves superimposed.Unbypass circle1 to see the effect of a bunch of pixels near the center. It's green (phase) is animated. Adjust the radius slightly.

label: Spectrum
text: spectrum1 to spectrum3, show the image converted to different arrangements of pixels in the DFT image.They differ in the Coordinate System menu and the Transorm Rows flag.Parameters in spectrum*_inverse are bound to their counterpart in spectrum*.

label: Spectrum
text: A test bed for playing around with re-synthesis.Pick all 3 Rectangle tOPs and change their parameters together (or change them separately but you will quickly get messed up).Play around with Rotate.The phase channel (green) has a big effect on the image as it causes all the sine waves to line up correctly to recreate the original image. If you change the Fill Color's green to values below 1, the image breaks up more.Other things to play with are Size and Center. Change input image to other simple patterns.

label: SSAO
text: The SSAO TOP performs Screen Space Ambient Occlusion on the output of a Render TOP or Render Pass TOP. To properly see the effect in the example scene, right-click the "view_output" node and select 'View'.Now bypass/unbypass the SSAO TOP to see the difference.To better understand what the SSAO TOP generates, look at ssao2. When this is multiplied with the Render Top output you'll get the same result as in the "view_output" node.Because this technique requires access to the Depth Buffer, no other TOP can be in between the Render/RenderPass TOP and the SSAO TOP. Screen Space Ambient Occlusion is a real-time rendering trick that creates an approximation of ambient occlusion by using the Depth Buffer of the rendered scene. It is done as 2D post-processes, so the complexity of the scene has no bearing on how expensive it is. This is in comparison to true ambient occlusion which becomes more expensive with each new object that is rendered.Play with the parameter to suit your scene.

label: Switch
text: This example blends between inputs smoothly in a cycle of 4 images. It uses tricky technique to handle the case of going from the last image back to the first image without passing through the other 2 images. There must be a better way!The switch_blend also forces the resolution to be 1280x720. Otherwise its output res switches between the various resolutions of the inputs

label: Texture 3D
text: To create a 3D texture, you need to fill the Texture 3D TOP with a sequence of 2D images, in this case 6 of them.To create the 3D teture: You "pre-fill" tex3d1 by turning on Pre Fill or pulsing Pre Fill. The TOP automatically goes to frame 1 on the timeline, cooks the image that feeds into the tex3d1 TOP, tex3d1 captures it as it first slice, then it goes to frame 2 and cooks the image again, until all 6 images are cooked. Cache size here is 6.So the way to make all the images different is to put "me.time.frame" in the the Switch TOP that feed into tex3d1.You can leave Pre-Fill on, it will cook once after you start your .toe file, or you can click the Pulse button to cook it when you change OPs in your network feeding into the CHOP.tex3d1 is used in the material for the Geometry COMP's instancing. The Geometry COMP's W parameter refers to the w channel of "instancer", which contains the slice number in the w channel, going from 0 to 5.

label: Texture 3D
text: To create a 3D texture, you need to fill it with a sequence of 2D images, in this case 32 of them.The way it's done now is to imagine it's building the 32 images by going to frame 1 on the timeline,cooking the image that feeds into the tex3d1 TOP, then going to frame 2 and cooking the image again,until all 32 images are cooked.So the way to make all the images different is to put "me.time.frame" in the OPs that feed into tex3d1.In this case, moviefilein1 has me.time.frame in the Index parameter to get the first image of the movie ontimeline frame 1. That parameter's index units is set to "I" (index), in case the movie has a different frames per second (such as 25 images per second) than the timeline.  So frame 23 is always going to be image 22 of the file (where index starts at 0, frame starts at 1).  In this way, no images are missed or duplicated in the 3D teture.The HSV Adjust TOP also has an expression involving me.time.frame, where it goes through the full color wheel in 32 steps.You can leave Pre-Fill on, it will cook once after you start your .toe file, or you can click the Pulse button to cook it when you change OPs in your network feeding into the CHOP.

label: Threshold
text: The Threshold TOP creates a matte with pixel values set to 0 for pixels below the threshold value, and 1 for pixels greater than or equal to the threshold value. Operator 'thresh2_greater' has its comperator set to greater instead of less, which results in an inverted matte.You can fill alpha with the red channel, or 1.

label: Tile
text: The main purpose of the Tile TOP is to produce seamless texture maps from most images.Once you are happy with its look as a 2x2 tile, you can switch it to a 1x1 to get the most out of your memory, and then use it as a texture elsewhere.  Try it on sopRender in Tools in the Dialogs -> Palette.

label: Tile
text: The Tile TOP, like its name suggests, will create a tiled image. This can make for some interesting kaleidoscopic effects. Here you can see four examples of how you might use the tile effect to create repeated patterns. Use the buttons to switch between using the photo and the ramp to see different effects. You might also use the bypass button to see what happens if you turn off the spin effect.Matthew Ragan 8/16/14

label: Time Machine
text: The Time Machine TOP is always used with the Texture 3D TOP. (See the snippets for Texture 3D TOP.)The 3D texture is loaded (via Pre Fill, which runs the timeline from frame 1 to frame (cache size * step size = 32 frames here) and  puts it in a 3D texture.The Time Machine TOP fetches from the 3D texture. Where level2 is black, it gets from the lower slices of the 3D texture. Where it is white it get from the upper slices of the 3D texture.That is, each pixel of the output uses a slice of the 3D texture based on the grey-scale value of the 2nd input (the ramp).You can adjust the lower and upper range of the ramp, which restricts which slices in the 3D texture it retrieves from.

label: Transform
text: The Transform's Pivot paramter controls where in the image the rotation is centered.

label: Transform
text: The transform order is crucial for assigning the order of the Transform TOP's operations.transform1 (deafault) rotates first then translates.  transform2 (via Transform Order menu) translates first and then rotates.

label: Transform
text: The extend conditions are used whenever you create blank space using the Transform TOP.When you want seamless repeating textures, use the Tile TOP.

label: Transform
text: Each Transform TOP is setting the resolution down to 1/16 the original resolution (80 pixels in x).To see the indiviual pixels more clearly, on the Common page on transform1, Viewer Smoothness is set to Nearest Pixel.Another way to see individual pixels is to turn on Viewer Active (bottom right flag on the nodem transform2), then right-click on the image and set Display Pixel Values. That also shows the pixel values at the bottom left of the image.Middle-mouse-click on the transform2 image to zoom into pixels.  'h' to home.None of the above affects the data in the pixels, it only affects how you see them in the viewer.On the other hand, transform4 affects the pixel values because Input Smoothness is set to Mipmap pixels. (Check 'mipmap" in Wikipedia.) Because the input is scaled down so much, transform4 combines 16x16 = 256 pixels into one by averaging them, but the others take only one pixel from the input as its value for an output pixel.transform noMipmap and transform_mipmap show how, even when the resolution of the output is the same as the input, turning on Mipmap on Input Smoothness in transform_mipmap makes 10 pixels averages into 1 pixel of the output.

label: Video Device In
text: Assuming you have a camera on this computer,Select a device and set Active to On.Then click on Options to adjust your camera's settings.

label: Video Stream Out
text: unbypass everything.In this example for SRT the sending operator uses the ipaddress 0.0.0.0 and it uses a port that has been forwarded using the router's firewall settings. In particular the port number has to be identified in the NAT/Gaming settings of the Internet router and correctly assigned to the listener computer ip address / mac address thats running the listening videostreamoutTOP.To get your internet IP address type "Whats my ip address" into google.  This help assumes you have an ip address assigned of the type ip4.Set videostreamoutTOP URL parameter to:srt://0.0.0.0:2030?mode=listenerSet videostreaminTOP URL parameter to:srt://[whats my ip]:2020?mode=callersrt://XXX.XXX.XXX.XXX:2030?mode=caller

label: Web Render
topic: display HTML saved in a DAT
text: By switching the Source parameter to 'DAT', HTML formated text can be displayed with the web Render TOP.The second example generates the HTML dynamically using a Substitute DAT.

label: Web Render
text: This is an example on how to use Javascript to dynamically change the content of what is being displayed via the webrenderTOP.All of this is done with the webrenderTOP's executeJavaScript() method.For it to work, first any css rule is removed from the web page.Next new css rules are being created.Finally the body of the document is updated which displays text formated with the specified style.All of this can be neatly organized in extensions and components to create a fully dynamic system for text layout and formating.For more information on javascript and css please refer to websites like https://www.w3schools.comTo try this, edit and run the text_stylesAndContent Text DAT.
